GBM and metastases show facilitated diffusion (high ADC) in their necrotic centres. Streptococcus milleri group is the most common organism in otogenic and dental cerebral abscesses. Treatment: surgical drainage + IV penicillin/ceftriaxone + metronidazole for 6 weeks.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 55-year-old woman is brought to the ED by her husband with a 4-day history of fever, headache, and increasingly bizarre behaviour.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute fever with encephalopathy (confusion, behavioural change) constitutes encephalitis until proven otherwise — a neurological emergency. The differential includes HSV encephalitis, autoimmune encephalitis, bacterial meningitis, cerebral abscess, non-convulsive status epilepticus, and metabolic encephalopathy. Behavioural change and confusion with fever in this time frame makes encephalitis the leading category.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has become acutely confused and spoken incoherently since this morning. Her husband describes her behaviour over 4 days as speaking to people who are not present, undressing inappropriately, and two episodes of lip smacking and repetitive hand movements lasting 2 minutes after which she was confused; she has also been unusually aggressive.
prompts.reasoning: The clinical triad of behavioural change, olfactory/gustatory hallucinations (visual hallucinations of absent people), and focal temporal lobe seizures (lip smacking, automatisms) are the hallmarks of temporal lobe involvement. HSV encephalitis has a predilection for the limbic system and temporal lobes, producing exactly this syndrome. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDA receptor, LGI1) can produce an identical clinical picture and must be actively excluded. Prior cold sores suggest HSV-1 latency in trigeminal ganglia.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological history. No immunosuppression. No prior encephalitis or meningitis. No family history of epilepsy or autoimmune disease. No recent foreign travel or insect bites. Takes metformin for type 2 diabetes. Non-smoker. No recreational drug use. No recent sexual partners with HSV history.
prompts.reasoning: No immunosuppression — HSV encephalitis affects immunocompetent individuals (reactivation of latent HSV-1 in the trigeminal ganglion). No recent travel reduces arboviral encephalitis (West Nile, Japanese encephalitis). No autoimmune history, though anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis can occur without prior autoimmune disease. Diabetes controlled on metformin — metabolic encephalopathy unlikely with controlled diabetes. Both HSV and autoimmune encephalitis remain equally plausible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.9°C. GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Agitated, disoriented to time and place. Mild neck stiffness. Right olfactory testing impaired. No focal hemiparesis. Plantar responses equivocal bilaterally. Oral herpes labialis visible (healing). MMSE untestable due to confusion. No rash. No lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Olfactory impairment (anosmia) reflects involvement of the olfactory cortex and temporal lobe — highly consistent with HSV encephalitis's predilection for the orbitofrontal and medial temporal regions. Mild neck stiffness indicates meningeal inflammation. GCS 11 confirms moderate encephalopathy. Healing herpes labialis confirms recent HSV-1 reactivation in the orofacial region. Autoimmune encephalitis can also produce this picture — both diagnoses must be investigated simultaneously.

prompts.label: Bloods, EEG, and CT
prompts.text: WBC 13.4 × 10⁹/L (lymphocytosis). CRP 62 mg/L. Blood cultures: no growth. EEG: periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) over the left temporal region — characteristic of temporal lobe encephalitis. CT head: subtle left temporal lobe hypodensity with focal cortical swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) on EEG over the temporal lobe are highly characteristic of HSV encephalitis — reflecting the focal temporal lobe destruction caused by the virus. Autoimmune encephalitis typically shows diffuse slow-wave activity. CT temporal lobe changes confirm focal structural abnormality. IV aciclovir must be started empirically before CSF results in any suspected HSV encephalitis — delay increases mortality and morbidity.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and CSF
prompts.text: MRI brain: asymmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the left medial temporal lobe, insula, and cingulate gyrus with restricted diffusion and mild enhancement. No haemorrhage yet. CSF: WBC 120 cells/µL (lymphocytes), protein 1.1 g/L, normal glucose. CSF HSV-1 PCR: positive. CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibody: negative. CSF culture: no bacterial growth.
prompts.reasoning: Medial temporal lobe, insular, and cingulate T2 hyperintensity with restricted diffusion is pathognomonic of HSV encephalitis — the virus spreads along olfactory pathways to the limbic system, producing this characteristic distribution. CSF HSV-1 PCR positive is the definitive confirmatory test (sensitivity 96%, specificity 99%). Negative anti-NMDA receptor antibody excludes the main autoimmune mimic. Continue IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg TDS for 14-21 days.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old man is referred to neurology by his GP with a 4-month history of progressive cognitive decline and slowed thinking.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive cognitive decline over 4 months in a middle-aged adult has a broad differential. Early-onset Alzheimer and frontotemporal dementia are less common at 45 but possible. Vascular dementia is plausible with vascular risk factors. Psychiatric causes (depression) can mimic cognitive decline. Infectious/neoplastic CNS disease must be excluded, particularly if there is underlying immunosuppression.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His partner reports he has become increasingly forgetful and socially withdrawn. He struggles with tasks that previously came easily, has noticed leg weakness when climbing stairs, and his walking pace has slowed. Mood has been low for 3 months; no headache, no fever, no seizures.
prompts.reasoning: Cognitive slowing with motor involvement (leg weakness, gait slowing) suggests a subcortical process — this distribution (cognitive + motor, subcortical pattern) is characteristic of HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder (HAND). Vascular dementia can also cause subcortical cognitive and motor slowing. Alzheimer disease is typically cortical (memory first, then language). CNS lymphoma can cause cognitive and motor deficits. The combination of cognitive slowing and motor symptoms is important.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV-positive — diagnosed 8 years ago. CD4 count 180 cells/µL at last check 6 months ago, viral load detectable. Has not been adherent to antiretroviral therapy for the past 18 months. Works as a teacher. Non-smoker. Moderate alcohol. No IV drug use. No prior AIDS-defining illnesses. No prior opportunistic infections. No family history of dementia.
prompts.reasoning: Poorly controlled HIV with CD4 180 and detectable viral load significantly narrows the differential — opportunistic CNS disease and HIV-direct CNS toxicity are now the leading considerations. At CD4 180, both CNS lymphoma (CD4 <50 more typical) and toxoplasmosis (CD4 <100 more typical) are less likely but possible. HIV encephalopathy (HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder, HAND) is the most common neurological complication in HIV patients on suboptimal treatment and can occur across the CD4 spectrum.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. MMSE 22/30 — impaired attention, slowed processing speed, poor recall; language intact; visuospatial tasks performed slowly but accurately. Mild bilateral lower limb spasticity. Gait: slow, wide-based with reduced arm swing. Bilateral plantar extensors. Mild bilateral hyperreflexia. No focal hemiparesis. No meningism. No papilloedema. No cranial nerve palsies.
prompts.reasoning: The neurological pattern — subcortical cognitive impairment (attention, processing speed, psychomotor slowing) with bilateral pyramidal signs (spasticity, upgoing plantars) and gait disorder, but with intact language — is the classic picture of subcortical dementia and HIV encephalopathy. CNS lymphoma typically causes focal deficits and mass effect. Absent papilloedema and absent focal deficits make a discrete focal mass lesion less likely. HIV encephalopathy causes diffuse white matter and basal ganglia involvement.

prompts.label: Bloods and CSF
prompts.text: CD4 count: 168 cells/µL. HIV viral load: 48,000 copies/mL (high, poorly controlled). Toxoplasma IgG: positive (prior exposure, not active disease). Serum cryptococcal antigen: negative. CSF: WBC 8/µL (borderline lymphocytosis), protein 0.62 g/L (mildly elevated), glucose normal. CSF HIV RNA: detectable (2,400 copies/mL). CSF culture: no organisms. CSF toxoplasma PCR: negative. CSF cytology: no malignant cells.
prompts.reasoning: Detectable HIV RNA in CSF confirms active HIV viral replication within the CNS compartment — the hallmark of HIV encephalopathy (HAND). Negative CSF toxoplasma PCR and negative cryptococcal antigen exclude opportunistic infections. No malignant cells exclude CNS lymphoma. CD4 168 with high plasma viral load confirms poor HIV control as the primary driver. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) optimisation is the treatment.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: bilateral symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the periventricular and deep white matter (leukoencephalopathy), most prominent in the posterior white matter. Generalised cerebral atrophy disproportionate to age. Basal ganglia T2 signal mildly elevated bilaterally. No focal enhancing lesions. No restricted diffusion. No mass effect. MRS (spectroscopy): elevated choline, reduced NAA in white matter — neuroinflammation and neuronal loss.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral symmetric periventricular and deep white matter T2 hyperintensity (leukoencephalopathy) with cerebral atrophy and basal ganglia involvement is the characteristic MRI pattern of HIV encephalopathy — reflecting the tropism of HIV for macrophages and microglia in the white matter and basal ganglia (HIV does not infect neurons directly). MRS changes (elevated choline indicating membrane turnover, reduced NAA indicating neuronal loss) confirm active neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration. Prognosis improves with ART initiation.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 48-year-old man is referred to neurology with a 6-month history of progressive personality change that his wife has found increasingly concerning.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive personality change with disinhibition, cognitive decline, and new-onset seizures in a middle-aged male is a broad presentation spanning neurodegenerative, infectious, autoimmune, and structural causes. Frontotemporal dementia causes disinhibition and personality change. Autoimmune encephalitis can present with similar features. CNS vasculitis and neurosyphilis are important to exclude as treatable conditions.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He was previously quiet and reserved but now makes crude jokes, has been disinhibited, and has used the company credit card for personal purchases. Memory for recent events has been worsening, and he has had two generalised seizures over the past 2 months. He also reports intermittent headaches.
prompts.reasoning: A self-healing penile ulcer 2 years ago strongly suggests primary syphilis (chancre) — a painless genital ulcer that heals spontaneously is the hallmark of primary syphilis. If untreated, syphilis progresses to secondary and later tertiary/neurosyphilis. The timeline of CNS involvement 2 years after a painless genital ulcer is consistent with neurosyphilis. Frontotemporal dementia and autoimmune encephalitis remain plausible but the sexual history is highly discriminatory.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior STI testing. Multiple sexual partners over the past 5 years including unprotected intercourse. Not vaccinated against HPV. HIV status unknown — never tested. No family history of neurological disease or dementia. Non-smoker. Heavy alcohol use — 30+ units/week. No prior psychiatric history. No recreational drug use.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple sexual partners without STI testing, combined with a prior undiagnosed genital ulcer, significantly raises the probability of syphilis. Alcohol use raises the possibility of alcohol-related frontal lobe damage — but the specific history of a genital ulcer makes syphilis more compelling. Unknown HIV status is important — HIV co-infection accelerates syphilis progression and increases neurosyphilis risk. STI serology is now essential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. MMSE 21/30 — frontal executive tasks impaired (poor abstraction, perseveration), recent memory 1/3. Disinhibited during consultation. Argyll Robertson pupils bilaterally — pupils accommodate but do not react to light (small, irregular, unequal). Deep tendon reflexes brisk throughout. No hemiparesis. Gait normal. Romberg: negative. No skin rashes. Penile scar present.
prompts.reasoning: Argyll Robertson pupils are almost pathognomonic of neurosyphilis — they are the classic 'prostitute's pupil' (accommodates but does not react) resulting from syphilitic damage to the pretectal nuclei in the midbrain. The combination of frontal executive dysfunction, disinhibition, brisk reflexes, and Argyll Robertson pupils in a patient with prior genital ulcer history constitutes the clinical syndrome of general paresis (GPI — general paresis of the insane), a form of neurosyphilis.

prompts.label: Serology and HIV Testing
prompts.text: Serum VDRL: reactive (titre 1:64). Serum TPHA (Treponema pallidum haemagglutination): positive. Serum FTA-ABS (fluorescent treponemal antibody absorbed): positive. HIV test: positive, CD4 320 cells/µL. CSF sent for further analysis.
prompts.reasoning: Reactive VDRL with positive TPHA and FTA-ABS confirms active syphilis infection. VDRL is a non-treponemal test (titre correlates with disease activity — 1:64 indicates active infection). TPHA/FTA-ABS are treponemal-specific confirmatory tests. HIV co-infection is confirmed — HIV accelerates syphilis progression and neurosyphilis is more common in HIV co-infected individuals. CSF analysis is now required to confirm CNS involvement.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis and MRI
prompts.text: CSF: WBC 24 cells/µL (lymphocytes), protein 0.92 g/L (elevated), normal glucose. CSF VDRL: reactive (positive CSF VDRL is diagnostic of neurosyphilis — highly specific, though insensitive). CSF FTA-ABS: positive. MRI brain: bilateral frontal and temporal cortical atrophy with T2 signal abnormality in the medial temporal lobes and basal ganglia, suggesting chronic meningovascular inflammation. Treatment: IV penicillin G 18-24 million units/day for 14 days (neurosyphilis regimen).
prompts.reasoning: Reactive CSF VDRL is the gold standard diagnostic test for neurosyphilis — while insensitive (~50%), a positive result in CSF is diagnostic. The CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein confirm CNS inflammation. MRI cortical and subcortical changes are consistent with chronic syphilitic meningoencephalitis. General paresis is caused by direct Treponema pallidum invasion of cortical neurons — explaining the frontal dementia and disinhibition. High-dose IV penicillin G is the only effective treatment.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 36-year-old man presents to the ED with a 2-week history of progressive right arm weakness and increasing difficulty finding words in speech.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute focal neurological deficits with fever and headache require urgent evaluation for both infectious and neoplastic CNS disease. In the absence of known immune status, the differential spans bacterial abscess, opportunistic infections, primary brain tumour, and metastases. The 2-week progressive course with fever points toward an infectious or inflammatory process.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He also reports severe constant headaches and intermittent low-grade fever over the same period. He has lost 10 kg over the past 3 months and has had drenching night sweats for 6 weeks.
prompts.reasoning: Significant weight loss and night sweats suggest systemic illness or immunosuppression. Cat ownership and undercooked meat consumption are the classic exposure history for Toxoplasma gondii — oocysts shed in cat faeces and tissue cysts in undercooked meat are the two primary transmission routes. These environmental exposures combined with systemic weight loss raise strong suspicion for HIV-associated toxoplasmosis reactivation. CNS lymphoma is an important differential in any immunosuppressed patient.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV-positive — diagnosed 4 years ago, never started on antiretroviral therapy. Last CD4 count 2 years ago was 62 cells/µL. Has avoided medical follow-up due to stigma concerns. Non-smoker. No IV drug use. No prior CNS infection. No prior malignancy. No cotrimoxazole prophylaxis.
prompts.reasoning: CD4 62 (and likely lower now after 2 years without treatment) places this patient at high risk for AIDS-defining opportunistic infections. Cerebral toxoplasmosis is the most common focal CNS lesion in AIDS (CD4 <100). Primary CNS lymphoma is the second most common cause. Both present with focal deficits and mass lesions. Absence of cotrimoxazole prophylaxis removes a key protective factor against toxoplasmosis reactivation. Cryptococcal meningitis can also occur at this CD4 count.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.3°C. GCS 13. Right hemiparesis (arm 3/5, leg 4/5). Broca's aphasia. Bilateral papilloedema. No meningism. No skin lesions or rashes. Oral thrush confirmed. No lymphadenopathy. Toxoplasma IgG: positive (sent from ED — result available).
prompts.reasoning: Positive Toxoplasma IgG confirms prior exposure — in a severely immunosuppressed HIV patient, this means reactivation of latent tissue cysts is the likely mechanism. IgG positivity does not confirm active disease but combined with the clinical picture strongly supports toxoplasmosis. Oral thrush confirms severe immunosuppression. Bilateral papilloedema indicates raised ICP from mass lesion(s). PCNSL is the main differential — empirical toxoplasma treatment is given first, with failure to respond prompting biopsy.

prompts.label: Bloods and CT Head
prompts.text: CD4 count: 28 cells/µL (severely immunosuppressed). HIV viral load: 520,000 copies/mL. WBC 4.2 × 10⁹/L (lymphopaenia). CRP 88 mg/L. CT head with contrast: multiple ring-enhancing lesions at the grey-white junction and basal ganglia bilaterally with surrounding oedema and mass effect. Lesions are hyperdense on non-contrast CT.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions in an AIDS patient with CD4 <100 and positive Toxoplasma IgG is the classic CT presentation of cerebral toxoplasmosis. PCNSL more typically presents as a single periventricular lesion. However, both can be multiple. Standard clinical management: 2-week empirical anti-toxoplasma treatment (pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin). Response confirms toxoplasmosis; failure to respond mandates biopsy for lymphoma.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Treatment Response
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: multiple (4) ring-enhancing lesions at grey-white junctions and left basal ganglia, largest 2.8cm in the left frontal lobe. 'Eccentric target sign' — eccentric nodule of enhancement within the ring. DWI: no restricted diffusion in lesion centres (unlike abscess). After 2 weeks of empirical pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine: repeat MRI shows >50% reduction in lesion size and surrounding oedema. Clinical improvement confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: The eccentric target sign on MRI (an eccentric enhancing nodule within a ring-enhancing lesion) is highly specific for cerebral toxoplasmosis — it represents the necrotic centre of a toxoplasma abscess with an eccentric viable tachyzoite-rich rim. PCNSL does not show this sign. Absence of restricted diffusion distinguishes toxoplasma from bacterial abscess (which restricts diffusion). Most importantly, >50% radiological improvement after 2 weeks of anti-toxoplasma treatment is diagnostic — PCNSL would not respond to anti-parasitic therapy.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old man is referred to neurology with a 6-week history of rapidly progressive cognitive decline.
prompts.reasoning: Rapidly progressive dementia (weeks to months) is a distinct clinical syndrome requiring urgent evaluation. The differential includes CJD, autoimmune and paraneoplastic encephalitides, Wernicke encephalopathy, and very rarely rapidly progressive Alzheimer disease. The tempo of decline — from independence to dependence in 2 months — is faster than typical neurodegenerative disease and demands urgent workup.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His wife reports he has gone from fully independent to requiring assistance with all daily activities in under 2 months. He has developed severe memory loss, confusion, and visuospatial difficulties — he cannot recognise familiar faces or navigate his home — and has developed involuntary jerking movements provoked by sudden noise or touch.
prompts.reasoning: Rapid multidomain cognitive decline with stimulus-sensitive myoclonic jerks is a critical combination — myoclonus with progressive dementia narrows the differential significantly. CJD typically produces myoclonus, cortical blindness, cerebellar ataxia, and akinetic mutism as terminal features. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDA, anti-VGKC/LGI1) can produce rapid cognitive decline with movement disorders. The fearfulness and sleep disturbance may reflect early psychiatric prodrome of CJD.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Retired farmer. Previously received a cadaveric-derived pituitary hormone injection in the 1980s (growth hormone therapy — prior to recombinant hormone era). No blood transfusions. No neurosurgical history. No family history of CJD or prion disease. No recent travel to BSE-endemic areas for vCJD. Non-smoker. Moderate alcohol. No prior psychiatric history.
prompts.reasoning: Cadaveric pituitary-derived growth hormone injection (used before 1985) is a recognised iatrogenic route for CJD transmission — contaminated growth hormone from CJD-infected pituitary glands has caused iatrogenic CJD with very long incubation periods (10-30 years). This is a highly significant risk factor. Farmer occupation was historically associated with exposure to BSE but vCJD from BSE has distinct features (younger patients, psychiatric prodrome, posterior thalamic pulvinar sign). Autoimmune and paraneoplastic encephalitis remain important to exclude as treatable conditions.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 11. Profound disorientation — does not know his name. Stimulus-sensitive myoclonus elicited by hand clap. Rigidity present in all limbs. Cerebellar ataxia on heel-shin test. Visual agnosia — cannot identify objects presented visually. Bilateral extensor plantars. MMSE 8/30 — profoundly impaired across all domains.
prompts.reasoning: The tetrad of rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, cerebellar signs, and visual agnosia constitutes the classic clinical picture of sporadic or iatrogenic CJD. Visual agnosia and cortical blindness reflect occipital cortex involvement. Stimulus-sensitive myoclonus is characteristic of CJD. The combination is less consistent with autoimmune encephalitis (which more typically shows seizures, psychiatric features, and movement disorders without cortical visual loss). EEG and CSF are now critical.

prompts.label: EEG and CSF
prompts.text: EEG: periodic sharp wave complexes (PSWCs) at approximately 1 Hz — bilateral synchronous triphasic sharp waves superimposed on slow background. CSF: normal WBC and glucose, protein mildly elevated. CSF 14-3-3 protein: positive. CSF RT-QuIC (real-time quaking-induced conversion): positive — detects misfolded prion protein seeding activity. Serum anti-NMDA receptor and paraneoplastic antibody panel: negative.
prompts.reasoning: Periodic sharp wave complexes (PSWCs) at 1 Hz on EEG are highly characteristic of CJD — they appear in the middle stage of disease. CSF 14-3-3 protein reflects rapid neuronal destruction (not specific to CJD, but supports the diagnosis). RT-QuIC is now the most sensitive and specific ante-mortem test for CJD (sensitivity 87%, specificity 98%) — it detects prion seeding activity in CSF. Negative autoimmune and paraneoplastic antibody panel excludes the treatable autoimmune encephalitides.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (DWI sequence): cortical ribboning — confluent restricted diffusion in the cortical ribbon pattern across multiple lobes (occipital, temporal, parietal). Bilateral caudate and putamen DWI restriction. T2/FLAIR: basal ganglia hyperintensity. No gadolinium enhancement. No mass lesions. DWI cortical ribboning with basal ganglia involvement confirms sporadic/iatrogenic CJD pattern. Neuropathology at autopsy confirmed: spongiform encephalopathy with PrP^Sc accumulation, neuronal loss, astrogliosis.
prompts.reasoning: Cortical ribboning on DWI (restricted diffusion in the cortical grey matter in a ribbon-like pattern) is the most sensitive MRI finding in CJD — it reflects cytotoxic oedema from rapidly progressive neuronal vacuolation. Basal ganglia DWI restriction (caudate and putamen) is the other characteristic finding. These MRI features, combined with positive RT-QuIC, PSWCs on EEG, and the iatrogenic risk factor, fulfil diagnostic criteria for probable CJD. There is no treatment — this is universally fatal.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old man presents to the ED with a 5-day history of fever, agitation, and intermittent episodes of extreme restlessness and muscle spasms.
prompts.reasoning: Fever with agitation, muscle spasms, and progressive confusion constitutes an encephalitic syndrome requiring broad differential. Rabies, viral encephalitis, tetanus (muscle spasms without encephalopathy), bacterial meningitis, and autoimmune encephalitis are all plausible. Psychiatric causes and drug intoxication should be excluded. The muscle spasms with fever and encephalopathy are the key distinguishing early features.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over the past 2 days he has become increasingly confused with labile mood, alternating between profound agitation and periods of lucidity. He has hypersalivation, episodic profuse sweating, and his heart rate has been fluctuating between 60 and 150 bpm without a clear cause.
prompts.reasoning: Hydrophobia (fear of water with pharyngeal spasm on drinking) and aerophobia (extreme fear/spasm on exposure to air currents) are the pathognomonic features of furious (encephalitic) rabies — they result from lyssavirus-induced limbic hyperexcitability and autonomic dysfunction. A dog bite 6 weeks ago in a rabies-endemic country (India) without post-exposure prophylaxis is the classic exposure history. This combination effectively makes rabies the diagnosis, though confirmation is required.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He is a backpacker recently returned from 6 months travelling through rural India and Southeast Asia; he is not vaccinated against rabies and received no pre-travel immunisations specific to animal-borne viral infections. No prior psychiatric history, no drug use, HIV negative.
prompts.reasoning: Travel through rural India and Southeast Asia without rabies pre-exposure vaccination maximises rabies risk — India has the highest global burden of human rabies deaths. No prior rabies vaccination means no pre-existing immunity. Once neurological symptoms develop, rabies is almost universally fatal regardless of treatment. Viral encephalitis from other arboviruses (Japanese encephalitis, dengue) is also possible given the travel history, but neither causes hydrophobia or aerophobia.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.1°C; HR 138 bpm; GCS fluctuating 9–13; profoundly agitated. A healed scar is visible on the dorsum of the left hand consistent with an old animal bite wound. Attempts to offer the patient water trigger visible distress and laryngopharyngeal spasm; directing a fan toward his face provokes violent spasms and terror. No focal hemiparesis.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of hydrophobia, aerophobia, autonomic instability (hypersalivation, diaphoresis, dilated pupils, tachycardia), agitation, and encephalopathy with the bite scar from a rabies-endemic area is the full clinical syndrome of furious rabies encephalitis. No other diagnosis produces this combination. The Rabies lyssavirus travels retrogradely along peripheral nerves to the CNS — the incubation period (2-12 weeks) reflects the distance of the bite from the brain (hand bite: ~6 weeks to CNS).

prompts.label: Bloods and CSF
prompts.text: WBC 14.2 × 10⁹/L (lymphocytosis); CRP 78 mg/L. CSF: WBC 48/µL (lymphocytes), protein 0.8 g/L, glucose normal — consistent with aseptic encephalitis. MRI brain: T2 hyperintensity in the brainstem, hippocampi, and hypothalamus; no ring-enhancing lesions; no abscess. CSF herpes simplex PCR, enterovirus PCR, and arbovirus panel: all negative.
prompts.reasoning: MRI brainstem and limbic system involvement is consistent with rabies encephalitis — the lyssavirus has a predilection for the limbic system (hippocampus, hypothalamus) and brainstem, explaining hydrophobia (brainstem), aerophobia (brainstem), and behavioural changes (limbic). CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis confirms viral encephalitis. The clinical syndrome with this MRI pattern and exposure history is sufficient for a clinical diagnosis of rabies.

prompts.label: Confirmatory Testing
prompts.text: Skin biopsy (nape of neck): direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) test positive for rabies antigen in cutaneous nerve fibres. Saliva PCR: rabies lyssavirus RNA detected. Serum and CSF rabies virus neutralising antibodies: positive with rising titres. Corneal impression smear: DFA positive. Diagnosis confirmed: rabies encephalitis (furious form); patient deteriorated to coma within 72 hours and died on day 14 despite intensive supportive care.
prompts.reasoning: Skin biopsy DFA of the nape of the neck (around hair follicles, where cutaneous nerve fibres are concentrated) is the most reliable ante-mortem diagnostic test for rabies — sensitivity 60-100%. Saliva PCR and corneal impression tests provide additional confirmation. Once clinical rabies develops, survival is <1% — only a handful of documented survivals (Milwaukee protocol) have been reported. Prevention through pre-exposure vaccination and immediate post-exposure prophylaxis (wound cleansing + RIG + vaccine) is the only effective intervention.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 5-year-old unvaccinated child is brought to the ED from a refugee camp with a 4-day history of fever, irritability, and progressive leg weakness that developed acutely 2 days ago.
prompts.reasoning: Acute flaccid weakness (floppy limbs) in a young child with fever requires urgent evaluation to distinguish poliomyelitis from other causes of acute flaccid paralysis (AFP). Guillain-Barré syndrome, transverse myelitis, acute flaccid myelitis (enterovirus D68), and spinal cord compression must all be considered. Vaccination status is critical — unvaccinated children in endemic or outbreak settings are at highest risk for polio.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Three days before the leg weakness, she had a fever, headache, and vomiting. Then she developed acute right leg weakness. The weakness appeared over hours and has not progressed further over 2 days. She has no arm weakness, no difficulty breathing, and no bladder problems. The weak leg appears floppy rather than stiff.
prompts.reasoning: The two-phase illness — febrile prodrome followed by acute asymmetric flaccid paralysis of one limb — is the classic pattern of poliomyelitis. Asymmetric, focal limb involvement is characteristic of polio (poliovirus destroys anterior horn cells in specific segments). GBS is typically ascending, symmetric, and bilateral. Transverse myelitis typically causes bilateral weakness with bladder dysfunction. The acute asymmetric nature significantly raises polio probability.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior vaccinations — family recently displaced from a conflict zone. Living in an overcrowded camp with poor sanitation and limited clean water access. Three other children in the same camp have developed similar leg weakness in the past 2 weeks. No family history of neuromuscular disease. No prior neurological illness.
prompts.reasoning: Cluster of similar cases in an unvaccinated community with poor sanitation is a polio outbreak pattern — poliovirus is transmitted faecal-orally and spreads rapidly in settings with poor sanitation and low vaccine coverage. Multiple cases in the same environment make a common infectious aetiology likely. Acute flaccid myelitis (EV-D68) typically follows a respiratory illness and also clusters. WHO requires reporting of all AFP cases in under-15s.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.1°C. Alert and communicating normally — no encephalopathy. Right leg: flaccid paralysis (power 0-1/5 throughout), absent deep tendon reflexes (areflexia), normal sensation throughout. Left leg and arms: normal power and reflexes. No bladder dysfunction. No cranial nerve abnormality. Neck stiffness present. Spinal tenderness absent.
prompts.reasoning: Focal right leg flaccid paralysis with areflexia and preserved sensation is the classic LMN pattern of anterior horn cell destruction — motor neurons destroyed, sensory neurons spared (poliovirus is neurotropic for motor anterior horn cells only). Preserved sensation distinguishes polio from most peripheral nerve disorders. Neck stiffness indicates meningeal irritation from the viral infection. GBS would show bilateral, ascending, symmetric weakness with sensory symptoms. The unilateral pure motor pattern is highly characteristic of polio.

prompts.label: Bloods and CSF
prompts.text: WBC 11.4 × 10⁹/L (lymphocytosis). CRP 38 mg/L. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis (WBC 88/µL), elevated protein (0.72 g/L), normal glucose — aseptic meningitis pattern. NCS/EMG: right leg — absent compound motor action potentials (CMAPs), preserved sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs), normal F-waves on left — unilateral anterior horn cell pattern. Stool specimens collected for poliovirus isolation.
prompts.reasoning: Preserved SNAPs with absent CMAPs on NCS confirms pure motor (anterior horn cell) pathology — sensory fibres are intact, motor fibres are destroyed. This is the electrophysiological hallmark of anterior horn cell disease (polio, ALS, acute flaccid myelitis). CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis confirms viral CNS infection. Acute flaccid myelitis (EV-D68) has an identical electrophysiological pattern — stool viral PCR is critical for differentiation.

prompts.label: Virology
prompts.text: Stool PCR: poliovirus type 1 detected. Stool virus isolation: poliovirus serotype 1 (Sabin-like sequence — vaccine-derived poliovirus, VDPV). CSF enterovirus PCR: poliovirus type 1 positive. WHO AFP surveillance notification submitted. MRI spine: T2 hyperintensity in the right anterior horn cells at L3-L5 spinal cord levels — anterior horn cell signal change confirms the site of neuronal destruction.
prompts.reasoning: Poliovirus isolation from stool is the definitive diagnostic test — stool shedding persists for 4-6 weeks and is the primary surveillance specimen. CSF poliovirus PCR confirms CNS involvement. MRI showing anterior horn cell T2 signal change at the lumbar levels corresponding to the affected limb confirms the site and mechanism of injury. Vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV) occurs when the oral polio vaccine Sabin strain reverts to neurovirulence in under-immunised populations — particularly in settings with low vaccine coverage and poor sanitation.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 74-year-old female is brought to the memory clinic by her son with a 2-year history of gradually progressive forgetfulness that has worsened over the past 6 months, interfering with her daily activities.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive cognitive decline over 2 years in a 74-year-old requires systematic evaluation for dementia subtypes and reversible causes. Alzheimer disease is the most common cause of dementia in this age group. Vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, and frontotemporal dementia are the main differentials. Normal pressure hydrocephalus, depression, and thyroid dysfunction must be excluded as reversible causes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She repeats questions within minutes of asking them and forgets appointments she has just made. She gets lost navigating familiar routes. She has difficulty managing her finances and recently left the gas cooker on twice. No personality change, no visual hallucinations, no urinary incontinence. Decline has been gradual and progressive without stepwise deterioration.
prompts.reasoning: Episodic memory failure (repetition, forgetting recent events) and topographical disorientation (getting lost on familiar routes) are characteristic cortical features of Alzheimer disease. Gradual progressive decline without stepwise deterioration argues against vascular dementia (which typically steps). Absence of visual hallucinations makes Lewy body dementia less likely. Absence of urinary incontinence and gait disorder reduces NPH. Financial difficulty suggests executive dysfunction as well as memory impairment.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Treated hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. No stroke or TIA history. No Parkinson disease. No head trauma. No family history of early-onset Alzheimer disease or genetic dementia syndromes. Non-smoker. No heavy alcohol use. Former teacher. Widowed, lives alone. Not depressed per son's account. APOE status unknown.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia are vascular risk factors — present but no prior stroke or TIA makes vascular dementia less prominent. No Parkinson disease or REM sleep behaviour disorder makes Lewy body dementia less likely. No prior head trauma (reduces chronic traumatic encephalopathy). No family history of early-onset AD reduces risk of dominantly inherited forms (PSEN1/2, APP mutations). High lifetime cognitive reserve (teacher) may delay symptom onset but does not prevent AD.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: MMSE 19/30: impaired delayed recall (0/3), disoriented to date and place, naming intact, registration intact. No focal neurological deficits. Normal gait. No extrapyramidal signs (no rigidity, no tremor). No apraxia or aphasia. Cardiovascular examination unremarkable. BP 148/86 mmHg. No postural hypotension. No frontal release signs. No primitive reflexes.
prompts.reasoning: Moderate MMSE impairment dominated by episodic memory failure (recall 0/3) with orientation errors. Normal gait and absence of extrapyramidal signs make Lewy body dementia and Parkinson dementia less likely. Normal gait without urinary symptoms makes NPH effectively excluded. No focal deficits makes vascular dementia less prominent. The memory-predominant cortical pattern with intact language is most consistent with Alzheimer disease.

prompts.label: Bloods and Neuropsychology
prompts.text: TFTs, B12, folate, FBC, renal function, LFTs, glucose, calcium, CRP: all normal — reversible causes excluded. Neuropsychological testing: severe episodic memory impairment (learning and delayed recall at <5th percentile), mild executive and visuospatial dysfunction. Language and praxis relatively preserved. MRI brain: hippocampal atrophy bilaterally (greater than expected for age), medial temporal lobe atrophy on coronal views, no vascular lesions, no hydrocephalus.
prompts.reasoning: Normal metabolic bloods exclude all reversible causes. Neuropsychological profile of severe episodic memory impairment (hippocampal-dependent) with relative sparing of language and praxis is the classical neuropsychological signature of Alzheimer disease. Hippocampal atrophy on MRI reflects entorhinal cortex and hippocampal neuronal loss — the earliest pathological site in AD (transentorhinal stage). No vascular lesions on MRI reduces vascular dementia.

prompts.label: Biomarker Confirmation
prompts.text: CSF biomarkers: low Aβ42 (reduced amyloid clearance), elevated total tau (neurodegeneration marker), elevated phospho-tau (neurofibrillary tangle marker). Aβ42/40 ratio reduced (consistent with amyloid pathology). Amyloid PET: diffuse cortical amyloid deposition in the precuneus, posterior cingulate, and frontal cortex — pattern consistent with Alzheimer disease amyloid pathology. Diagnosis confirmed: probable Alzheimer disease (2018 NIA-AA criteria met — clinical + biomarker evidence).
prompts.reasoning: CSF biomarker profile (low Aβ42, high p-tau, high t-tau) reflects the dual pathology of AD: amyloid plaque deposition (reduces free Aβ42 in CSF) and neurofibrillary tau tangles (release tau into CSF). Amyloid PET positivity confirms in vivo amyloid pathology. This combination fulfils the 2018 NIA-AA AT(N) framework: A (amyloid positive), T (tau positive), N (neurodegeneration positive) — satisfying the biological definition of Alzheimer disease regardless of clinical severity.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 68-year-old male presents to the neurology outpatient clinic with a 2-year history of tremor affecting his right hand that is most noticeable when he is sitting quietly and diminishes when he reaches for objects.
prompts.reasoning: Rest tremor in a 68-year-old has a broad differential. Essential tremor is the most common tremor disorder and is typically postural/action rather than rest. Parkinson disease presents with rest tremor. Drug-induced parkinsonism is common in older adults on dopamine-blocking medications. Multiple system atrophy and PSP are Parkinson-plus syndromes. Wilson disease is unlikely at 68 but should not be dismissed without appropriate history.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The tremor is present at rest and stops when he picks up objects. Over the past year he has noticed his writing has become smaller and cramped. His wife says his face looks expressionless. He finds it difficult to start walking — he describes his feet 'sticking to the floor'. His voice has softened. He also mentions that he has not been able to smell properly for 3 years and has had constipation for 4 years.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of rest tremor, micrographia, hypomimia (masked facies), initiation difficulty (freezing of gait), hypophonia, anosmia, and constipation together constitute the full pre-motor and motor syndrome of Parkinson disease. Anosmia and constipation are pre-motor features reflecting early Lewy body pathology in the olfactory bulb and enteric nervous system (Braak stages 1-2). MSA can mimic PD but typically shows more prominent autonomic and cerebellar features. Drug-induced parkinsonism requires medication history.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Hypertension treated with amlodipine. Not on any dopamine-blocking medications (no antipsychotics, no metoclopramide, no prochlorperazine). No prior head trauma. No stroke history. No family history of Parkinson disease or tremor disorder. Non-smoker. No pesticide or herbicide exposure. Works as a retired accountant.
prompts.reasoning: No dopamine-blocking medications effectively excludes drug-induced parkinsonism — the most important clinical differential. No prior stroke history makes vascular parkinsonism less likely. No family history reduces but does not exclude genetic forms (LRRK2, PINK1, GBA-associated PD — common with late-onset sporadic PD). MSA and vascular parkinsonism remain on the differential. Examination will be key for distinguishing idiopathic PD from atypical parkinsonism.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Marked hypomimia (masked facies). Right-hand rest tremor at 4-5 Hz, 'pill-rolling' quality, suppressed with voluntary action. Right-sided cogwheel rigidity (racheted resistance throughout range). Bradykinesia: slowness and decrement in amplitude on finger tapping. Gait: reduced right arm swing, stooped posture, short shuffling steps. Postural instability: mild. No cerebellar signs. No ophthalmoplegia. BP sitting 128/78, standing 126/76 (no orthostasis). MMSE 28/30.
prompts.reasoning: The cardinal motor triad of Parkinson disease is confirmed: rest tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia. Asymmetric onset (right-sided) is a key feature of idiopathic PD — MSA and PSP typically present symmetrically. No orthostatic hypotension at this stage (MSA typically shows early prominent autonomic failure). No vertical gaze palsy (PSP). No cerebellar ataxia (MSA-C). Relatively good cognition (MMSE 28). Asymmetric presentation favours idiopathic PD over atypical parkinsonism.

prompts.label: DaTscan and Bloods
prompts.text: Bloods: TFTs normal (excludes thyroid tremor), FBC normal, copper/caeruloplasmin normal (excludes Wilson disease), metabolic panel normal. MRI brain: mild age-appropriate atrophy, no basal ganglia infarcts, no hydrocephalus, no midbrain atrophy (no 'hummingbird sign' of PSP). DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT): asymmetrically reduced uptake in the left putamen (right > left deficit, consistent with right-sided symptoms) — confirmed presynaptic dopaminergic deficit.
prompts.reasoning: DaTscan confirms presynaptic dopaminergic deficit — distinguishing true dopaminergic parkinsonism (PD, MSA, PSP) from drug-induced parkinsonism and essential tremor (both have normal DaTscans). Asymmetric putaminal uptake reduction (left putamen predominantly) is characteristic of early idiopathic PD — MSA and PSP are more symmetric. No hummingbird sign (midbrain atrophy on MRI) excludes PSP. Normal copper excludes Wilson disease.

prompts.label: Levodopa Response Trial
prompts.text: Levodopa challenge (co-careldopa 100/25 mg): administered with UPDRS motor scoring. At 60 minutes post-dose: motor UPDRS improved by 38% from baseline (reduction in tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia). Sustained improvement maintained at 12 weeks on regular levodopa with no motor fluctuations. Diagnosis: Idiopathic Parkinson Disease (UK Brain Bank criteria met — bradykinesia + at least one of: rigidity, rest tremor, postural instability; asymmetric onset, sustained levodopa response, no atypical features).
prompts.reasoning: A sustained and robust levodopa response (>30% UPDRS motor improvement) is a key supportive criterion for idiopathic PD and helps differentiate it from MSA and PSP (which show poor or transient levodopa response). Excellent levodopa response reflects intact postsynaptic striatal dopamine receptors — in atypical parkinsonism, receptor damage is also present. UK Brain Bank criteria are met: bradykinesia present, plus rest tremor and rigidity; asymmetric onset; no atypical features; sustained levodopa response confirms the diagnosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old male is referred to neurology by his GP with a 2-year history of involuntary writhing movements of his arms, irritability, and progressive decline in work performance noticed by his employer.
prompts.reasoning: Involuntary writhing movements (chorea) with behavioural change and cognitive decline in a middle-aged adult requires systematic evaluation. Huntington disease is the most common hereditary chorea in adults. Drug-induced chorea (levodopa, dopamine agonists, oral contraceptives), Wilson disease, neuroacanthocytosis, and autoimmune chorea are all important differentials. Family history and genetic testing will be critical.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The movements are involuntary, flitting from limb to limb, and interfere with purposeful movement. He has been increasingly irritable and aggressive at home and has made financially reckless decisions over the past year. His wife reports he has also become mildly forgetful. He was dismissed from his management position 6 months ago due to poor judgement. He noticed his gait becoming awkward over the past 18 months.
prompts.reasoning: The triad of chorea, behavioural/psychiatric disturbance (irritability, disinhibition), and cognitive decline is the classic clinical presentation of Huntington disease. In HD, psychiatric symptoms (depression, irritability, OCD, disinhibition) often precede motor features by years. Executive dysfunction and behavioural change are frontostriatal features. Wilson disease can also cause chorea with psychiatric features but typically presents in younger patients (5-35 years). Family history is now essential.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Father died aged 54 in a residential care facility — his family believes he had 'a nervous condition' and spent his last years unable to walk or speak. A paternal aunt had 'jerky movements' and died young. No consanguinity. No antipsychotic or dopamine-blocking medication use. Non-smoker. Minimal alcohol. Not on OCP. No prior rheumatic fever.
prompts.reasoning: Father's death in care with progressive neurological disability and a paternal aunt with similar movements strongly suggests an autosomal dominant hereditary condition — consistent with Huntington disease (autosomal dominant, CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in HTT gene on chromosome 4). Anticipation in HD means each generation may have earlier onset. No rheumatic fever history excludes Sydenham chorea. No antipsychotic use excludes drug-induced chorea. No jaundice or liver disease makes Wilson disease less likely.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Generalised chorea — flowing, involuntary movements of face, trunk, arms, and legs. Saccadic eye movements (intrusion saccades during smooth pursuit). Gait: wide-based, lurching, 'drunken' quality. Dysarthria. MMSE 23/30 — executive function and attention impaired. Impaired tandem gait. No Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit lamp examination. No hepatomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: Saccadic pursuit eye movements are a sensitive early sign of HD — reflecting caudate nucleus and frontal eye field degeneration. Generalised chorea with saccadic pursuit, executive dysfunction, and gait disturbance in a 42-year-old with positive family history is clinically diagnostic of HD. Absent Kayser-Fleischer rings and no hepatomegaly effectively exclude Wilson disease. Neuroacanthocytosis can cause chorea but also causes orofacial dyskinesia, peripheral neuropathy, and acanthocytes on blood film.

prompts.label: Bloods and MRI Brain
prompts.text: FBC: no acanthocytes on blood film. Liver function tests, serum copper, caeruloplasmin: normal — Wilson disease excluded. VDRL: negative. Anti-NMDA receptor and anti-CASPR2 antibodies: negative. Thyroid function normal. MRI brain: bilateral caudate nucleus atrophy (loss of normal caudate head convexity — 'boxcar' ventricles with lateral concavity rather than normal medial convexity). Putaminal atrophy. Cortical atrophy disproportionate to age.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral caudate atrophy ('boxcar' ventricles — the normal caudate head convexity is lost, causing the lateral ventricles to appear enlarged and square) is the characteristic MRI finding of HD — reflecting selective striatal neurodegeneration. Normal copper/caeruloplasmin excludes Wilson disease. Absent acanthocytes excludes neuroacanthocytosis. Negative autoimmune panel excludes autoimmune chorea. Genetic testing is now the definitive confirmatory step.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing
prompts.text: Huntington disease genetic test (HTT CAG repeat analysis, after formal genetic counselling): 47 CAG repeats on one allele (normal <27; 27-35 intermediate; 36-39 reduced penetrance; ≥40 full penetrance). ≥40 CAG repeats confirms full-penetrance Huntington disease. Genetic counselling provided to patient and family regarding autosomal dominant inheritance (50% risk to each child), predictive testing options for at-risk relatives, and end-of-life planning.
prompts.reasoning: 47 CAG repeats in the HTT gene on chromosome 4p16.3 confirms full-penetrance Huntington disease. The number of CAG repeats inversely correlates with age of onset — longer repeats cause earlier onset (juvenile HD >60 repeats). Anticipation (increasing repeat length with successive generations) is seen in paternally inherited HD. This is caused by instability of the CAG repeat during spermatogenesis. No cure exists — treatment is symptomatic (tetrabenazine for chorea, SSRIs for depression, antipsychotics for agitation).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with a 9-month history of progressive weakness starting in his right hand and spreading to his right arm.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive limb weakness with bulbar involvement (dysarthria) in a middle-aged male spans motor neuron disease, cervical myelopathy, multifocal motor neuropathy, and myopathic causes. The combination of peripheral (hand) and bulbar symptoms involving motor pathways at multiple levels is the key clinical problem. MND must be considered early as aggressive diagnostic evaluation is needed. Cervical myelopathy is a critical treatable alternative.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The hand weakness began with difficulty gripping and turning keys and has spread up the arm; he can no longer raise it above his head. Over the past 3 months his speech has become slurred and his wife finds him harder to understand at the end of long sentences. He has also noticed intermittent twitching in his arms, thighs, and calves over the past 2 months; no sensory symptoms, no bladder or bowel dysfunction.
prompts.reasoning: Fasciculations (twitching) in multiple limb muscles indicate lower motor neuron degeneration — fasciculations arise from spontaneous discharge of motor units. Combined with progressive weakness and bulbar symptoms without sensory symptoms, this is highly suspicious for MND. Cervical myelopathy can cause upper and lower limb weakness but rarely causes fasciculations without sensory symptoms. Kennedy disease (SBMA) causes slowly progressive LMN disease with bulbar involvement. Preserved sensation argues strongly against cervical cord pathology.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological history. No family history of MND, dementia, or hereditary neuropathy. Non-smoker. Moderate alcohol. No heavy metal exposure. Not on statins or other myotoxic drugs. Retired electrician. No prior trauma. BMI 24. No dysphagia yet.
prompts.reasoning: No family history of MND (90% of ALS is sporadic). No toxic exposure or medication cause. Sporadic ALS peak onset is 55-75 years. Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) is an important treatable differential — it causes pure progressive motor deficits with conduction block on NCS and responds to IVIg. Cervical myelopathy remains until MRI excludes it. The spreading nature across multiple body regions raises the level of concern for MND.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Dysarthria (slurred, spastic-spasmodic quality). Right arm: wasting and weakness (deltoid 3/5, biceps 3/5, intrinsic hand muscles 2/5). Fasciculations visible in right arm, bilateral thighs, and calves. Right arm reflexes brisk (paradoxically brisk in a wasted limb — UMN + LMN coexisting). Bilateral hyperreflexia in legs with upgoing plantars. Hoffman's sign positive right. Jaw jerk brisk. No sensory deficit on examination. Forced vital capacity 82% predicted.
prompts.reasoning: The coexistence of UMN signs (brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars, brisk jaw jerk, Hoffman's sign) and LMN signs (wasting, fasciculations, weakness) in the same limb is the cardinal feature of ALS — the simultaneous degeneration of both upper and lower motor neurons. MMN shows only LMN signs. Cervical myelopathy can cause UMN below and LMN at the level of the lesion, but not fasciculations throughout, and would show sensory signs. The combination of bulbar + upper limb + lower limb involvement with both UMN and LMN signs at multiple levels is characteristic of ALS.

prompts.label: EMG/NCS and Bloods
prompts.text: NCS: normal motor conduction velocities, no conduction block (excludes MMN). Sensory NCS: normal throughout (excludes peripheral sensory neuropathy). EMG: widespread active denervation (fibrillation potentials, positive sharp waves) and chronic reinnervation (large polyphasic motor units, reduced recruitment) in 3 body regions (right arm, left leg, bulbar on tongue EMG). Anti-GM1 ganglioside antibodies: negative (excludes MMN). CK mildly elevated (388 U/L).
prompts.reasoning: EMG showing denervation in ≥3 body regions (limbs + bulbar) fulfils the El Escorial/Awaji criteria for definite ALS — widespread LMN involvement at multiple levels. Normal sensory NCS confirms pure motor pathology. Absent conduction block and negative anti-GM1 antibodies definitively exclude MMN. Mildly elevated CK reflects active denervation and motor unit degeneration. MRI spine required to exclude structural pathology as a contributing factor.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Spine
prompts.text: MRI cervical and thoracic spine: no cord compression, no signal change, no structural pathology — cervical myelopathy excluded. MRI brain: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in bilateral corticospinal tracts (posterior limb of internal capsule to cerebral peduncles) — UMN axonal degeneration. No mass lesions. Genetic testing: SOD1, FUS, TARDBP mutations not detected — sporadic ALS confirmed. Riluzole initiated. Multidisciplinary team referral: physiotherapy, dietetics, speech and language, respiratory.
prompts.reasoning: MRI corticospinal tract T2 signal (Wallerian-like degeneration of UMN axons) provides imaging evidence of UMN involvement — when present, it is highly supportive of ALS but is not present in all patients. Normal spine MRI definitively excludes cervical myelopathy as a confounding structural cause. Sporadic ALS is confirmed with no SOD1/FUS/TARDBP mutations. Riluzole (glutamate antagonist) is the only disease-modifying treatment — it extends survival by approximately 3-4 months.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 16-year-old boy is brought by his parents with a 3-year history of progressively worsening balance problems and frequent falls.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive ataxia in a teenager requires evaluation for hereditary ataxia syndromes. Friedreich's ataxia is the most common autosomal recessive hereditary ataxia with onset typically 5-15 years. Spinocerebellar ataxias (autosomal dominant) typically present in adulthood. Multiple sclerosis can cause ataxia in young people. Ataxia-telangiectasia, vitamin E deficiency, and metabolic causes are important to consider. Age of onset and mode of inheritance will narrow the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He was previously active in school sports but had to stop 18 months ago as his balance has deteriorated to the point where he now requires a wall or walking aid. He has also noticed progressive weakness in his legs and increasing clumsiness in his hands, and his speech has become mildly slurred over the past 6 months.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive ataxia with limb weakness, dysarthria, and — critically — cardiac symptoms (palpitations, exertional chest pain) in a teenager is a highly specific combination. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is present in ~65% of Friedreich's ataxia patients and is the leading cause of death. Pes cavus (high foot arch) is a structural foot deformity from early chronic peripheral neuropathy in FA. Spinocerebellar ataxias rarely cause cardiac disease. This combination is strongly suggestive of FA.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: His parents are first cousins and an older sister has milder balance difficulties diagnosed as early-onset ataxia of unknown cause. No family history of dominant neurological disease; no environmental exposures, toxins, or drug use; normal birth and early developmental milestones.
prompts.reasoning: Consanguinity (parental first-cousin marriage) greatly increases the risk of autosomal recessive disease — the statistical probability of homozygosity for a recessive allele is 1/16 in first-cousin offspring. An affected sibling with similar symptoms further supports autosomal recessive inheritance. Spinocerebellar ataxias are autosomal dominant and would not have this inheritance pattern. Friedreich's ataxia follows an autosomal recessive pattern, consistent with this family history.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Wide-based lurching gait requiring support; bilateral limb ataxia on finger-nose and heel-shin testing; absent ankle and knee jerks with bilateral extensor plantar responses; impaired vibration and proprioception in both feet; pes cavus bilaterally; scoliosis present; soft systolic murmur at the left sternal edge; dysarthria with scanning quality.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of cerebellar ataxia, absent deep tendon reflexes, extensor plantar responses, impaired vibration/proprioception (posterior column involvement), pes cavus, scoliosis, and cardiac murmur constitutes the classic examination findings of Friedreich's ataxia. The coexistence of UMN signs (extensor plantars) and LMN signs (areflexia) with sensory ataxia reflects the mixed spinocerebellar, dorsal column, and corticospinal tract degeneration of FA. No other diagnosis produces this precise combination.

prompts.label: Cardiac and Neurophysiology
prompts.text: Echocardiogram: concentric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (interventricular septum 15mm, normal <12mm) — symmetrical LV wall thickening without outflow obstruction. ECG: T-wave inversion in lateral leads. NCS/EMG: absent sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) bilaterally — sensory axonal neuropathy. Motor NCS: normal conduction velocity. MRI brain and spine: cerebellar and spinal cord atrophy, particularly posterior columns.
prompts.reasoning: Concentric hypertrophic cardiomyopathy confirmed on echocardiogram — the characteristic cardiac lesion of FA (not asymmetric septal hypertrophy as in genetic HCM). Absent SNAPs with preserved CMAPs confirm large fibre sensory axonal neuropathy — the peripheral component of FA's dorsal root ganglion neurodegeneration. Posterior column and cerebellar atrophy on MRI are consistent. All three components (cerebellar, sensory, cardiac) confirm FA.

prompts.label: Genetic Confirmation
prompts.text: Frataxin gene (FXN) analysis: homozygous GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in intron 1 of the FXN gene — 780 GAA repeats on allele 1, 640 GAA repeats on allele 2 (normal <33 repeats; disease: >66 repeats). This GAA repeat expansion causes epigenetic silencing of the FXN gene, reducing frataxin protein production. Frataxin protein level (blood): severely reduced (8% of normal). Diagnosis confirmed: Friedreich's ataxia (GAA-expansion confirmed).
prompts.reasoning: Homozygous GAA repeat expansion in FXN intron 1 is pathognomonic for Friedreich's ataxia — present in 96% of cases. The GAA expansion causes triplex DNA formation and heterochromatin, silencing the FXN gene and reducing frataxin (a mitochondrial iron-sulfur protein). Frataxin deficiency impairs mitochondrial iron handling, leading to oxidative stress in neurons and cardiomyocytes. Repeat length inversely correlates with age of onset and directly with disease severity. Omaveloxolone is the first FDA-approved treatment for FA.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 5-day history of painless visual loss in her right eye.
prompts.reasoning: Acute unilateral visual loss in a young adult requires urgent ophthalmological evaluation. Optic neuritis, ischaemic optic neuropathy, retinal detachment, uveitis, and compressive lesions all enter the differential. The subacute 5-day onset with colour desaturation in a young female suggests demyelinating optic neuritis — the commonest initial presentation of MS. NMOSD is an important differential as it has a different treatment strategy.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Her vision has become blurred centrally — she cannot read with the right eye — and colours appear dim and washed out, particularly reds. She also reports pain behind the right eye that worsens on eye movement. Two years ago she had an episode of left leg numbness and tingling lasting 4 weeks that resolved spontaneously without medical attention.
prompts.reasoning: Pain with eye movement is characteristic of optic neuritis — the inflamed optic nerve is tethered at the annulus of Zinn, causing pain when extraocular muscles tug on it. Dyschromatopsia (colour desaturation, particularly for red) is an early and sensitive sign of optic neuritis. A prior episode of limb paraesthesia 2 years ago suggests a prior demyelinating episode — making this the second clinically isolated demyelinating syndrome in time and space, which fulfils criteria for MS. NMOSD is a critical differential.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior diagnosed neurological disease — previous leg paraesthesia episode not investigated. No family history of MS or autoimmune disease. No prior uveitis or joint disease. Northern European ancestry. Non-smoker. Takes the oral contraceptive pill. No prior head or spinal trauma. Works as a physiotherapist. No Leber hereditary optic neuropathy family history.
prompts.reasoning: Northern European ancestry confers higher MS genetic susceptibility (HLA-DRB1*15:01 association). OCP use does not significantly affect MS risk. No family history (MS is multifactorial — 3% risk in first-degree relatives). No prior uveitis argues against sarcoidosis. No family history of Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy (mitochondrial optic neuropathy — often confused with optic neuritis). NMOSD is more common in non-European populations and associated with anti-AQP4 antibodies.

prompts.label: Physical Examination and Ophthalmology
prompts.text: Visual acuity right eye: 6/24 (reduced from 6/6 left). Relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) right eye (Marcus Gunn pupil — right pupil dilates when swung from left to right). Colour vision: Ishihara plates — 2/14 correct with right eye (8/14 left). Fundoscopy: right optic disc normal (retrobulbar neuritis — most optic neuritis is retrobulbar). Mild right eye pain on movement. No exophthalmos. Neurological examination: intact, no residual deficit from prior episode.
prompts.reasoning: RAPD (afferent pupillary defect) confirms optic nerve dysfunction — when light is swung to the affected eye, both pupils paradoxically dilate because the diseased optic nerve transmits less light signal. RAPD is a sensitive objective sign of optic neuritis. Normal fundus (retrobulbar neuritis — 65% of cases) distinguishes from anterior optic neuritis (papillitis) seen in young children or NMOSD. The combination of current optic neuritis with prior unexplained limb sensory episode fulfils McDonald criteria for MS (dissemination in space and time). AQP4 and MOG antibodies are needed.

prompts.label: Bloods and VEPs
prompts.text: AQP4 antibody (NMO-IgG): negative — NMOSD effectively excluded. MOG-IgG antibody: negative. Visual evoked potentials (VEPs): right P100 latency prolonged (132ms, normal <115ms) — right optic nerve conduction delay confirming demyelination. FBC, ESR, CRP, ANA, ANCA: normal — systemic inflammation or vasculitis excluded. CSF: mild lymphocytosis (8 cells/µL), mildly elevated protein, oligoclonal bands (OCBs) present (not in matching serum) — intrathecal IgG synthesis confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: Negative AQP4 antibody definitively excludes NMOSD — the most important clinical differential with very different management implications. Negative MOG-IgG excludes MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD). Prolonged VEP P100 latency is objective evidence of right optic nerve demyelination. Oligoclonal bands in CSF (not serum) indicate intrathecal antibody production — present in >95% of MS patients. All findings confirm MS with high confidence.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Spinal Cord
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: multiple periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions — three of the periventricular lesions are ovoid and perpendicular to the corpus callosum ('Dawson's fingers'). One right optic nerve T2 signal change and enhancement on fat-saturated gadolinium sequences. MRI cervical spine: one T2 lesion at C5 (partial width, <3 vertebral segments — MS pattern). McDonald 2017 criteria met: dissemination in space (≥2 characteristic CNS regions) and time (acute enhancing lesion + non-enhancing lesions). Diagnosis: Relapsing-remitting MS. Treatment: high-efficacy disease-modifying therapy initiated.
prompts.reasoning: Dawson's fingers (ovoid periventricular lesions perpendicular to the corpus callosum on sagittal FLAIR) are the classic MRI hallmark of MS — caused by perivenular demyelination along medullary veins. The combination of periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord lesions fulfils the 2017 McDonald dissemination in space criteria. Enhancing optic nerve lesion confirms the current active clinical lesion. Partial transverse myelitis cord lesion (<3 vertebral segments) is the MS pattern — NMOSD typically shows long confluent cord lesions (>3 segments).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 47-year-old man is brought to the ED by his flatmate in a confused and unsteady state.
prompts.reasoning: Acute encephalopathy in a patient with heavy alcohol use has a broad differential. Wernicke encephalopathy, alcohol withdrawal, hepatic encephalopathy, hypoglycaemia, subdural haematoma (common after falls), meningitis, and sepsis must all be rapidly evaluated. The combination of heavy alcohol use and malnutrition is the classical setting for Wernicke encephalopathy.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His flatmate reports he has been drinking heavily and barely eating for the past 3 weeks, with persistent vomiting preventing him from keeping food down, and that this deterioration began over the past 2 days. He is unsteady on his feet and can barely stand without support; his flatmate also notes that one eye does not move properly when he tries to follow objects.
prompts.reasoning: The clinical triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia (abnormal eye movements) in a malnourished heavy drinker is the classic presentation of Wernicke encephalopathy. Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency from malnutrition, impaired absorption (alcohol), and increased demand causes failure of thiamine-dependent enzymes in the mammillary bodies, periaqueductal grey, and ocular motor nuclei. Alcohol withdrawal causes agitation and seizures but not ophthalmoplegia or ataxia. Hepatic encephalopathy causes asterixis and confusion. The ophthalmoplegia is the key discriminating feature.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Long-standing alcohol use disorder — 40+ units/week for 10 years. Poor dietary intake — lives on beer and white bread. No vegetable or meat intake for months. Previous admission for alcohol withdrawal 2 years ago — no prior Wernicke's episode documented. No prior liver disease diagnosed. Non-smoker. Lives alone (flatmate visits occasionally). No IV drug use.
prompts.reasoning: Severe long-standing alcohol use with gross malnutrition is the highest-risk profile for Wernicke encephalopathy — thiamine stores are depleted in 3-4 weeks without dietary intake. Any administration of glucose (IV dextrose) in a thiamine-depleted patient can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy by consuming remaining thiamine for glucose metabolism. Persistent vomiting (3 weeks) independently causes thiamine loss. Prior alcohol withdrawal without WE does not prevent current WE.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 12 (E3V4M5). Temperature 36.8°C. Bilateral horizontal nystagmus. Bilateral CN VI palsy (failure of lateral gaze bilaterally). Left CN III partial palsy (partial ptosis). Gait: unable to stand without support — severe truncal ataxia. No asterixis. No meningism. Hepatomegaly present. Malnourished appearance. Blood glucose (fingerprick): 5.2 mmol/L (normal). Jaundice absent.
prompts.reasoning: The clinical triad is confirmed: ophthalmoplegia (bilateral CN VI palsy, partial CN III palsy, nystagmus) + truncal ataxia + encephalopathy (GCS 12). This triad appears simultaneously only in Wernicke encephalopathy — it reflects thiamine-dependent degeneration of the periaqueductal grey (CN VI, CN III nuclei, vestibular nuclei) and vermis (ataxia) and mammillary bodies (memory/confusion). Normal glucose excludes hypoglycaemia. Absent asterixis and jaundice makes hepatic encephalopathy less likely.

prompts.label: Bloods
prompts.text: Serum thiamine: undetectable (<7 nmol/L; normal 70-180 nmol/L). Blood glucose: normal. LFTs: elevated GGT and mildly elevated ALT/AST — alcoholic hepatitis pattern. Bilirubin normal. Albumin low (28 g/L). FBC: macrocytic anaemia (MCV 106 fL). Folate low. B12 normal. PT mildly prolonged. CRP 18 mg/L. Blood cultures: negative. IMMEDIATELY after sampling: IV Pabrinex (thiamine 500mg TDS) commenced — before any glucose infusion.
prompts.reasoning: Undetectable serum thiamine confirms severe thiamine deficiency — the biochemical cause of Wernicke encephalopathy. The macrocytic anaemia reflects concurrent folate and likely B12/folate deficiency from malnutrition. Normal bilirubin and absent jaundice with mildly elevated LFTs indicate alcoholic liver disease without hepatic failure — hepatic encephalopathy effectively excluded. IV high-dose thiamine must be administered BEFORE or with any glucose — glucose without thiamine can precipitate or worsen WE by depleting remaining thiamine stores.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (performed after thiamine commenced): T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in bilateral mammillary bodies, periaqueductal grey matter, and medial thalami (around 3rd ventricle). Diffusion restriction in mammillary bodies (cytotoxic oedema). Gadolinium enhancement of mammillary bodies. Mammillary body signal change is symmetric. After 5 days of IV Pabrinex 500mg TDS: ophthalmoplegia resolved, ataxia improved. Confusion partially improving. Diagnosis confirmed: Wernicke encephalopathy with partial treatment response.
prompts.reasoning: Symmetric T2 hyperintensity in the mammillary bodies, periaqueductal grey, and medial thalami is the pathognomonic MRI pattern of Wernicke encephalopathy — reflecting thiamine-dependent oxidative damage in these metabolically active structures. Mammillary body enhancement indicates active blood-brain barrier breakdown from acute thiamine-deficient injury. Early response to thiamine (ophthalmoplegia resolves first, then ataxia) confirms the diagnosis. Persistent confusion after treatment may indicate progression to Korsakoff syndrome if mammillary body damage is irreversible.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 54-year-old male is referred to the memory clinic from a psychiatric unit with a 6-month history of profound memory impairment and confabulation following an acute hospital admission.
prompts.reasoning: Severe amnesia with confabulation following a prior acute neurological episode requires evaluation for acquired amnestic syndrome. Korsakoff syndrome following Wernicke encephalopathy is a leading diagnosis. Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia, and other dementias must be considered. The history of an acute episode preceding the amnesia is a critical distinguishing feature from insidiously progressive dementias.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Six months ago he was hospitalised for a 2-day confusional state with unsteady gait and double vision — treated briefly and discharged. Since discharge he has been unable to form any new memories. He cannot remember meeting any of his care team despite daily interactions. When asked about recent activities, he invents detailed and plausible but false stories. His remote memory for events from 20 years ago is well preserved.
prompts.reasoning: Profound anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories) with intact remote memory and confabulation following an acute encephalopathic episode with ophthalmoplegia/ataxia is the classic presentation of Korsakoff syndrome developing after Wernicke encephalopathy. Confabulation (producing plausible false memories without awareness of their falsity) is specific to amnestic syndromes from diencephalic damage. Bilateral hippocampal infarction causes a similar amnestic syndrome but without confabulation as a prominent feature.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has a 25-year history of heavy alcohol dependence (50+ units/week) and severe malnutrition confirmed at his prior hospital admission 6 months ago. The prior acute episode involved confusion, unsteady gait, and an eye movement abnormality lasting 2 days before discharge. No family history of dementia; no prior head trauma; cirrhosis on prior liver biopsy; currently in an alcohol rehabilitation unit.
prompts.reasoning: The prior acute episode with ophthalmoplegia and confusion was Wernicke encephalopathy — inadequately treated with only 24 hours of thiamine (current guidelines recommend minimum 5-7 days IV Pabrinex 500mg TDS). Wernicke encephalopathy progresses to Korsakoff syndrome when mammillary body and thalamic damage is irreversible — thiamine must be given early and in adequate doses to prevent this. Child-Pugh B cirrhosis confirms advanced liver disease. Hepatic encephalopathy is excluded by absence of current liver decompensation.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Alert and cooperative. MMSE 15/30 — severely impaired delayed recall (0/3), disoriented to date and place, language intact, praxis intact. When asked about recent activities he gives fluent, detailed, entirely false accounts without apparent awareness of their inaccuracy. No ophthalmoplegia. Peripheral neuropathy present: absent ankle jerks and impaired vibration sense bilaterally. Hepatomegaly on palpation.
prompts.reasoning: Selective severe anterograde amnesia (recall 0/3) with intact language, praxis, and remote memory, combined with confabulation, is the cardinal syndrome of Korsakoff syndrome — reflecting diencephalic (mammillary body + thalamus) damage rather than hippocampal or cortical damage. In Alzheimer disease, language and praxis also deteriorate. Peripheral neuropathy reflects co-existing thiamine/B1 deficiency affecting peripheral nerves. Absent ophthalmoplegia confirms Wernicke phase has passed.

prompts.label: Neuropsychological Testing and Bloods
