prompts.text: Neuropsychological testing: severe anterograde amnesia (learning and delayed recall at <1st percentile), severely impaired recognition memory; intact language (VOSP naming normal), intact visuospatial skills, mildly impaired executive function. Remote memory (autobiographical interview): well-preserved for events >10 years prior. Thiamine level: low-normal. LFTs: mildly elevated. No acute hepatic decompensation. MRI requested.
prompts.reasoning: The neuropsychological profile — profoundly impaired memory encoding and retrieval with preserved language, visuospatial, and remote memory — is the signature of diencephalic amnesia. In Alzheimer disease, all cognitive domains progressively deteriorate. This selective memory impairment with confabulation and intact other domains is specific to Korsakoff syndrome. The double dissociation between severe memory and relatively intact other cognition is a hallmark.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: bilateral mammillary body atrophy (small, shrunken — best seen on T1 sagittal midline view). Bilateral mediodorsal thalamic signal change and mild atrophy. Old T2* (GRE/SWI) haemosiderin in mammillary bodies (from prior haemorrhagic necrosis during Wernicke episode). Mild generalised cerebral atrophy. No new enhancing lesions. No hippocampal atrophy out of proportion to age. No vascular lesions. The memory circuit (hippocampus → fornix → mammillary bodies → mammillothalamic tract → anterior thalamus → cingulate) is disrupted by irreversible mammillary body and thalamic damage.
prompts.reasoning: Mammillary body atrophy with haemosiderin deposits (indicating prior haemorrhagic necrosis during the Wernicke episode) and mediodorsal thalamic atrophy are the structural correlates of Korsakoff syndrome. The mammillary bodies and mediodorsal thalami are the critical nodes in the Papez circuit (memory circuit) — their destruction irreversibly impairs episodic memory consolidation. Unlike Alzheimer disease, hippocampal atrophy is not prominent. Korsakoff syndrome is irreversible — prevention through adequate thiamine treatment of Wernicke encephalopathy is the only effective strategy.

case_0049.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
case_0049.finalDiagnosis: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old male presents with a 4-month history of progressive tingling and numbness in both feet and hands, along with increasing unsteadiness when walking, particularly in the dark.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive bilateral sensory symptoms in the distal limbs with gait ataxia in a 62-year-old requires evaluation for both peripheral and central sensory pathway disease. Peripheral neuropathy (metabolic, toxic), cervical myelopathy, subacute combined degeneration (B12/copper deficiency), and multiple sclerosis all enter the differential. The symmetric bilateral distribution and gait ataxia suggest dorsal column involvement.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The tingling affects both feet symmetrically and has spread to his hands over 4 months. He has become increasingly unsteady when walking in dim light or with his eyes closed — he grabs walls in dark corridors. His legs feel weak. He has also noticed subtle difficulty concentrating and mild forgetfulness over the past 2 months. He has been a strict vegan for the past 4 years without B12 supplementation.
prompts.reasoning: Ataxia worse in the dark with eyes closed (Romberg-positive pattern) indicates loss of proprioception from dorsal column damage — the vestibular and visual systems can partially compensate in light, but without vision the deficit is unmasked. Four years of strict veganism without B12 supplementation is a classic risk factor for B12 deficiency — vegans have no dietary source of B12. Cognitive changes suggest cerebral involvement. Symmetric sensory symptoms with posterior column ataxia raises SCD strongly.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Long-standing strict vegan diet without B12 supplementation. No alcohol use. No prior gastric surgery or gastrointestinal disease. Not on metformin or proton pump inhibitors (which can reduce B12 absorption). No family history of neurological disease. No toxic exposures. No prior malignancy. Non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Strict veganism for 4 years without B12 supplementation is the most common non-autoimmune cause of B12 deficiency in younger adults — animal products are the sole dietary source of B12. No gastric surgery (which reduces intrinsic factor) and no malabsorptive disease means dietary deficiency is the likely mechanism. No metformin use (which reduces B12 absorption in the terminal ileum). Copper deficiency myelopathy produces an identical MRI and clinical syndrome — important to test serum copper alongside B12.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: MMSE 25/30 (attention mildly reduced). Bilateral reduced vibration sense in feet (absent at ankles, reduced at knees). Bilateral absent proprioception at the toes. Romberg's sign positive (falls with eyes closed). Gait: wide-based, high-stepping (steppage gait). Power bilaterally 4/5 in legs. Reflexes: absent ankle jerks (LMN component from peripheral neuropathy), brisk knee jerks and bilateral extensor plantars (UMN component from cord involvement). No cranial nerve abnormality.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of absent ankle jerks (LMN peripheral neuropathy) with brisk knee jerks and upgoing plantars (UMN cord involvement) — the same coexistence of UMN and LMN signs — is the hallmark of subacute combined degeneration of the cord. Dorsal column loss (vibration, proprioception) with corticospinal tract involvement (UMN signs) and peripheral nerve involvement (LMN signs) represents the 'combined' in SCD — simultaneous posterior and lateral column degeneration. This combination is essentially pathognomonic.

prompts.label: Bloods
prompts.text: Serum B12: 78 pmol/L (severely low; normal 150-700 pmol/L). Methylmalonic acid: markedly elevated (2.8 µmol/L; normal <0.4). Homocysteine: elevated (42 µmol/L; normal <15). FBC: macrocytic anaemia (MCV 112 fL). Blood film: hypersegmented neutrophils. Serum copper: normal (excludes copper deficiency myelopathy). Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies: positive — confirming autoimmune pernicious anaemia as the underlying cause.
prompts.reasoning: Severe B12 deficiency confirmed biochemically. Elevated methylmalonic acid and homocysteine are metabolic markers of functional B12 deficiency — B12 is required for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (MMA) and methionine synthase (homocysteine). Macrocytic anaemia with hypersegmented neutrophils is the haematological hallmark. Normal copper excludes copper deficiency myelopathy. Positive anti-intrinsic factor antibodies diagnose pernicious anaemia — the autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells prevents intrinsic factor secretion and thus B12 absorption in the terminal ileum.

prompts.label: MRI Spine
prompts.text: MRI cervical and thoracic spine (T2-weighted, sagittal and axial): bilateral symmetric T2 hyperintensity in the posterior columns (dorsal cord) and lateral corticospinal tracts of the cervical and thoracic cord — the characteristic 'inverted V' or 'inverted rabbit ears' sign on axial T2 imaging. No cord compression or mass lesion. MRI brain: periventricular T2 white matter changes (mild). Intramuscular B12 injections commenced — at 3 months, sensory symptoms improved and MRI cord changes partially resolved.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral posterior and lateral column T2 signal in the spinal cord — creating the 'inverted V' pattern on axial views (the posterior columns appear as two bright dots like rabbit ears) — is the MRI hallmark of subacute combined degeneration. This reflects demyelination of the fasciculus gracilis and cuneatus (posterior columns) and lateral corticospinal tracts (lateral columns). Response to B12 replacement confirms the metabolic aetiology. Pernicious anaemia requires lifelong IM B12 injections (as oral absorption requires intrinsic factor).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old man with known liver cirrhosis is brought to the ED by his wife with a 2-day history of increasing confusion and drowsiness.
prompts.reasoning: Acute confusion in a cirrhotic patient has a broad differential. Hepatic encephalopathy is the most common cause in this setting but subdural haematoma (coagulopathy from cirrhosis), septic encephalopathy (SBP, UTI, pneumonia), Wernicke encephalopathy (malnutrition), and hypoglycaemia must all be excluded rapidly. Alcohol withdrawal is possible if recent cessation. Each diagnosis has a different management pathway.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over the past 3 days he has become progressively confused and disoriented. His wife reports he was constipated for 5 days before the confusion started, and he had a large upper GI bleed 10 days ago (haematemesis, managed conservatively). He had a urinary tract infection treated with antibiotics 2 weeks ago. No fever currently. He has not had alcohol for 3 months.
prompts.reasoning: Two precipitating factors for hepatic encephalopathy are identified: constipation (increased gut transit time → increased ammonia absorption from bacterial proteolysis) and the recent GI bleed (blood in the gut provides a large protein load → high ammonia production). Both are classic precipitants of HE. No current fever makes active sepsis less likely but UTI may have been undertreated. No recent alcohol intake makes withdrawal unlikely at 3 months. These precipitants strongly suggest HE.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Alcohol-related liver cirrhosis — Child-Pugh B, MELD score 14 at last review. Previous episodes of decompensation: two prior ascitic taps, one prior HE episode (Grade II). Not on lactulose or rifaximin at home. On spironolactone and propranolol. Non-drinker for 3 months (confirmed by wife). No IV drug use. No family history of liver disease. No Wilson disease known.
prompts.reasoning: Established cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B) with prior HE episodes confirms vulnerability to recurrent HE. Absence of lactulose/rifaximin prophylaxis (despite prior HE) is a management gap — lactulose reduces ammonia absorption, rifaximin reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria. Alcohol abstinence for 3 months makes Wernicke less acute but malnutrition in cirrhosis can still cause thiamine deficiency. Prior HE sets the pattern for recurrent episodes with identifiable precipitants.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.2°C. GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Jaundice present. Bilateral asterixis (flapping tremor — cannot maintain posture with wrists extended). Mild neck stiffness absent. No focal hemiparesis. Bilateral hyperreflexia. Mild parotid enlargement. Ascites moderate. Spider naevi on chest. No meningism. Fetor hepaticus (sweet, musty smell on breath). Urinalysis: nitrites negative.
prompts.reasoning: Asterixis (flapping tremor from inability to maintain sustained posture — due to metabolic brain dysfunction impairing motor circuits) is the clinical hallmark of hepatic encephalopathy. Fetor hepaticus (the characteristic sweet/musty smell from mercaptans produced by gut bacteria acting on sulfur compounds) further supports HE. Jaundice, ascites, and spider naevi confirm decompensated cirrhosis. Normal temperature makes acute sepsis less likely. Negative urinalysis makes UTI precipitant less likely. Asterixis with fetor in a cirrhotic is effectively diagnostic of HE.

prompts.label: Bloods
prompts.text: Serum ammonia: 138 µmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <50). Bilirubin elevated (82 µmol/L). ALT, AST moderately elevated. PT/INR: 1.8 (prolonged coagulopathy). Albumin: 24 g/L (low). Creatinine: 96 µmol/L (normal). Glucose: 4.6 mmol/L (normal — excludes hypoglycaemia). FBC: thrombocytopaenia 82 × 10⁹/L. CRP: 22 mg/L. Blood cultures: no growth. CT head: cerebral oedema, no subdural collection, no haemorrhage.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated serum ammonia confirms the biochemical basis of HE — ammonia from gut bacterial proteolysis bypasses the urea cycle (impaired in cirrhosis) and enters the systemic circulation, crossing the BBB to cause astrocyte swelling and cerebral dysfunction. Normal glucose excludes hypoglycaemia. Normal CT head excludes subdural haematoma. Absent infection on blood cultures and CRP makes septic encephalopathy less likely. HE precipitants (constipation + GI bleed + missed lactulose) explain the acute decompensation.

prompts.label: Treatment Response and EEG
prompts.text: Treatment initiated: lactulose titrated to 2-3 loose stools/day, oral rifaximin commenced, dietary protein adjusted to 1.2 g/kg/day (adequate but not excessive), IV thiamine given empirically. EEG: triphasic waves on background slow activity — pattern consistent with metabolic/toxic encephalopathy, characteristic of HE. At 48 hours: GCS improved to 14, asterixis resolved. Ammonia reduced to 68 µmol/L. Diagnosis confirmed: hepatic encephalopathy Grade III, precipitated by constipation and prior GI bleed.
prompts.reasoning: Triphasic waves on EEG are characteristic of metabolic encephalopathies (particularly hepatic and uraemic) — they are not specific to HE but support the metabolic aetiology and exclude non-convulsive status epilepticus. Response to lactulose/rifaximin treatment with ammonia reduction and clinical improvement confirms HE. HE is graded I-IV (West Haven criteria): Grade I (mild confusion), II (drowsy, gross disorientation), III (stupor, incomprehensible speech), IV (coma). This patient had Grade III. Maintenance rifaximin and lactulose should prevent recurrence.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 7-month-old infant is brought to the paediatric clinic by her parents because she appears to be regressing — she is less responsive than before and no longer reaches for toys as she did at 4 months.
prompts.reasoning: Developmental regression (loss of previously acquired skills) in an infant is always pathological and requires urgent investigation. The differential spans lysosomal storage diseases (Tay-Sachs, Niemann-Pick A, Gaucher type 2), mitochondrial disorders, neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis, and epileptic encephalopathy. Regression at 7 months after normal early development is the classic pattern of late infantile-onset storage disease.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She was developing normally until 4 months — rolling, reaching for objects, socially smiling. Since 5 months, she has stopped reaching, lost interest in her surroundings, and has become hypotonic. Her parents have noticed she startles violently to sudden sounds — even soft sounds cause the whole body to jerk. She has had two brief seizure-like episodes last week. No hearing difficulty suspected by parents.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of developmental regression, progressive hypotonia, and an exaggerated startle response (hyperekplexia) in an infant is a highly specific presentation pointing to neuronal lipid storage disease. In Tay-Sachs, the hyperekplexia results from neuronal dysfunction in the brainstem reticular formation from GM2 ganglioside accumulation. Niemann-Pick A and Gaucher type 2 can produce similar early neurodegeneration. Seizure onset adds to the severity.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Both parents are Ashkenazi Jewish — born in Israel. Neither parent has had prior carrier screening. A maternal cousin died in infancy with 'a nervous condition' that was never diagnosed. Normal pregnancy and birth — full term, normal birth weight. No consanguinity. No family history of epilepsy. No medications. No toxin exposure.
prompts.reasoning: Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry is a critical risk factor for Tay-Sachs disease — carrier frequency is 1/30 in Ashkenazi Jews vs 1/300 in the general population (due to a founder effect). Niemann-Pick type A also has elevated frequency in Ashkenazi Jews (carrier frequency 1/80). The maternal cousin dying in infancy with a neurological condition suggests another affected family member — consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance. Prenatal screening was not performed. Both diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Weight below 3rd percentile. Generalised profound hypotonia ('floppy infant'). No voluntary movement of limbs. Absent visual tracking — does not follow faces. Hyperekplexia — violent full-body jerk to hand clap. Fundoscopy: bilateral cherry-red spot at the fovea — central bright red area surrounded by pale opaque retina. No hepatosplenomegaly. No lymphadenopathy. Head circumference normal.
prompts.reasoning: The cherry-red macular spot is the pathognomonic finding of GM2 gangliosidosis: the fovea appears red because it is perfused by the choroid (visible through the thin foveal tissue), while the surrounding retinal ganglion cells — swollen and opaque from GM2 ganglioside accumulation — appear white, creating the striking contrast. CRITICALLY: absence of hepatosplenomegaly distinguishes Tay-Sachs from Niemann-Pick A and Gaucher type 2, which both have organomegaly. Sandhoff disease is the main remaining differential — clinically identical to Tay-Sachs but with organomegaly.

prompts.label: Enzymatic Assay
prompts.text: Leucocyte enzyme assay: Hexosaminidase A (HexA): severely reduced (3% of normal control). Hexosaminidase B (HexB): normal. HexA/Total hexosaminidase ratio: severely reduced. (In Sandhoff disease: both HexA AND HexB are reduced.) This pattern confirms isolated HexA deficiency — characteristic of Tay-Sachs disease (HEXA gene mutation). MRI brain: T2 hyperintensity in basal ganglia and thalami bilaterally. EEG: multifocal spike-wave complexes.
prompts.reasoning: Isolated HexA deficiency with normal HexB is the enzymatic signature of Tay-Sachs disease — distinguishing it from Sandhoff disease (HexA + HexB both reduced). HexA normally degrades GM2 ganglioside — its deficiency causes progressive neuronal GM2 accumulation and the relentless neurodegeneration of Tay-Sachs. MRI basal ganglia and thalamic signal is consistent. No treatment exists — Tay-Sachs is uniformly fatal, typically by age 2-3 years. Prevention is through carrier screening of Ashkenazi Jewish couples.

prompts.label: Genetic Confirmation
prompts.text: HEXA gene sequencing: homozygous c.1278insTATC (p.Tyr427IlefsTer5) frameshift mutation — the most common Ashkenazi Jewish Tay-Sachs mutation, causing complete absence of HexA protein production. Both parents confirmed heterozygous carriers. Genetic counselling provided: 25% recurrence risk per pregnancy; prenatal diagnosis available via CVS or amniocentesis for future pregnancies. Confirmed: infantile Tay-Sachs disease. Palliative care initiated.
prompts.reasoning: Homozygous c.1278insTATC mutation in HEXA is the classic Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutation — responsible for ~80% of Tay-Sachs cases in this population. The frameshift causes a premature stop codon, producing no functional HexA protein. Autosomal recessive inheritance confirmed — both parents are heterozygous carriers. The combination of enzymatic assay and genetic confirmation provides a definitive molecular diagnosis. Carrier screening programmes in Ashkenazi Jewish communities have dramatically reduced Tay-Sachs incidence.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 5-year-old boy is referred to the paediatric neurology clinic with a 2-year history of progressive clumsiness and frequent falls.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive ataxia and cognitive decline in a 5-year-old spans hereditary ataxias, metabolic storage diseases, and developmental disorders. Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) typically presents between ages 2-15 with ataxia and cognitive decline. Hereditary ataxias, other lysosomal storage diseases, and metabolic conditions must be considered. The combination of motor and cognitive regression is key.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He is falling behind his peers at school and his teacher has noted attention difficulties and deteriorating behaviour over the past year. His parents describe clear neurological deterioration: he has become dysarthric, and has had episodes of sudden loss of muscle tone causing him to drop objects or fall briefly without losing consciousness. His parents have also noticed he seems to have difficulty looking upward and tilts his head back to look up at things.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of ataxia, dysarthria, and — critically — vertical gaze palsy (difficulty looking upward, compensated by head tilting) is the classic triad of Niemann-Pick disease type C. Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (failure of upward gaze with normal oculocephalic reflex — the eyes can move up reflexively but not voluntarily) is the pathognomonic sign of NPC, reflecting cholesterol accumulation in the rostral interstitial nucleus of the medial longitudinal fasciculus. Gelastic cataplexy (sudden partial loss of muscle tone) also occurs in NPC.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Prolonged neonatal jaundice managed conservatively. Hepatosplenomegaly noted at age 18 months but not further investigated. No family history known initially — parents are first cousins from a consanguineous Pakistani family. A sibling died in infancy with unexplained liver disease. No BCG scar. Not on medications. Born and raised in the UK.
prompts.reasoning: Neonatal cholestatic jaundice with subsequent hepatosplenomegaly is a recognised early systemic manifestation of NPC — occurring in 50-60% of patients before CNS signs develop. A sibling dying in infancy with liver disease suggests another affected family member, consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance. Consanguinity (parental first-cousin marriage) increases homozygosity risk. NPC type A also causes hepatosplenomegaly but has earlier severe neurological onset (infantile). NPC type C causes milder visceral disease with later-onset neurological involvement.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy — complete failure of voluntary upward gaze; normal oculocephalic reflex (doll's eye) upward (confirming supranuclear, not nuclear, lesion). Saccadic horizontal pursuit. Ataxic gait. Dysarthria. Mild spasticity in legs. Hepatosplenomegaly confirmed on palpation. MMSE equivalent for age: below 5th percentile. No cherry-red spot on fundoscopy. No peripheral neuropathy.
prompts.reasoning: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy is the pathognomonic sign of Niemann-Pick type C — the preserved oculocephalic reflex (brainstem ocular motor circuits intact) with absent voluntary vertical saccades (supranuclear pathway damaged) confirms the lesion is above the brainstem nuclei. Absent cherry-red spot distinguishes NPC from Tay-Sachs and Niemann-Pick A (which have cherry-red spots). Hepatosplenomegaly with neurological involvement confirms a combined visceral-neurological storage disease.

prompts.label: Biochemistry and Filipin Test
prompts.text: Plasma oxysterol levels (7-ketocholesterol, 3β,5α,6β-triol): markedly elevated — reflecting impaired intracellular cholesterol trafficking. Filipin staining of skin fibroblasts (cholesterol fluorescence test): markedly positive — intense perinuclear fluorescent cholesterol accumulation (classic pattern, not variant). Liver biopsy: foamy histiocytes with NPC1-associated cholesterol storage. MRI brain: cerebellar atrophy, T2 signal change in the thalami.
prompts.reasoning: Elevated plasma oxysterols are the most sensitive biomarker for NPC (sensitivity >99%) — reflecting impaired NPC1/NPC2-mediated intracellular cholesterol transport, causing oxysterols to accumulate in plasma. Filipin staining of fibroblasts is the gold standard functional diagnostic test — filipin binds free cholesterol and fluoresces; in NPC, cholesterol accumulates perinuclearly in late endosomes/lysosomes. Cerebellar atrophy on MRI reflects Purkinje cell loss from cholesterol accumulation.

prompts.label: Genetic Confirmation
prompts.text: NPC1 gene sequencing: compound heterozygous mutations (p.I1061T on one allele — the most common NPC1 mutation — and a novel splice-site mutation on the second allele). NPC1 mutations account for 95% of NPC cases (NPC2 mutations account for 5%). Miglustat initiated (approved for NPC — substrate reduction therapy reducing glucosylceramide synthesis, stabilising disease progression). Genetic counselling provided for family members. Diagnosis confirmed: Niemann-Pick Disease type C.
prompts.reasoning: Compound heterozygous NPC1 mutations confirm the diagnosis — p.I1061T is the most common allele, causing protein misfolding and premature degradation. NPC1 protein normally shuttles cholesterol from late endosomes to the ER and plasma membrane — its loss causes cholesterol to accumulate in the late endosomal/lysosomal compartment. Miglustat (N-butyldeoxynojirimycin) is the only approved disease-modifying treatment for NPC — it inhibits glucosylceramide synthase, reducing glycosphingolipid synthesis and slowing neurological progression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 2-day-old female neonate is reviewed in the neonatal unit following identification of a visible mass on the lower back at birth.
prompts.reasoning: A visible posterior midline mass at birth overlying the spine requires urgent evaluation to distinguish neural tube defects (myelomeningocele, meningocele) from other sacral masses. Myelomeningocele is the most serious — involving herniated spinal cord and nerve roots. Lipomyelomeningocele and sacrococcygeal teratoma are important differentials. The character of the mass (sac versus solid, skin-covered versus ulcerated) guides urgency.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The mass is located at the lumbosacral level; it is approximately 4 cm in diameter, fluid-filled with a thin translucent membrane wall, and has been present since birth with no change in size. The surrounding skin is intact at the periphery of the mass.
prompts.reasoning: A thin-walled, non-skin-covered sac with raw membrane at the base in the lumbosacral region indicates open spina bifida — the failure of posterior neural tube closure. Absence of leg movement and fixed club feet indicate motor denervation of the lower limbs from spinal cord involvement — distinguishing myelomeningocele (cord herniated into sac, causing paralysis) from meningocele (only meninges herniate, spinal cord intact, neurological function preserved). Absent urine voiding suggests neurogenic bladder. Sacrococcygeal teratoma is a solid mass, not a thin-walled sac.

prompts.label: Maternal History
prompts.text: The neonate has not voided urine since birth; on examination she shows no spontaneous or stimulus-induced movements in either leg, and both feet are fixed in equinovarus position. The mother took folic acid only from 12 weeks gestation rather than pre-conceptually, and the 20-week anatomy scan did not identify this lesion.
prompts.reasoning: Folic acid initiated at 12 weeks (after neural tube closure at 28 days post-conception) provides no protection against NTD — folic acid must be taken from at least 4 weeks before conception through the first 12 weeks. The recommended dose is 400 µg/day (5 mg/day for high-risk women including prior NTD pregnancies). Valproate use during pregnancy increases NTD risk 10-fold (inhibits folic acid metabolism). No valproate use removes this risk factor. Normal anomaly scan does not exclude NTD — sensitivity depends on operator and machine quality.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Detailed examination of the sac: the surface at the centre is raw and non-epithelialised with CSF seeping at the margins. Anal sphincter tone is absent on gentle stimulation; bladder is distended — catheterisation drains 300 mL of urine. Anterior fontanelle is full but not tense; head circumference 35 cm (50th centile).
prompts.reasoning: Absent leg movement with intact pain stimulus confirms motor and sensory denervation at the lumbosacral level — confirming spinal cord involvement (myelomeningocele, not meningocele). Absent anal tone and neurogenic bladder (urinary retention) indicate sacral plexus denervation (S2-S4). Meningocele preserves motor and sensory function because only meninges herniate. Chiari II malformation (hindbrain herniation through the foramen magnum) is present in virtually all myelomeningocele cases and must be assessed urgently.

prompts.label: Imaging
prompts.text: Urgent MRI spine and brain: spinal cord and nerve roots herniated into the posterior sac at the L3–L4 level, confirming neural tissue within the sac; tethered cord identified; Chiari II malformation present with hindbrain herniation through the foramen magnum; bilateral ventriculomegaly consistent with early hydrocephalus.
prompts.reasoning: MRI confirming spinal cord herniation into the sac, Chiari II malformation, and ventriculomegaly completes the diagnostic picture. Chiari II is pathognomonic of myelomeningocele — it is caused by the chronic CSF leak from the open NTD which fails to distend the embryonic rhombencephalic vesicle, causing the hindbrain to herniate caudally. Hydrocephalus occurs in 80-90% of myelomeningocele patients from the Chiari II obstruction and will require ventriculoperitoneal shunting.

prompts.label: Surgical Management and Genetics
prompts.text: Urgent surgical closure of the defect performed within 24 hours of birth. VP shunt placed for hydrocephalus at day 10. Neurological outcome at 6 months: bilateral lower limb paralysis at the L3 level with preserved hip flexion but absent ankle and foot movement, neurogenic bladder requiring clean intermittent catheterisation, and neurogenic bowel; high-dose pre-conceptual folic acid (5 mg/day) recommended for future pregnancies given 3–5% recurrence risk.
prompts.reasoning: The surgical and neurological outcome confirms lumbosacral myelomeningocele (spina bifida cystica) — the most severe form of spina bifida. Neurological level at L3 means hip flexion and partial knee extension are preserved but distal leg function is absent. Clean intermittent catheterisation is the standard management for neurogenic bladder. The 3-5% recurrence risk drives recommendations for high-dose folic acid (5mg/day) in subsequent pregnancies. In utero surgical repair (before 26 weeks) is now an option in specialist centres and improves neurological outcomes compared to postnatal repair.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 24-year-old woman in her first pregnancy is referred urgently to the fetal medicine unit after an abnormality at the fetal head is identified on her 20-week routine anomaly scan.
prompts.reasoning: An anomaly at the fetal head on second-trimester ultrasound requires systematic evaluation to distinguish neural tube defects (anencephaly, encephalocele) from other structural abnormalities (microcephaly, hydranencephaly). Anencephaly is the most severe NTD — failure of anterior neural tube closure causing absence of the cerebral hemispheres. Encephalocele involves herniation of brain tissue through a skull defect. The distinction has profound implications for prognosis and counselling.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The sonographer has documented absence of the calvarium (skull vault) above the orbits and absence of the cerebral hemispheres — the fetal head appears as a flat, frog-like appearance at the top. Polyhydramnios is noted (excessive amniotic fluid). The fetal spine and limbs appear structurally normal on initial survey. There is normal cardiac activity. No maternal symptoms other than routine pregnancy discomforts.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of the skull vault (calvarium) and cerebral hemispheres on ultrasound is the defining finding of anencephaly — the 'frog-eye' or flat-headed appearance is classic. Polyhydramnios results from impaired fetal swallowing (no cerebral cortex to coordinate swallowing reflex). Acrania (absent skull without brain destruction) is an earlier stage — exencephaly (brain exposed without skull) progresses to anencephaly. Encephalocele would show a skull defect with herniated brain tissue, not absence of the entire calvarium.

prompts.label: Maternal History
prompts.text: Unplanned pregnancy — no folic acid supplementation taken before or during pregnancy. No prior pregnancies. No family history of neural tube defects. No antiepileptic medication use (valproate, carbamazepine). No diabetes. No obesity. No consanguinity. No MTHFR mutation history known. No radiation exposure. Born in the UK — not from a high-risk population. First-trimester nuchal translucency scan was normal.
prompts.reasoning: No folic acid supplementation is the most preventable risk factor — periconceptional folic acid 400 µg/day prevents approximately 70% of NTDs. No antiepileptic use (particularly valproate — most teratogenic AED for NTDs). Normal nuchal translucency at 12 weeks does not exclude NTD. Anencephaly typically becomes apparent on ultrasound between 11-14 weeks (acrania stage) and is clearly evident by 20 weeks. No first-trimester diagnosis suggests the scan was not specifically evaluated for NTD markers.

prompts.label: Fetal Medicine Detailed Ultrasound
prompts.text: Detailed fetal medicine ultrasound: absent cerebral hemispheres (telencephalon absent). Absent calvarium above the orbits — exposed neural tissue ('brain stem/area cerebrovasculosa' remnant visible). Frog-like appearance of the fetal head. Normal cardiac anatomy. Normal renal anatomy. Normal spine — no additional vertebral defects. Normal limb anatomy. Amniotic fluid index elevated (AFI 28cm — moderate polyhydramnios). Normal placental morphology.
prompts.reasoning: Detailed fetal medicine ultrasound confirming absent calvarium and absent cerebral hemispheres with exposed area cerebrovasculosa (vascular remnant tissue where the brain should be) is diagnostic of anencephaly. Normal cardiac, renal, and spinal anatomy confirms isolated anencephaly without additional structural anomalies — important for genetic counselling. Polyhydramnios results from absent fetal swallowing centre. Exencephaly (earlier stage) cannot be distinguished from anencephaly at 20 weeks — both lead to the same prognosis.

prompts.label: Genetic and Biochemical Testing
prompts.text: Maternal serum AFP: markedly elevated (6.8 MoM — multiples of median; normal <2.5 MoM at 20 weeks). Amniotic fluid AFP: markedly elevated (open NTD marker). Acetylcholinesterase in amniotic fluid: positive (confirms open NTD — CSF leaks into amniotic fluid). Chromosomal microarray of fetal DNA (amniocentesis): normal female karyotype (46,XX). No chromosomal aneuploidy. Genetic counselling provided: isolated anencephaly (non-syndromic), no chromosomal abnormality.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated maternal serum AFP and amniotic fluid AFP are the classic biochemical markers of open NTDs — the elevated AFP reflects leakage of fetal proteins through the exposed neural tissue into the amniotic fluid and thence into maternal blood. Positive amniotic fluid acetylcholinesterase is specific for open NTDs. Normal chromosomal microarray confirms isolated non-chromosomal anencephaly. The recurrence risk for the next pregnancy is 3-5% — high-dose folic acid (5mg/day) from pre-conception is recommended.

prompts.label: Prognosis and Management
prompts.text: Parents counselled: anencephaly is uniformly lethal — most affected infants are stillborn or die within hours to days of birth due to absent cerebral hemispheres. Brainstem-mediated reflexes (heartbeat, breathing) may be temporarily preserved. Options discussed: termination of pregnancy (accepted at any gestation for lethal fetal anomaly in UK law) or expectant management with palliative birth plan. Parents opted for induction of labour. Infant born alive, died at 6 hours of age. Histopathology confirmed anencephaly.
prompts.reasoning: Anencephaly is a uniformly fatal NTD — there is no treatment. The cerebral cortex, which is necessary for conscious existence, is absent. Brainstem function (cardiac and respiratory centres) may be temporarily preserved, allowing brief survival. UK law (Abortion Act 1967) permits termination at any gestation for lethal fetal abnormality incompatible with survival. Prevention through periconceptional folic acid supplementation is the only effective intervention — food fortification programmes in many countries have reduced NTD rates by 50-70%.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of intermittent severe occipital and neck pain that is consistently worsened by coughing, sneezing, and straining.
prompts.reasoning: Occipital headache worsened by Valsalva manoeuvres (coughing, sneezing) is a characteristic pattern of pathology at the craniocervical junction or involving the posterior fossa. Arnold-Chiari malformation, posterior fossa mass, raised ICP from any cause, and basilar invagination all need consideration. Cough-exacerbated pain is the key feature pointing to pressure-dependent pathology at the foramen magnum.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She also reports intermittent episodes of loss of balance over the same period. The headache radiates from the back of the head to the top, lasts seconds to minutes, and occurs reliably with every Valsalva manoeuvre. She has noticed intermittent numbness and tingling in both hands — worse after looking up or turning her head — and progressive leg stiffness when walking on uneven ground.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral hand paraesthesia worsened by neck extension, combined with leg stiffness, suggests cervical cord involvement. This pattern could be Chiari I malformation with syringomyelia (cervical cord cavitation from CSF flow obstruction), cervical spondylotic myelopathy, or a posterior fossa lesion. Cough-exacerbated craniocervical junction pain with myelopathic signs is the key combination pointing to a craniocervical junction structural abnormality.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological diagnosis. No prior head or neck trauma. No prior spinal surgery. No connective tissue disorder (no EDS features — no joint hypermobility, no skin laxity). No family history of Chiari malformation or syringomyelia. Non-smoker. Works as a physiotherapist. Headaches have been present since teenage years but have progressively worsened. No prior MRI.
prompts.reasoning: The long history of occipital headaches since teenage years suggests a congenital structural abnormality (Chiari I is a congenital hindbrain abnormality, often becoming symptomatic in adulthood when compensatory mechanisms fail). No EDS/connective tissue disorder (Chiari I has an association with craniocervical instability in EDS). Progressive myelopathy without trauma suggests intrinsic cord pathology (syringomyelia from CSF flow obstruction at the foramen magnum) or an extrinsic compressive lesion.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral reduced pinprick sensation in a 'cape distribution' — across both shoulders and upper arms (C4-T2 dermatomes) bilaterally with preserved sensation below. Bilateral hand intrinsic muscle weakness (4/5). Bilateral lower limb spasticity. Brisk knee and ankle jerks bilaterally. Bilateral upgoing plantars. Mild truncal ataxia. Downbeat nystagmus on lateral gaze. Normal cranial nerve examination. BP normal. No papilloedema.
prompts.reasoning: The 'cape distribution' sensory loss (bilateral suspended sensory level affecting the shoulders and upper arms, sparing lower limbs and face) is the pathognomonic pattern of syringomyelia — caused by a cavity within the cervical cord disrupting the crossing spinothalamic fibres at the central grey matter. This creates a dissociated sensory loss (pain/temperature impaired, vibration/proprioception preserved early). Downbeat nystagmus is characteristic of lesions at the craniocervical junction — the hallmark of Chiari I malformation affecting the lower brainstem. UMN signs in the legs confirm cord compression below.

prompts.label: Bloods and CT Head
prompts.text: FBC, metabolic panel, B12, copper: all normal — metabolic causes excluded. CT head: low cerebellar tonsils visible extending below the foramen magnum. No posterior fossa mass. No hydrocephalus. Normal skull base. MRI ordered urgently for better characterisation of the craniocervical junction.
prompts.reasoning: CT head showing low-lying cerebellar tonsils at the foramen magnum is consistent with Chiari I malformation — though CT is not the ideal modality. No posterior fossa mass excludes tumour as the cause. Normal skull base makes basilar invagination less likely. MRI is the gold standard for measuring tonsillar descent, assessing CSF flow obstruction, and identifying syringomyelia in the spinal cord.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Spine
prompts.text: MRI brain (sagittal T1): cerebellar tonsils herniated 12mm below the foramen magnum (Chiari I: ≥5mm descent). Crowded foramen magnum. CSF flow study (phase-contrast MRI): absent normal CSF pulsatile flow at the foramen magnum — CSF outflow obstruction confirmed. MRI cervical and thoracic spine: central syrinx (fluid-filled cavity) extending from C3 to T4 within the spinal cord. Syrinx width 7mm (abnormal). Diagnosis: Chiari I malformation with secondary cervical-thoracic syringomyelia. Surgical treatment: suboccipital craniectomy + C1 laminectomy + duraplasty (posterior fossa decompression). At 6 months: syrinx reduced, cape distribution sensory loss improved.
prompts.reasoning: Cerebellar tonsil herniation ≥5mm below the foramen magnum defines Chiari I malformation — the 12mm descent in this patient is severe. Absent CSF pulsatile flow confirms functional obstruction at the foramen magnum, which is the mechanism of syrinx formation: impaired CSF outflow causes pressure waves to dissect into the central canal of the spinal cord, forming a syrinx. The syrinx explains the cape distribution sensory loss (central cord syndrome). Posterior fossa decompression is the standard surgical treatment — it re-establishes CSF flow, allowing the syrinx to gradually collapse.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 4-month-old infant is referred by the community paediatrician for progressive increase in head circumference that has crossed two centile lines since the 6-week check.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive macrocephaly (head circumference crossing centile lines) in early infancy requires urgent evaluation to distinguish benign causes from pathological expansion of the cranial vault. Hydrocephalus (communicating or obstructive), posterior fossa malformations (Dandy-Walker, Arnold-Chiari), and arachnoid cysts all cause progressive macrocephaly. Subdural collections and benign external hydrocephalus are also important to exclude. Urgent cranial imaging is required.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The parents report the infant has not achieved social smile and has poor head control for age; they have also noticed the back of the head appears unusually prominent compared to siblings at the same age.
prompts.reasoning: Developmental delay (absent social smile at 4 months, poor head control) combined with prominent occiput localises the pathology to the posterior fossa or indicates significant raised intracranial pressure affecting development. A prominent occiput suggests expansion of the posterior fossa compartment — seen in Dandy-Walker malformation, large posterior fossa arachnoid cysts, and occasionally Arnold-Chiari type II. Benign external hydrocephalus and communicating hydrocephalus without posterior fossa expansion become less likely. At least three diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: The 20-week anomaly scan identified an abnormality in the posterior fossa that the parents were told 'needed monitoring'; karyotype from amniocentesis was normal (46,XX); no teratogen exposure, no maternal diabetes, no family history of neural tube defects.
prompts.reasoning: Antenatal detection of a posterior fossa abnormality narrows the differential substantially. Dandy-Walker malformation, mega cisterna magna, and posterior fossa arachnoid cysts are all detectable antenatally. Normal karyotype reduces (but does not exclude) association with trisomy 18 or 13. Arnold-Chiari type II is characteristically associated with myelomeningocele — its antenatal detection would typically include a spinal lesion. Normal karyotype and absent teratogen exposure do not exclude sporadic Dandy-Walker malformation, which can occur with normal chromosomes.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Head circumference 46 cm (>99th centile); tense, bulging anterior fontanelle; prominent occiput; bilateral sunset sign (downward conjugate gaze deviation with visible sclera above iris); dilated scalp veins; truncal hypotonia with absent head control.
accepted: Posterior Fossa Arachnoid Cyst with Hydrocephalus
prompts.reasoning: The sunset sign (Parinaud-equivalent in infants) indicates obstructive hydrocephalus causing midbrain tectal compression, elevating the pressure on the dorsal midbrain. Tense fontanelle confirms raised ICP. Truncal hypotonia with absent head control points to cerebellar vermis dysfunction — the vermis coordinates axial (truncal) movements. The combination of posterior fossa expansion, raised ICP, and truncal ataxia/hypotonia narrows the differential to posterior fossa lesions causing obstructive hydrocephalus. Mega cisterna magna (normal vermis, no hydrocephalus) is excluded by the clinical picture.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: markedly enlarged posterior fossa with upward displacement of the tentorium; a large midline cystic structure is continuous with an enlarged fourth ventricle; the cerebellar hemispheres are small and splayed laterally; obstructive hydrocephalus with dilated lateral and third ventricles.
prompts.reasoning: The MRI triad for Dandy-Walker malformation is: (1) enlarged posterior fossa with elevated tentorium, (2) large cyst communicating with/representing the fourth ventricle, and (3) hypoplastic/absent cerebellar vermis. The cyst communicating directly with the fourth ventricle distinguishes Dandy-Walker malformation from mega cisterna magna (which is posterior to an anatomically normal vermis and fourth ventricle) and from arachnoid cysts (which compress but do not communicate with the fourth ventricle). Obstructive hydrocephalus results from failed CSF egress through the obstructed fourth ventricular outlet foramina.

prompts.label: Confirmatory Imaging & Genetic Testing
prompts.text: Dedicated posterior fossa MRI sequences confirm near-complete agenesis of the cerebellar vermis with the remnant rotated superiorly; partial agenesis of the corpus callosum (absent splenium); chromosomal microarray identifies a 3q24 microdeletion encompassing ZIC1 and ZIC4.
prompts.reasoning: The diagnostic triad is complete: absent cerebellar vermis + fourth ventricle cyst + enlarged posterior fossa. Mega cisterna magna is excluded by confirmation of vermis agenesis — mega cisterna magna has an anatomically normal vermis. Dandy-Walker malformation is sporadic in most cases but associations include trisomy 18/13/21 and mutations in ZIC1/ZIC4 (3q24), which encode transcription factors essential for cerebellar vermis morphogenesis. Associated cortical malformations (corpus callosum agenesis, heterotopias) occur in approximately 50% and worsen developmental prognosis. Management: ventriculoperitoneal shunting for hydrocephalus; posterior fossa cyst fenestration is an alternative.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A newborn is referred to the neonatal neurology team following an antenatal MRI at 32 weeks that identified an abnormality of cerebral development.
prompts.reasoning: An antenatal MRI identifying a cerebral developmental abnormality in a newborn encompasses a broad differential of neuronal migration disorders, midline malformations, and destructive lesions. Holoprosencephaly (failure of forebrain cleavage), agenesis of corpus callosum, septo-optic dysplasia, lissencephaly (migration failure), schizencephaly (cortical clefts), and hydranencephaly (destructive) must all be considered before imaging defines the anatomy.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The infant is having repetitive tonic posturing episodes and is unable to feed; the antenatal scan also noted a concern about midline facial structures, and the pregnancy was complicated by poorly controlled maternal type 1 diabetes mellitus.
prompts.reasoning: Neonatal seizures plus antenatal cerebral midline abnormality and concurrent midline facial concerns narrow the differential toward disorders of midline brain development. Maternal diabetes is a recognised risk factor for holoprosencephaly (and septo-optic dysplasia) due to disruption of the prechordal plate during early embryogenesis. Schizencephaly is less commonly associated with these features. At least five clinically plausible diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: The mother had HbA1c of 82 mmol/mol in the first trimester; NIPT was not performed; no family history of chromosomal abnormalities; no teratogen use other than metformin started in the second trimester.
prompts.reasoning: Poorly controlled diabetes in the first trimester (when forebrain cleavage occurs at gestational weeks 4–8) is the most important environmental risk factor for holoprosencephaly. Chromosomal causes (trisomy 13, trisomy 18) remain possible without NIPT data. Septo-optic dysplasia shares some features (midline) and has a diabetes association. Lissencephaly is less strongly diabetes-associated. The absence of NIPT means chromosomal diagnoses cannot yet be excluded.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: The infant is microcephalic; hypotelorism is present; there is a single central maxillary incisor tooth bud palpable; the infant has profound hypotonia, absent suck reflex, and no response to visual stimuli; tonic seizures are observed.
prompts.reasoning: Midline facial malformations (hypotelorism, single central incisor) reflect the common embryological origin of the prechordal plate in directing both facial midline development and forebrain separation — these features are highly associated with holoprosencephaly but are not specific enough at this stage to exclude other severe cerebral malformations. Profound hypotonia, absent visual response, and neonatal seizures indicate severe cortical dysfunction consistent with holoprosencephaly, lissencephaly, or hydranencephaly. Agenesis of corpus callosum alone would not produce this degree of neonatal depression.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain demonstrates complete failure of the cerebral hemispheres to divide, forming a single monoventricle; there is no interhemispheric fissure; the thalami are fused in the midline; no identifiable corpus callosum or olfactory bulbs.
prompts.reasoning: The alobar variant of holoprosencephaly is defined by complete failure of forebrain (prosencephalon) division: a single monoventricle, fused thalami, absent interhemispheric fissure, absent corpus callosum, and absent olfactory bulbs. Lissencephaly shows a smooth cortex but the hemispheres are separated. Hydranencephaly shows absent hemispheres replaced by fluid sacs but with preserved posterior fossa structures and a normal skull shape. The MRI is diagnostic of alobar holoprosencephaly.

prompts.label: Chromosomal Analysis & Outcome
prompts.text: Chromosomal microarray confirms trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome); FISH analysis confirms non-disjunction pattern; cardiology review identifies a ventricular septal defect; palliative care plan established following family meeting.
prompts.reasoning: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) is the most important chromosomal cause of holoprosencephaly — the HPE locus on chromosome 13 (ZIC2, HPE1, HPE4) is disrupted by trisomy. Trisomy 13 is associated with the full syndrome: HPE, midline facial defects (cyclopia, proboscis, cleft lip/palate, single central incisor), polydactyly, cardiac defects, and cutis aplasia. Prognosis for alobar HPE with trisomy 13 is universally fatal — median survival is days to weeks. Septo-optic dysplasia and ACC have relatively better prognoses, distinguishing them. The diagnosis is holoprosencephaly (alobar type) secondary to trisomy 13.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 6-month-old infant is referred for severe global developmental delay and increasingly frequent seizures that began at 3 months of age.
prompts.reasoning: Early-onset refractory epilepsy with global developmental delay in infancy is a broad presentation requiring systematic evaluation. Structural causes (lissencephaly, tuberous sclerosis, Aicardi syndrome), genetic epilepsy syndromes (Dravet), metabolic disorders, and congenital infections (CMV) all cause this picture. The age of seizure onset (3 months) and severity of developmental delay will help narrow the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The seizures are multiple types — infantile spasms in clusters on waking, tonic seizures, and absence-like episodes — and are refractory to two antiepileptic drugs; no skin lesions have been noticed by the parents.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple seizure types with infantile spasms that are drug-refractory indicate a severe underlying structural or genetic epilepsy. Absence of skin lesions makes tuberous sclerosis less likely (hypopigmented macules are typically present), but cannot be excluded without Wood's lamp examination. Aicardi syndrome occurs only in females (XX). Metabolic encephalopathy typically has multisystem features and progressive clinical deterioration rather than a relatively stable developmental plateau.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: The mother reports markedly reduced fetal movements from 20 weeks gestation; birth was at 39 weeks with normal birth weight; neonatal period was unremarkable; no family history of epilepsy or neurological disease; no consanguinity.
prompts.reasoning: Reduced fetal movements from 20 weeks onwards suggests significantly impaired cortical or neuromuscular function in utero — consistent with a severe cortical malformation such as lissencephaly (where cortical development failure reduces fetal movement from mid-gestation). Normal birth weight and neonatal period make metabolic causes less likely (metabolic encephalopathies often cause neonatal crisis). No family history or consanguinity does not exclude de novo mutations causing lissencephaly (most cases are sporadic de novo).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Occipitofrontal circumference below the 3rd centile (microcephaly); bitemporal narrowing; generalised hypotonia; brisk deep tendon reflexes bilaterally despite hypotonia; bilateral extensor plantar responses; no purposeful hand movements; no social smile or visual tracking.
prompts.reasoning: Microcephaly with the combination of hypotonia (LMN-like) AND brisk reflexes with extensor plantars (UMN signs) reflects simultaneous cortical and corticospinal tract dysfunction — a pattern strongly suggesting a severe cortical malformation rather than a metabolic or genetic epilepsy syndrome without structural change. Bitemporal narrowing is a morphological feature associated with lissencephaly. Pachygyria (simplified gyral pattern) represents the less severe end of the same spectrum as lissencephaly (agyria). Both remain in the differential at this stage.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain reveals a smooth cerebral cortex with near-complete absence of sulci and gyri (agyria); the cortex is abnormally thick (measured at 12 mm, normal 2–4 mm); the brain has an hourglass appearance on axial sections; the white matter volume is markedly reduced.
prompts.reasoning: Classic MRI features of lissencephaly: smooth brain surface (agyria, or pachygyria in milder forms), thickened cortex (>10 mm due to failure of normal six-layer lamination — neurons arrested in migration form only four layers), hourglass or figure-of-eight configuration on axial slices due to shallow Sylvian fissures, and reduced white matter. Pachygyria shows some simplified, broad gyri rather than complete absence — lissencephaly is the severe end of the same spectrum. The structural diagnosis is confirmed. Genetic testing will determine the specific mutation.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Classification
prompts.text: Chromosomal microarray and targeted gene panel confirm a de novo deletion of chromosome 17p13.3 encompassing the LIS1 (PAFAH1B1) gene; diagnosis: classic lissencephaly type I, Miller-Dieker syndrome variant; EEG shows hypsarrhythmia pattern.
prompts.reasoning: Lissencephaly type I (classic) results most commonly from mutations in LIS1 (17p13.3, encodes platelet-activating factor acetylhydrolase subunit — regulates dynein-mediated neuronal migration along radial glia) or DCX (doublecortin, X-linked — males more severely affected). Miller-Dieker syndrome = 17p13.3 deletion including LIS1 + YWHAE + dysmorphic features. The pathology: neurons fail to migrate from the periventricular zone to the cortical plate → only four cortical layers form instead of six → agyria/pachygyria spectrum. EEG hypsarrhythmia (chaotic, high-amplitude) is characteristic of infantile spasms. Prognosis: severe — most patients do not reach independence; seizures are lifelong and drug-refractory.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric clinic because her class teacher raised concerns about learning difficulties, and the school nurse noted several unusual skin markings at a routine health check.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple pigmented skin lesions in a child with learning difficulties form the basis of a broad differential including several neurocutaneous and genetic syndromes. NF1 is the most common neurocutaneous syndrome (1:3000), but Legius syndrome (SPRED1 mutation), McCune-Albright, and LEOPARD/Noonan syndromes with multiple lentigines also cause pigmented lesions and developmental difficulties. The number, distribution, and morphology of skin lesions will be essential for discrimination.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The skin markings have been present since infancy and have slowly increased in number over the years; she has no seizures, no headaches, and no visual symptoms, but she has struggled specifically with reading, mathematics, and attention.
prompts.reasoning: Congenital skin lesions increasing in number (rather than appearing acutely) favour a constitutional genetic syndrome over an acquired condition. Specific learning difficulties (reading, maths, attention) without global delay or seizures are characteristic of NF1-associated neurocognitive profile (affects approximately 60% of NF1 patients). McCune-Albright typically presents with precocious puberty and bone lesions. LEOPARD syndrome has cardiac features. Legius syndrome closely mimics NF1's skin findings but lacks neurofibromas and CNS manifestations — the most important differential at this stage.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Her father has similar skin pigmentation on his trunk and underwent an operation to remove a soft tissue lump from his forearm in his early 30s; no family history of malignancy; the girl's height is on the 25th centile and she has a mildly prominent forehead.
prompts.reasoning: Autosomal dominant transmission (affected father) with similar skin lesions and a soft tissue tumour (likely neurofibroma) in the father is strongly suggestive of NF1 or Legius syndrome — both are autosomal dominant. Legius syndrome does NOT cause neurofibromas — the father's excised soft tissue lump on the arm favours NF1 over Legius syndrome, but pathological confirmation of the father's lesion is not available. Familial café-au-lait macules (without other NF1 features) remain in the differential. NF1 is caused by mutations in the NF1 gene on chromosome 17q11.2 (a tumour suppressor encoding neurofibromin).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Skin examination reveals 9 uniformly hyperpigmented flat macules on the trunk and proximal limbs, the largest measuring 2.1 cm; axillary skin shows a cluster of small freckle-like pigmented spots bilaterally; no palpable subcutaneous nodules; no scoliosis; normal blood pressure.
prompts.reasoning: NF1 diagnosis requires ≥2 of 7 NIH criteria: (1) ≥6 café-au-lait macules >5 mm (prepubertal) or >15 mm (postpubertal), (2) ≥2 neurofibromas or 1 plexiform neurofibroma, (3) axillary/inguinal freckling (Crowe sign), (4) optic glioma, (5) ≥2 Lisch nodules, (6) distinctive osseous lesion, (7) first-degree relative with NF1. This child has ≥6 CAL macules (criterion 1) AND axillary freckling (criterion 3) — meeting 2 criteria, strongly suggesting NF1. Legius syndrome can produce CAL macules and axillary freckling but does NOT cause Lisch nodules or neurofibromas. Ophthalmological assessment is now critical for discrimination.

prompts.label: Ophthalmology & Neuroimaging
prompts.text: Slit-lamp examination identifies multiple small, well-defined pigmented hamartomas on the anterior surface of the iris bilaterally; MRI brain (with gadolinium) shows bilateral T2 hyperintensities in the basal ganglia and cerebellar white matter (unenhancing focal areas of signal intensity).
prompts.reasoning: Iris hamartomas (Lisch nodules) are pathognomonic for NF1 — they do not occur in Legius syndrome or other café-au-lait syndromes. Their presence satisfies a third NF1 criterion, securing the clinical diagnosis (≥2 criteria required; this patient now meets 3). Focal areas of signal intensity (FASI) on MRI T2/FLAIR — unenhancing T2-bright areas in basal ganglia, cerebellum, brainstem — are found in 60–70% of NF1 patients and likely represent abnormal myelination or intramyelinic oedema; they are not tumours and typically resolve in adulthood. No optic glioma is identified on this MRI.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Surveillance Plan
prompts.text: Genetic testing confirms a pathogenic frameshift mutation in the NF1 gene (chromosome 17q11.2), consistent with autosomal dominant NF1; annual ophthalmology surveillance for optic glioma commenced; neuropsychological assessment confirms NF1-associated specific learning disability; family genetic counselling arranged.
prompts.reasoning: NF1 gene encodes neurofibromin — a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that downregulates RAS signalling. Loss of neurofibromin leads to constitutive RAS/MAPK pathway activation, driving cell proliferation. NF1 is the most common single-gene disorder predisposing to tumours (neurofibromas, optic gliomas, malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumours). Penetrance is 100% but expressivity is highly variable. Annual surveillance: ophthalmology (optic glioma — occurs in 15%), blood pressure (renal artery stenosis/phaeochromocytoma), neuropsychological support. Plexiform neurofibromas (congenital, diffuse) have a 10–15% malignant transformation risk to MPNST — the most feared complication.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old man presents to his GP with a 6-month history of progressive hearing loss affecting primarily his right ear.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss in a young adult is the commonest presenting symptom of vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma), but the differential is broad. Ménière's disease (endolymphatic hydrops) causes fluctuating hearing loss with episodic vertigo. Autoimmune inner ear disease causes rapidly progressive bilateral SNHL. Otosclerosis causes predominantly conductive hearing loss in young adults. Acoustic trauma and cholesteatoma are important to exclude. NF2 (bilateral vestibular schwannomas) must be considered in any young patient with a vestibular schwannoma.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He also describes constant tinnitus in the right ear and intermittent imbalance when walking in the dark; he denies any ear discharge, ear pain, or prior noise exposure, and has not taken any ototoxic medications.
prompts.reasoning: Tinnitus with unilateral progressive SNHL and vestibular disturbance (imbalance in dark — vestibulo-ocular reflex impaired, relies on vision) is the triad of vestibular schwannoma. Ménière's disease is distinguished by episodic attacks of vertigo lasting 20 minutes to hours with fluctuating hearing and aural fullness. The absence of ear discharge or pain reduces cholesteatoma likelihood. Otosclerosis causes predominantly conductive loss. Autoimmune IEDL is typically bilateral and responds to steroids. The age (22 years) for a vestibular schwannoma raises the probability of NF2 compared to a sporadic tumour (sporadic schwannomas typically present in the 40s–50s).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: His mother underwent surgery for a 'brain tumour involving the hearing nerve' in her early 40s and subsequently became deaf; he noticed a firm, non-tender subcutaneous lump on the left side of his neck approximately 2 years ago that has not changed.
prompts.reasoning: A first-degree relative (mother) with a vestibular schwannoma fulfils the Manchester NF2 diagnostic criteria: first-degree relative with NF2 plus ipsilateral vestibular schwannoma, OR first-degree relative with NF2 plus any two NF2-associated tumours (meningioma, schwannoma, ependymoma, cataract). The subcutaneous neck lump may represent a peripheral schwannoma — a characteristic NF2-associated tumour. NF2 is autosomal dominant (50% of offspring affected); the mother's deafness following surgery for a CPA lesion strongly suggests she has NF2. Sporadic bilateral disease (rare) remains possible but becomes far less likely.

prompts.label: Physical Examination & Audiometry
prompts.text: Pure-tone audiometry: right-sided high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss; left ear shows mild high-frequency SNHL also present; speech discrimination score disproportionately impaired on the right; firm 1.5 cm subcutaneous nodule palpable on the left neck; neurological examination otherwise normal; no café-au-lait spots, no axillary freckling.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral SNHL (right > left) with disproportionately impaired speech discrimination (retrocochlear pattern, as opposed to cochlear hearing loss where speech discrimination is proportionally preserved) strongly suggests bilateral retrocochlear lesions — the hallmark of NF2. Speech discrimination impairment out of proportion to pure-tone threshold loss indicates cochlear nerve compression. Absence of café-au-lait spots and axillary freckling distinguishes NF2 from NF1 (Lisch nodules and neurofibromas characterise NF1). Autoimmune IEDL remains — responds rapidly to steroids, unlike schwannoma.

prompts.label: MRI Brain with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: a 2.3 cm intensely enhancing mass in the right cerebellopontine angle extending into and widening the right internal auditory canal; a separate 0.8 cm enhancing nodule is identified in the left CPA; both lesions have an 'ice cream cone' appearance extending through the porus acusticus.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral enhancing CPA masses extending into both internal auditory canals is the defining imaging feature of NF2. The 'ice cream cone' sign (mass in CPA with tail extending into IAC) is characteristic of vestibular schwannoma, distinguishing it from meningioma (which does not extend into the IAC and has a broad dural base). Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are the pathognomonic criterion for NF2 (NIH diagnostic criteria): bilateral CPA tumours in a patient <30 years of age = NF2 until proven otherwise. Sporadic bilateral schwannomas are exceedingly rare. Autoimmune IEDL has no mass lesion on MRI.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Systemic Assessment
prompts.text: Germline genetic testing confirms a pathogenic truncating mutation in the NF2 gene (chromosome 22q12.2, encoding Merlin protein); spinal MRI reveals two additional intradural extramedullary enhancing lesions at C3 and T6 (spinal schwannomas/meningiomas); ophthalmology identifies posterior subcapsular cataracts bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: NF2 gene on chromosome 22q12.2 encodes Merlin (moesin-ezrin-radixin-like protein) — a tumour suppressor that regulates cell proliferation and contact inhibition. Loss of Merlin leads to dysregulated Schwann cell growth. NF2-associated tumours: bilateral vestibular schwannomas (hallmark), other schwannomas (spinal, cutaneous), meningiomas (often multiple), spinal ependymomas, and posterior subcapsular cataracts. Unlike NF1, NF2 has relatively few café-au-lait macules (if any) and no Lisch nodules. Management options for vestibular schwannomas: surgical resection, stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), or observation. Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) may reduce tumour growth in NF2. Hearing rehabilitation planning essential.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 9-month-old infant is brought to the paediatric emergency department following two episodes of brief clusters of sudden, repetitive jerking movements this morning.
prompts.reasoning: Cluster seizures in a 9-month-old requiring emergency assessment encompasses a broad differential. Brief repetitive jerking movements in clusters in infancy may represent infantile spasms — a serious epilepsy syndrome associated with structural, metabolic, or genetic causes. Febrile seizures are typically isolated tonic-clonic events, not clusters. Metabolic causes (hypocalcaemia, pyridoxine deficiency) and structural causes (tuberous sclerosis, cortical malformations) must all be considered urgently.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The episodes have been occurring in clusters of 10–20 brief spasms each morning for approximately 3 weeks, and the parents have noticed the infant's developmental progress has plateaued — previously reaching for objects, now no longer doing so.
prompts.reasoning: Clusters of sudden axial spasms on waking, associated with developmental regression over 3 weeks, describes infantile spasms (West syndrome). West syndrome is characterised by the triad: infantile spasms, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, and developmental regression. It is a syndrome requiring investigation for an underlying cause — structural (tuberous sclerosis most common, cortical malformations), chromosomal (trisomy 21, Down syndrome), and metabolic causes. Dravet syndrome typically presents with febrile hemiclonic seizures in the first year. No skin findings have been mentioned yet, so tuberous sclerosis cannot be confirmed or excluded.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: The infant was developmentally normal until 6 months; the neonatal period was unremarkable; no family history of epilepsy or skin disease; the parents are not consanguineous; no antenatal complications or teratogen exposure.
prompts.reasoning: Normal development until 6 months with subsequent regression and onset of infantile spasms suggests a condition that either manifests at this developmental stage (tuberous sclerosis — cortical tubers cause hyperexcitability as myelination progresses) or a process that has become symptomatic over time. The absence of family history does not exclude tuberous sclerosis — approximately two-thirds of TSC cases are de novo mutations. No consanguinity and unremarkable neonatal period reduce the probability of autosomal recessive metabolic disorders but do not exclude them.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Wood's lamp examination of the skin: two oval hypopigmented macules (3 cm and 2 cm) on the trunk; fundoscopy: an elevated pale lesion adjacent to the optic disc on the right (retinal lesion); the infant is hypotonic with reduced social interaction and absent purposeful grasp; no café-au-lait spots.
prompts.reasoning: Hypopigmented skin macules detected on Wood's lamp are a key early feature of tuberous sclerosis — they are present at birth in over 90% of TSC patients but are subtle without UV illumination. A retinal hamartoma (pale elevated retinal lesion near the optic disc) is a major diagnostic feature of TSC, distinct from the retinal manifestations of Incontinentia Pigmenti or Hypomelanosis of Ito. Hypomelanosis of Ito causes irregular hypopigmented whorls (following Blaschko's lines) without retinal hamartomas. Incontinentia Pigmenti occurs almost exclusively in females. The combination of hypopigmented macules and retinal hamartoma in a male infant with infantile spasms makes tuberous sclerosis the leading diagnosis.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & EEG
prompts.text: EEG: hypsarrhythmia pattern (high-amplitude chaotic multifocal spikes and slow waves); MRI brain: multiple T2-hyperintense cortical/subcortical tubers in bilateral hemispheres; several calcified subependymal nodules lining the lateral ventricles; no hydrocephalus.
prompts.reasoning: Hypsarrhythmia (chaotic, high-amplitude, asynchronous pattern) confirms West syndrome. The combination of cortical tubers and subependymal nodules on MRI is diagnostic of tuberous sclerosis. Cortical tubers are focal dysplastic regions of disorganised neurons and giant cells — they form the epileptogenic foci (seizure focus correlates with tuber burden). Subependymal nodules are calcified hamartomatous lesions lining the ventricles; if they grow at the foramen of Monro, they can transform to subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs) causing obstructive hydrocephalus — a key surveillance target. Hypomelanosis of Ito and Incontinentia Pigmenti do not produce these MRI findings.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing & Multisystem Assessment
prompts.text: Genetic testing identifies a pathogenic variant in TSC2 (chromosome 16p13.3); echocardiography reveals two echogenic cardiac rhabdomyomas; renal ultrasound: multiple small bilateral echogenic lesions (angiomyolipomas confirmed); ophthalmology confirms two retinal astrocytic hamartomas.
prompts.reasoning: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is caused by mutations in TSC1 (hamartin, chromosome 9q34) or TSC2 (tuberin, chromosome 16p13.3) — both encode proteins that form the hamartin-tuberin complex, a tumour suppressor that inhibits mTORC1. Loss of function → constitutive mTOR activation → hamartoma formation in brain, skin, heart, kidney, and lung. Cardiac rhabdomyomas (present in 50–60% of neonates with TSC) often regress spontaneously. Renal angiomyolipomas (fat, smooth muscle, blood vessels) cause haematuria and haemorrhage risk. Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) is used for SEGAs, renal AML, and pulmonary lymphangioleiomyomatosis. TSC2 mutations cause more severe phenotype than TSC1.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive unsteadiness, persistent headache, and nausea that is worst in the mornings.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive cerebellar syndrome with morning headache and nausea over 3 months suggests a posterior fossa space-occupying lesion causing obstructive hydrocephalus, or a demyelinating lesion. Morning-dominant headache is a classic feature of raised ICP (due to accumulation of CO2 during recumbent sleep). In a 28-year-old, posterior fossa tumours (medulloblastoma — peaks in childhood/young adults, cerebellar astrocytoma, haemangioblastoma, ependymoma) must be prioritised. MS can cause cerebellar syndromes but less commonly causes raised ICP.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache is worse on leaning forward and has been associated with two episodes of vomiting; over the past 3 weeks he has developed slurred speech and a sensation of drunkenness when walking; no sensory symptoms, no limb weakness.
prompts.reasoning: Worsening headache on Valsalva-equivalent manoeuvres (leaning forward) confirms raised ICP, likely from a posterior fossa lesion causing obstructive hydrocephalus at the fourth ventricle. Dysarthria and gait ataxia without sensory or motor deficits localise to the cerebellar hemispheres or vermis. MS with posterior fossa demyelination becomes less likely given the mass effect features. The age of presentation and progressive course favour a primary posterior fossa tumour — at 28, medulloblastoma (can occur in adults), haemangioblastoma, and pilocytic astrocytoma are realistic.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: His father was diagnosed with renal cell carcinoma at age 41 and died at age 47; a paternal uncle had an eye operation for a 'vascular problem' in his early 30s; no personal history of malignancy; non-smoker; blood pressure documented as elevated at his last GP visit.
prompts.reasoning: A first-degree relative (father) with early-onset renal cell carcinoma and a second relative with an ocular vascular lesion strongly raises the probability of a hereditary tumour syndrome. Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome is characterised by cerebellar haemangioblastoma, retinal haemangioblastoma (the uncle's 'eye operation'), renal cell carcinoma (RCC), phaeochromocytoma, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumours — all occurring at unusually young ages due to germline VHL mutation. The combination of a posterior fossa cystic tumour in a young adult with family history of early RCC and ocular vascular lesion makes VHL the leading syndromic diagnosis. Isolated metastatic RCC (from the father) would not explain the uncle's eye lesion.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Blood pressure 158/96 mmHg; right-sided cerebellar signs: past-pointing, dysdiadochokinesia, intention tremor; gait ataxia; bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy; no focal limb weakness; no café-au-lait spots or skin lesions; no palpable abdominal masses.
prompts.reasoning: Right-sided cerebellar hemisphere signs with papilloedema confirm a right cerebellar mass with raised ICP. Hypertension may reflect phaeochromocytoma (a VHL-associated tumour causing catecholamine excess) or secondary hypertension from renal involvement. No cutaneous stigmata of NF1 (café-au-lait, axillary freckling) distinguish this from a NF1-related tumour. Medulloblastoma remains in the differential — it is the most common posterior fossa tumour in adults under 40, typically arising in the cerebellar vermis. MRI is urgently required.

prompts.label: MRI Brain with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain: a well-defined 3.2 cm cystic lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere with an intensely gadolinium-enhancing mural nodule; obstructive hydrocephalus at the fourth ventricle; spinal MRI reveals two small enhancing nodules at C5 and T9 (intradural, extramedullary).
prompts.reasoning: The cystic cerebellar lesion with intensely enhancing mural nodule is the characteristic MRI appearance of a haemangioblastoma — a benign, highly vascular WHO Grade I tumour of uncertain origin (possibly arising from hemangioblastic stem cells). The mural nodule is the true tumour; the cyst is secondary. Additional spinal intradural lesions make a sporadic single haemangioblastoma far less likely — multiple haemangioblastomas strongly indicate VHL syndrome. Medulloblastoma is typically a solid midline (vermian) tumour in adults, not a peripheral cystic lesion with mural nodule. The combination of cerebellar + spinal haemangioblastomas is the VHL hallmark.

prompts.label: Systemic Staging & Genetic Confirmation
