prompts.text: He has fair skin, blue eyes, and burns easily. His GP counted more than 80 melanocytic naevi at a check-up three years ago. His mother had melanoma diagnosed at age 60. He works outdoors and has had several episodes of sunburn in his lifetime.
prompts.reasoning: A phenotypically high-risk profile (fair skin, blue eyes, easy burning), more than 80 naevi, a first-degree family history of melanoma, and a history of intermittent intense sun exposure represent a substantial cumulative risk profile for malignant melanoma. Dysplastic naevus syndrome (atypical mole syndrome) with >100 atypical naevi and a family history of melanoma is a recognised risk category. Pigmented BCC is also driven by UV exposure. The family history specifically elevates melanoma risk in a patient who already has many naevi. At least three diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A 1.8 cm asymmetric pigmented lesion with a notched, irregular border and variegated colouration (tan, brown, and dark brown, with a central pale zone) is present on the upper back. No satellite lesions, no ulceration. No regional lymphadenopathy palpated.
prompts.reasoning: Asymmetry, irregular border, and variegated colour (multiple shades including a focal pale area suggesting possible regression) are the core clinical features of concern for melanoma. However, a severely dysplastic naevus can share these features. Pigmented BCC typically shows a more uniform dark area or pearlescent border without this degree of colour variegation; it is now less likely. Absence of lymphadenopathy is reassuring but does not exclude melanoma — micrometastatic disease would not be palpable. Dermoscopy is required to discriminate between melanoma and a dysplastic naevus.

prompts.label: Dermoscopy
prompts.text: Dermoscopy shows an atypical reticular pigment network that abruptly ends at the lesion periphery, irregular brown streaks radiating from the border, white scar-like areas centrally (regression structures), and a focal blue-white veil.
prompts.reasoning: Regression structures (white scar-like areas replacing pigment) and a blue-white veil (blue pigmentation over a white ground glass area, corresponding to melanin in the dermis overlying fibrosis) are dermoscopic features specific for melanoma. Irregular radial streaks at the periphery indicate radial growth. An atypical pigment network with abrupt peripheral ending is characteristic of melanoma rather than a dysplastic naevus (which shows a fading, irregular network). Together these features provide sufficient dermoscopic evidence to classify this as melanoma and proceed to excision biopsy.

prompts.label: Histopathological Confirmation
prompts.text: Excision biopsy shows atypical melanocytes with prominent nucleoli proliferating as single cells and nests at all levels of the epidermis (pagetoid spread), with invasion into the papillary dermis. Mitotic figures are identified in the dermal component. Breslow depth measured at 1.2 mm.
prompts.reasoning: Pagetoid spread — atypical melanocytes scattered throughout all epidermal layers, not just the basal layer — is a hallmark of melanoma and is not seen in dysplastic naevi. Dermal invasion with mitotic figures confirms invasive melanoma. The Breslow depth of 1.2 mm (>1 mm, T2 category) is the most important local prognostic factor for primary cutaneous melanoma, guiding surgical margin width and sentinel lymph node biopsy planning. The histological findings confirm malignant melanoma.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old woman develops a widespread rash four days after starting a new course of medication.
prompts.reasoning: A rash temporally associated with a new drug must first be classified as a cutaneous adverse drug reaction, and the type of CADR must then be identified. At four days, the latency is consistent with a re-exposure reaction or early sensitisation. Drug exanthem, AGEP, DHS, and urticaria are all possible CADRs. Viral exanthem is included as a non-drug alternative that must not be prematurely excluded. The discriminatory axis introduced is the temporal relationship to drug initiation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The rash began on the trunk and has spread to the arms and legs over 48 hours; it is mildly itchy but she has no fever, no swelling of the face or lips, no mucosal symptoms, and no joint pain.
prompts.reasoning: The centrifugal spread from the trunk over 48 hours is characteristic of a morbilliform drug exanthem or a viral exanthem. Absence of fever makes DHS/DRESS and AGEP less likely — both characteristically involve fever above 38°C. Absence of facial/lip swelling reduces the likelihood of urticaria with angioedema. Absence of mucosal symptoms reduces SJS/TEN probability. Urticaria is now less likely given the 48-hour spreading morbilliform pattern (urticaria typically shifts and resolves within 24 hours per lesion). AGEP remains in differential if pustules develop. At least four diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: She was started on amoxicillin-clavulanate four days ago for a dental infection. She has no documented prior drug allergies. She has had no fever, cough, sore throat, or any other illness preceding the rash. No recent travel.
prompts.reasoning: Beta-lactam antibiotics (particularly amoxicillin and amoxicillin-clavulanate) are the most common cause of maculopapular drug exanthem, establishing a direct temporal and pharmacological association. Absence of a viral prodrome (fever, cough, sore throat) makes viral exanthem less likely, though not impossible — viral exanthem can occasionally present without prodromal symptoms. Urticaria from beta-lactams is IgE-mediated and typically begins within minutes to hours of exposure, not at four days; this reduces urticaria's probability but it remains in the differential as delayed urticaria is possible. DHS/DRESS is now less likely — it requires a 2–8 week latency, not four days.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.1°C, HR 82 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg. Diffuse, symmetric erythematous macules and papules are present over the trunk, proximal arms, and thighs, sparing the face and palms. All lesions are blanchable on pressure. No pustules, no vesicles or bullae, no mucosal erosions. No lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: Diffuse, symmetric, blanchable erythematous macules and papules (morbilliform pattern) are the morphological hallmark of both maculopapular drug exanthem and viral exanthem — the pattern is essentially identical between the two, explaining why a detailed drug and illness history is critical. Absence of pustules excludes AGEP. Absence of bullae or mucosal erosions excludes SJS/TEN. Absence of lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly reduces DHS probability further. Urticaria is now excluded — morbilliform macules and papules are not wheals, and the lesions are not transient. Two diagnoses remain: drug exanthem and viral exanthem.

prompts.label: Blood Tests
prompts.text: Full blood count: mild eosinophilia at 0.65 × 10⁹/L (reference <0.5). No atypical lymphocytes on blood film. Liver function tests: normal. Renal function: normal.
prompts.reasoning: Mild eosinophilia is a feature of drug hypersensitivity reactions, supporting an immunological drug reaction over a viral exanthem. Absence of atypical lymphocytes makes EBV (a common cause of viral exanthem and also a cause of a rash with amoxicillin use) less likely, though EBV serology would confirm this. Normal liver and renal function exclude systemic organ involvement, effectively ruling out DHS/DRESS (which requires multi-organ involvement). The combination of eosinophilia, normal organ function, drug exposure at four days, and absence of viral prodrome confirms maculopapular drug exanthem.

prompts.label: Clinical Course and Confirmation
prompts.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is discontinued. The rash fades progressively and clears completely over seven days without specific treatment. No systemic organ involvement develops during follow-up. The temporal relationship — rash onset at day four of drug use, complete resolution within one week of cessation — confirms the diagnosis.
prompts.reasoning: Complete spontaneous resolution of the rash within one week of drug cessation, without specific treatment and without development of systemic organ involvement, is the definitive clinical confirmation of a maculopapular drug exanthem. Drug exanthem is self-limiting upon drug withdrawal. A viral exanthem would also resolve spontaneously, but the temporal association with drug initiation, the mild eosinophilia, and absence of any viral prodrome together confirm drug as the cause. DHS/DRESS is definitively excluded — it does not resolve within one week and requires organ involvement.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old man is admitted with a widespread painful rash that developed approximately two weeks after starting a new medication.
prompts.reasoning: A painful, widespread rash appearing at two weeks after drug initiation and severe enough to require admission introduces a broad differential of serious cutaneous adverse drug reactions and blistering disorders. The two-week latency is consistent with SJS/TEN, DHS, and erythema multiforme. The axis of pain distinguishes this from simple drug exanthem, which is typically mildly pruritic rather than painful. All mucosal and epidermal blistering conditions remain in play.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The rash began as dark, painful spots on the trunk and face; over the past two days, he has been unable to eat due to painful sores inside his mouth, and he reports burning eye discomfort. He has had a low-grade fever for the past three days.
prompts.reasoning: Mucosal involvement (oral erosions causing inability to eat, conjunctival symptoms) combined with a painful skin eruption and low-grade fever following drug exposure is a pattern of serious concern. SJS/TEN and erythema multiforme major both cause mucosal erosions and skin lesions. DHS may occasionally involve the mucosa but it is not a primary feature. Maculopapular drug exanthem does not cause mucosal involvement and is now excluded. Pemphigus vulgaris remains plausible given mucosal involvement and blistering potential, though its onset would not be drug-related in a 35-year-old man with no prior history. Four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He was started on allopurinol two weeks ago for his first episode of gout; he had never taken it before. He is of Han Chinese ethnicity. No prior history of severe drug reactions. No prior skin conditions, no prior autoimmune disease.
prompts.reasoning: Allopurinol is one of the most common causes of SJS/TEN worldwide, and the risk is markedly elevated in individuals of Han Chinese ethnicity who carry the HLA-B*58:01 allele. The two-week latency is consistent with the sensitisation period for SJS/TEN and DHS. DHS from allopurinol classically presents at 2–8 weeks. Erythema multiforme major is now less likely — it is more commonly triggered by herpes simplex virus infection than by drugs, particularly in the absence of recurrent herpetic episodes. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.5°C. Dusky, flat, targetoid lesions with a dark centre are distributed on the trunk and proximal extremities. Haemorrhagic crusting of the lips and multiple shallow erosions are present across the buccal mucosa. The skin is exquisitely tender to light touch over the trunk. No frank bullae are visible at this stage.
prompts.reasoning: Dusky targetoid lesions with haemorrhagic mucosal crusting and skin tenderness are the early clinical features of epidermal necrosis in the SJS/TEN spectrum. Skin tenderness to light touch suggests early epidermal-dermal separation not yet visible as bullae. DHS does not typically cause mucosal erosions or exquisite skin tenderness; it causes infiltrated oedematous plaques. DHS is now less likely. The clinical picture is most consistent with SJS/TEN. The BSA involvement and presence of epidermal detachment need to be formally assessed in the next step to distinguish SJS from TEN.

prompts.label: Clinical Assessment and Specialist Review
prompts.text: Allopurinol is immediately discontinued. Formal assessment finds areas of easily detachable epidermis on the upper trunk and forearms totalling approximately 7% of body surface area. Nikolsky sign is positive on perilesional skin. Ophthalmology confirms bilateral conjunctival erosions. No other organ involvement.
prompts.reasoning: Epidermal detachment of 7% BSA places this case firmly within the SJS category (<10% BSA detachment), as opposed to TEN (>30% BSA) or SJS-TEN overlap (10–30% BSA). A positive Nikolsky sign confirms the fragility of the epidermis at the level of the dermo-epidermal junction. Bilateral conjunctival erosions indicate mucosal spread to a second mucosal site, confirming the multi-mucosal involvement typical of SJS. Drug cessation, supportive care, and specialist management are required urgently.

prompts.label: Histopathological Confirmation
prompts.text: Skin biopsy from the edge of a tender area shows full-thickness epidermal necrosis with scattered apoptotic keratinocytes throughout all epidermal layers, a sparse superficial dermal lymphocytic infiltrate, and early subepidermal cleft formation. No intraepidermal blister. No eosinophilic infiltrate.
prompts.reasoning: Full-thickness epidermal necrosis — death of keratinocytes at all levels of the epidermis — is the histological hallmark of SJS/TEN. Scattered apoptotic keratinocytes reflect the underlying T-cell cytotoxic mechanism (CD8+ lymphocyte-mediated keratinocyte death). The sparse lymphocytic infiltrate distinguishes SJS from pemphigus (which shows acantholysis without full-thickness necrosis) and from DHS (which shows interface dermatitis without full-thickness necrosis). The absence of eosinophilia and intraepidermal blister further confirms the SJS pattern. These histological findings, combined with <10% BSA clinical assessment, confirm Stevens-Johnson Syndrome.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a widespread rash and high fever that began yesterday.
prompts.reasoning: An acute-onset widespread rash with high fever presenting to the ED requires rapid assessment. AGEP, SJS/TEN, and DHS are all SCARs (severe cutaneous adverse reactions), while generalised pustular psoriasis and disseminated folliculitis are non-drug-related alternatives. The acute, same-day onset distinguishes this from DHS (weeks-long course). The primary discriminatory axis is the combination of acute onset with both skin and systemic (fever) involvement.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The rash started suddenly in the skin folds and has spread to cover most of the body over the past 24 hours; she describes it as hundreds of tiny yellowish spots on a bright red, swollen background. She is febrile at 38.8°C and feels generally unwell.
prompts.reasoning: The description of hundreds of tiny yellowish spots (pustules) on erythematous, oedematous skin, starting in skin folds and rapidly spreading, is the characteristic morphological pattern of AGEP. Generalised pustular psoriasis shares this morphology. Disseminated folliculitis typically shows follicular-based pustules rather than non-follicular ones. DHS tends to present with indurated erythematous plaques rather than dense pustular eruptions. SJS presents with targetoid lesions and mucosal erosions rather than tiny pustules. Three to four diagnoses remain highly plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: She started amoxicillin 36 hours ago for a urinary tract infection, the rash appearing approximately 24 hours after the first dose. She had a milder similar-appearing rash several years ago, also following a penicillin prescription. She has no history of psoriasis and no family history of psoriasis or skin conditions.
prompts.reasoning: A drug-free latency of only 24 hours is characteristic of AGEP on re-challenge — in a previously sensitised individual, the reaction can develop within 1–2 days. A prior similar reaction to penicillin indicates previous sensitisation, making AGEP on re-challenge very plausible. DHS/DRESS requires 2–8 weeks to develop and is now effectively excluded by the 36-hour timeline. Absence of personal and family history of psoriasis significantly reduces generalised pustular psoriasis probability, though it cannot be completely excluded in de novo cases. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.1°C, HR 110 bpm. Diffuse, bright-red oedematous plaques cover the trunk and limbs, studded with hundreds of 1–3 mm non-follicular pustules; the axillae, groin, and sub-mammary folds are most severely affected. No oral erosions, no blisters, no epidermal detachment.
prompts.reasoning: Non-follicular pustules on oedematous erythema with predilection for intertriginous areas (axillae, groin, submammary folds) is the hallmark examination finding of AGEP. Absence of mucosal erosions and epidermal detachment excludes SJS/TEN. The high fever and acute onset favour AGEP over generalised pustular psoriasis, though the two remain indistinguishable on morphology alone. No psoriatic plaques are present elsewhere. Distinguishing AGEP from pustular psoriasis at this stage requires laboratory assessment and histology.

prompts.label: Blood Tests and Pustule Analysis
prompts.text: Full blood count: WBC 21.3 × 10⁹/L with 88% neutrophils. CRP: 74 mg/L. Pustule content: Gram stain — no organisms; culture — no growth after 48 hours. Liver function: normal. Renal function: normal.
prompts.reasoning: Sterile pustules (no organisms on Gram stain or culture) with marked neutrophilia confirm a non-infective pustular eruption. AGEP pustules are characteristically sterile, driven by IL-8/CXCL8-mediated neutrophil recruitment. Normal liver and renal function exclude multi-organ involvement, distinguishing this from DHS/DRESS. The combination of sterile pustules, marked neutrophilia, drug trigger with a very short latency, and intertriginous predilection confirms AGEP. Generalised pustular psoriasis is now effectively excluded — it is not associated with a new drug at this latency and would typically show a psoriatic history or background.

prompts.label: Histopathological Confirmation
prompts.text: Skin biopsy shows subcorneal and intraepidermal spongiform pustules densely filled with neutrophils, with papillary dermal oedema and a mixed perivascular infiltrate. No psoriasiform epidermal hyperplasia, no acanthosis, and no dilated suprapapillary blood vessels.
prompts.reasoning: Subcorneal spongiform pustules on a background of oedematous dermis without psoriasiform epidermal changes is the histological hallmark of AGEP. Psoriasiform acanthosis, regular epidermal thickening, and dilated suprapapillary capillaries would be expected in generalised pustular psoriasis and are absent here. The absence of follicular involvement distinguishes this from infective folliculitis. Together with the clinical, microbiological, and haematological findings, the histology confirms Acute Generalised Exanthematous Pustulosis triggered by amoxicillin.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 40-year-old man is admitted with a widespread rash that has been worsening over three weeks alongside increasing fatigue and malaise.
prompts.reasoning: A three-week progressive rash with constitutional symptoms (fatigue, malaise) requiring admission suggests a systemic process rather than a simple localised skin eruption. DHS/DRESS, drug exanthem (if severe), EBV/CMV, and SLE are all associated with rash and systemic involvement. SJS is introduced given the severity that required admission, though a three-week progressive course would be atypical for SJS. The discriminatory axis is systemic involvement alongside skin disease.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over the past week he has noticed swelling around his eyes and enlargement of the lymph nodes in his neck, in addition to the worsening rash. No mucosal sores, no blistering, and no breathlessness. His rash is not pruritic.
prompts.reasoning: Periorbital oedema and cervical lymphadenopathy in the context of a progressive rash and systemic illness are hallmark features of DHS/DRESS. EBV (infectious mononucleosis) also causes cervical lymphadenopathy, rash, and malaise — it must remain in the differential. SLE can produce facial oedema and lymphadenopathy alongside a rash. Absence of mucosal erosions and blistering excludes SJS. A simple maculopapular drug exanthem would not typically cause lymphadenopathy or periorbital oedema and is now less likely. At least four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He was started on carbamazepine five weeks ago for trigeminal neuralgia, his first ever course. He has no documented prior drug reactions and no history of skin disease or autoimmune conditions. He denies recent sore throat, fever prior to the rash, or close contact with anyone who was unwell.
prompts.reasoning: Carbamazepine is one of the classic culprit drugs for DHS/DRESS, with a typical latency of 2–8 weeks. The five-week latency is squarely within this window. Absence of prodromal sore throat or fever before the rash makes infectious mononucleosis (EBV) less likely, though it cannot be excluded serologically yet. SLE can present with this constellation but typically has a more insidious onset and requires autoimmune serological markers. Drug exanthem from carbamazepine would typically present at 1–2 weeks without systemic involvement. Three diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.3°C. Generalised erythematous, oedematous, and thickened macules and papules coalescing into plaques on the trunk and proximal limbs, with striking periorbital and facial oedema. Tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. No oral or genital erosions, no bullae, no epidermal detachment.
prompts.reasoning: Infiltrated (thickened, oedematous) erythematous plaques with striking periorbital oedema and lymphadenopathy is the characteristic examination pattern of DHS/DRESS. Infiltration distinguishes DHS from a simple drug exanthem (which produces flat macules and papules) and from viral exanthem. EBV can produce a morbilliform rash with lymphadenopathy, and periorbital oedema can occur in severe EBV. Absence of mucosal erosions excludes SJS. SLE typically produces a photodistributed malar rash rather than infiltrated truncal plaques. Two diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Blood Tests
prompts.text: Full blood count: eosinophilia 2.3 × 10⁹/L, atypical lymphocytes seen on blood film. ALT 312 U/L, AST 278 U/L (upper limit of normal: 40 U/L). ANA: negative. EBV IgM and IgG: negative. CMV PCR: negative.
prompts.reasoning: Marked eosinophilia and atypical lymphocytes are characteristic of DHS, reflecting the drug-induced immune dysregulation. Elevated transaminases confirm hepatic involvement, satisfying the multi-organ involvement required for DHS diagnosis. Negative EBV and CMV serology and PCR definitively exclude viral exanthem as the cause. Negative ANA excludes SLE. The combination of carbamazepine at five weeks' latency, eosinophilia, atypical lymphocytes, hepatitis, periorbital oedema, and lymphadenopathy confirms Drug Hypersensitivity Syndrome (DRESS).

prompts.label: Clinical Course and Confirmation
prompts.text: Carbamazepine is immediately discontinued. The patient is started on systemic corticosteroids. The rash, periorbital oedema, and lymphadenopathy gradually improve over four weeks. Repeat liver function tests at six weeks return to normal. The diagnosis is confirmed by the RegiSCAR scoring criteria, which classifies this as a definite case of DRESS.
prompts.reasoning: Gradual resolution of rash, oedema, lymphadenopathy, and hepatitis following drug cessation and immunosuppression over weeks is the characteristic clinical course of DHS/DRESS. The RegiSCAR scoring system (incorporating rash, fever, lymphadenopathy, atypical lymphocytes, eosinophilia, and organ involvement) provides a validated framework for confirming the diagnosis. The 5-week latency, carbamazepine exposure, multi-organ involvement (skin, liver, lymph nodes), and resolution on drug withdrawal fulfil diagnostic criteria for DHS/DRESS.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with painful sores in her mouth that have persisted for two months without healing.
prompts.reasoning: Persistent painful oral ulceration over two months in a middle-aged woman establishes a broad differential of mucosal diseases. Pemphigus vulgaris and mucous membrane pemphigoid are autoimmune blistering diseases with a predilection for mucosa. Behçet's disease causes recurrent oral and genital ulcers. Recurrent aphthous stomatitis is common but typically self-limiting. Oral candidiasis produces white patches rather than erosions. SJS is introduced as a cause of mucosal erosions. The primary discriminatory axis is the chronic, non-healing character of the oral lesions.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The oral sores began as painful blisters that ruptured quickly, leaving ragged, slow-healing erosions across the inside of the mouth; eating has become very difficult. Over the past two weeks she has noticed painful, fluid-filled blisters appearing on her chest and upper back.
prompts.reasoning: The progression from oral mucosal blistering to cutaneous blistering on the trunk is highly characteristic of pemphigus vulgaris, which typically begins with mucosal disease (often oral) before skin involvement. Blisters that rupture easily (flaccid bullae) leaving ragged erosions suggest intraepidermal blistering. Bullous pemphigoid typically begins with cutaneous tense bullae rather than mucosal blisters and is now in the differential given the cutaneous extension. Mucous membrane pemphigoid predominantly affects mucosa, with skin involvement less prominent. Behçet's disease does not produce cutaneous blisters of this type. SJS typically evolves rapidly over days, not over two weeks to months.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: She is on no medications and has no history of recent drug use. She has no genital sores, no eye symptoms, and no joint pain. She has no prior autoimmune diagnosis, though she has a first-degree relative with autoimmune thyroid disease. She has lost 4 kg due to difficulty eating.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of medications excludes drug-induced blistering. Absence of genital sores and eye symptoms reduces Behçet's disease probability significantly; Behçet's classically causes oral and genital ulcers, with or without ocular involvement. The family history of autoimmune disease increases the prior probability of any autoimmune blistering disorder. Three autoimmune blistering conditions remain: pemphigus vulgaris (intraepidermal, IgG against desmoglein), mucous membrane pemphigoid (subepidermal, mucosal-dominant), and bullous pemphigoid (subepidermal, cutaneous-dominant in older patients). Weight loss from dysphagia reflects disease severity.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Extensive shallow erosions and ragged ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, palate, and tongue. On the chest and back, multiple flaccid, thin-walled vesicles and bullae are present alongside moist erythematous erosions where bullae have already ruptured. Nikolsky sign is positive: gentle lateral pressure on normal-appearing perilesional skin produces epidermal shearing.
prompts.reasoning: Flaccid bullae that rupture easily, leaving moist erosions, indicate intraepidermal blistering — the roof of an intraepidermal blister is only a thin layer of upper epidermis and therefore collapses easily. A positive Nikolsky sign (epidermal shearing with lateral pressure) confirms poor intraepidermal cohesion. Bullous pemphigoid produces tense, intact bullae with a thick roof (full epidermis) and a characteristically negative Nikolsky sign. Mucous membrane pemphigoid is now less probable given the significant cutaneous involvement alongside mucosal disease. Two diagnoses remain: pemphigus vulgaris (most likely given flaccid bullae and oral predominance) versus bullous pemphigoid (which would be atypical in this presentation).

prompts.label: Serum Autoantibody Testing
prompts.text: Serum ELISA: anti-desmoglein 3 (Dsg3) antibodies strongly positive (index value 185, reference <20). Anti-desmoglein 1 (Dsg1) antibodies: negative. Anti-BP180 and anti-BP230 antibodies: both negative.
prompts.reasoning: Anti-Dsg3 antibodies target desmoglein 3, a desmosomal adhesion molecule predominant in mucosal epithelium, causing the mucosal-predominant pattern of pemphigus vulgaris. The strong positivity confirms the diagnosis. Negative anti-Dsg1 antibodies indicate that the cutaneous epidermis (where Dsg1 predominates) is less involved, consistent with the clinical finding of predominantly oral and relatively modest cutaneous disease. Negative anti-BP180 and anti-BP230 definitively exclude bullous pemphigoid. The combination confirms pemphigus vulgaris, mucosal-predominant subtype.

prompts.label: Histopathological and Immunofluorescence Confirmation
prompts.text: Skin biopsy shows suprabasal acantholysis — loss of cohesion between keratinocytes above the basal layer — with a row of intact basal keratinocytes remaining attached to the dermis. Direct immunofluorescence (DIF) of perilesional skin shows IgG deposition in an intercellular (net-like) pattern throughout the epidermis.
prompts.reasoning: Suprabasal acantholysis — the separation of keratinocytes from one another above the basal layer — is the histological hallmark of pemphigus vulgaris, directly resulting from autoantibody-mediated disruption of desmoglein 3 at desmosomes. The remaining row of basal cells attached to the basement membrane creates the characteristic 'tombstone' appearance. Intercellular IgG on DIF confirms active autoantibody deposition within the epidermis, diagnostic of pemphigus. The combination of suprabasal acantholysis, IgG intercellular DIF, and strongly positive serum anti-Dsg3 confirms Pemphigus Vulgaris.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 82-year-old man is referred with a widespread, intensely itchy rash that has been progressively worsening over the past three weeks.
prompts.reasoning: An intensely pruritic widespread rash in an elderly man encompasses a broad differential including autoimmune blistering disease, scabies (common cause of generalised pruritus in the elderly), urticaria, contact dermatitis, and drug reactions. The primary discriminatory axis is the generalised distribution and intense pruritus over three weeks. Bullous pemphigoid characteristically presents with intense pruritus in older adults, but the differential must be explored systematically.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The rash began as intensely itchy, raised red wheals; over the past ten days, large fluid-filled blisters have developed over some of the urticarial areas, mainly on the arms, thighs, and abdomen. He has no mouth sores and no difficulty eating.
prompts.reasoning: The sequence of urticarial plaques followed by large blister formation is the classical prodromal pattern of bullous pemphigoid. Large blisters developing on urticarial bases suggest subepidermal blistering, which produces tense, thick-roofed bullae. Absence of mucosal involvement significantly reduces the probability of pemphigus vulgaris, which typically begins with or prominently involves oral mucosal erosions. Scabies, urticaria, and contact dermatitis do not produce bullae and are now excluded. Linear IgA bullous dermatosis is introduced as a subepidermal blistering alternative in older patients. Drug-induced blistering remains possible. At least four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He has Parkinson's disease managed with levodopa-carbidopa for seven years, and heart failure managed with furosemide for two years. He has not started any new medications in the preceding four months. No prior history of blistering skin disease or autoimmune conditions.
prompts.reasoning: Neurological disease (particularly Parkinson's disease, dementia, and stroke) is a well-established risk factor for bullous pemphigoid — the proposed mechanism involves anti-BP180 autoantibodies that cross-react with neuronal isoforms of this antigen. Furosemide is a recognised drug trigger for bullous pemphigoid. No new medications in four months reduces the likelihood of an acute drug-induced reaction but does not eliminate it, as furosemide itself can trigger BP. These associations substantially elevate bullous pemphigoid on the differential. Pemphigus vulgaris remains possible given the age and progressive course. Linear IgA bullous dermatosis is associated with drugs (particularly vancomycin) rather than with neurological disease.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 36.8°C. Large, tense bullae (up to 4 cm) filled with clear fluid arising on erythematous urticarial plaques on the bilateral inner thighs, forearms, and abdomen. The bullae appear thick-walled and intact. No oral, genital, or conjunctival erosions. Nikolsky sign is negative on perilesional normal-appearing skin.
prompts.reasoning: Tense, large, thick-walled bullae on urticarial bases reflect subepidermal blister formation — the full thickness of the epidermis forms the blister roof, giving a tense, resistant blister. A negative Nikolsky sign confirms epidermal cohesion is intact (only the basement membrane zone has been disrupted). This pattern directly contrasts with pemphigus vulgaris (flaccid bullae, positive Nikolsky). Absence of mucosal involvement confirms pemphigus vulgaris is now very unlikely. Two diagnoses remain: bullous pemphigoid and drug-induced subepidermal blistering (given the furosemide history).

prompts.label: Serum Autoantibody Testing
prompts.text: Serum ELISA: anti-BP180 antibodies strongly positive (index value 230, reference <9); anti-BP230 antibodies positive. Anti-desmoglein 3 and anti-desmoglein 1: both negative.
prompts.reasoning: Anti-BP180 (anti-collagen XVII) and anti-BP230 are the defining autoantibodies of bullous pemphigoid, targeting the hemidesmosomal adhesion complex at the basement membrane zone. Their strong positivity confirms an autoimmune subepidermal blistering disease of the bullous pemphigoid type. Negative anti-Dsg3 and anti-Dsg1 definitively exclude pemphigus vulgaris. Drug-induced subepidermal blistering would not typically produce a strongly positive anti-BP180 ELISA. The autoantibody profile confirms the diagnosis of bullous pemphigoid.

prompts.label: Histopathological and Immunofluorescence Confirmation
prompts.text: Skin biopsy shows a subepidermal blister — the epidermis is separated from the dermis at the level of the basement membrane — with a blister cavity rich in eosinophils and a dermal infiltrate of eosinophils and lymphocytes. Direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin shows linear deposition of IgG and C3 along the basement membrane zone.
prompts.reasoning: Subepidermal blistering with eosinophil-rich infiltrate reflects damage at the level of the hemidesmosome-basement membrane complex by IgG autoantibodies activating complement and eosinophil-mediated destruction. Linear IgG and C3 deposition along the basement membrane zone on DIF is the immunopathological hallmark of bullous pemphigoid, produced by IgG binding to BP180/BP230 at the dermal-epidermal junction. This pattern differs from the intercellular IgG seen in pemphigus. The histological and DIF findings, together with strongly positive serum anti-BP180 and anti-BP230, confirm the diagnosis of Bullous Pemphigoid.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old woman presents with persistent scaly patches on her face and scalp that have been slowly worsening over six months.
prompts.reasoning: Persistent scaly patches on the face and scalp in a young woman establishes a differential across inflammatory, infective, and autoimmune conditions. DLE, psoriasis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, tinea capitis, lichen planopilaris, and sarcoidosis are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is the facial and scalp distribution of a chronic, scaling eruption in a young adult female.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The patches are red with adherent, rough scale, and the areas on her scalp have progressively lost hair — she can see her scalp through the affected areas. The facial lesions become noticeably more inflamed after sun exposure.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive hair loss within the affected scalp patches suggests scarring alopecia — permanent destruction of hair follicles by the inflammatory process. DLE and lichen planopilaris are the primary causes of scarring alopecia. Psoriasis causes alopecia but it is non-scarring and typically reversible. Tinea capitis can cause hair loss but is more common in children and associated with scaling and broken hair shafts. Photosensitivity (worsening with sun exposure) favours DLE — ultraviolet light is a key trigger of DLE. Seborrhoeic dermatitis does not cause alopecia or photosensitivity and is now excluded. Sarcoidosis is now less likely without systemic features. Four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: She has no joint pain, no mouth sores, no systemic symptoms, and no history of kidney problems. She has no family history of autoimmune disease and is not on any medications. She has normal energy levels and no fevers.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of systemic symptoms — arthritis, oral ulcers, renal disease, fatigue, or serositis — makes systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) with cutaneous involvement less likely and is consistent with purely cutaneous, localised DLE. DLE is most often a localised skin disease without systemic involvement, affecting primarily the head and neck. Absence of medications excludes drug-induced lichenoid reactions (which can mimic lichen planopilaris). Tinea capitis is now effectively excluded — no history consistent with fungal infection in an adult. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Well-defined, erythematous, hyperpigmented plaques with adherent scale and follicular plugging are present on both cheeks and the bridge of the nose. Within the scalp, patchy areas of permanent-appearing alopecia with follicular plugging at the lesion edges and central scarring with depigmentation are seen.
prompts.reasoning: Follicular plugging — keratin debris plugging individual hair follicles and visible as small spicules on the surface — in the context of scarring alopecia and facial plaques is highly characteristic of DLE. Lichen planopilaris also produces scarring alopecia with follicular inflammation and shares some features. Psoriasis does not produce follicular plugging or scarring alopecia on the face and is now excluded. The combination of facial plaques with hyperpigmentation, follicular plugging, and scalp scarring alopecia narrows the differential to DLE versus lichen planopilaris. Histology is required to differentiate.

prompts.label: Blood Tests
prompts.text: ANA: positive at 1:80 titre, speckled pattern. Anti-dsDNA antibody: negative. Complement levels (C3, C4): normal. Full blood count: normal. Urinalysis: no proteinuria, no casts.
prompts.reasoning: A low-titre ANA is found in up to 30% of patients with DLE and does not indicate systemic lupus — it is a non-specific finding. Negative anti-dsDNA, normal complement levels, normal CBC, and normal urinalysis confirm the absence of systemic lupus erythematosus. This profile excludes SLE but does not distinguish DLE from lichen planopilaris, since lichen planopilaris is typically ANA-negative. Skin biopsy is required for definitive histological and immunofluorescence differentiation.

prompts.label: Histopathological and Immunofluorescence Confirmation
prompts.text: Skin biopsy from the active edge of a facial plaque shows hyperkeratosis with follicular plugging, basal layer vacuolar degeneration (interface dermatitis), a thickened basement membrane zone, and dense perivascular and periappendageal lymphocytic infiltrates extending into the deep dermis. Direct immunofluorescence shows granular deposits of IgG and IgM at the dermo-epidermal junction.
prompts.reasoning: The histological triad of DLE — hyperkeratosis with follicular plugging, interface dermatitis at the basement membrane, and deep perivascular/periappendageal lymphocytic infiltrates — confirms discoid lupus erythematosus. Lichen planopilaris shows a lichenoid lymphocytic infiltrate around the upper hair follicle with a band-like pattern and typically prominent colloid bodies, but lacks follicular plugging and deep perivascular infiltrates. Direct immunofluorescence showing granular IgG and IgM at the DEJ ('lupus band') is a characteristic finding in DLE and is not seen in lichen planopilaris. These findings confirm the diagnosis of Discoid Lupus Erythematosus.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 14-year-old boy presents with a rough, hard growth on the sole of his right foot that has been present for four months.
prompts.reasoning: A rough, hard focal growth on the plantar surface of the foot in an adolescent raises a differential across acquired hyperkeratotic lesions and infections. Verruca vulgaris, plantar corn, callus, molluscum contagiosum, and foreign body granuloma are all plausible. The discriminatory axis is the plantar foot location and the four-month chronicity in a young person. No morphological detail is yet available.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The growth is painful when he walks on it, particularly with direct pressure; the pain is described as being like walking on a small stone. He uses the school swimming pool regularly and showers barefoot in communal changing rooms.
prompts.reasoning: Pain on direct pressure from a plantar lesion is consistent with both a wart and a plantar corn. Communal barefoot exposure in swimming pool areas is a well-recognised route of HPV transmission, raising the probability of verruca vulgaris. Molluscum contagiosum is now less likely — it produces soft, umbilicated papules and is not typically painful on weight-bearing pressure. Foreign body granuloma would require a history of penetrating injury and does not typically worsen gradually over four months. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He is otherwise healthy with no skin conditions. A classmate recently told him they also have a similar growth on their foot. He plays football and his feet are frequently wet inside sports boots for extended periods.
prompts.reasoning: A classmate with a similar lesion suggests a contagious aetiology, favouring viral wart over a corn or callus — which are mechanical lesions not transmitted between individuals. Prolonged moisture inside sports boots creates maceration of the stratum corneum, facilitating HPV entry. However, a plantar corn and callus are still possible as football boots create repetitive mechanical pressure. Physical examination is required to distinguish between these three. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A 1.2 cm firm, hyperkeratotic papule with a rough, irregular surface is present on the plantar surface of the right heel. Careful examination with a scalpel reveals the normal parallel ridges of the skin surface (dermatoglyphics) are interrupted and disrupted by the lesion rather than flowing over it. The lesion is tender on direct vertical compression but not on lateral pinch.
prompts.reasoning: Interruption of dermatoglyphics — where the lesion disrupts and breaks the continuity of the normal skin ridge pattern — is a key feature distinguishing a wart from a corn. A corn forms within the epidermis and the ridge pattern flows over or around it; a wart grows through the epidermis, displacing the ridges. However, interrupted dermatoglyphics alone are not fully specific, as an atypical corn can occasionally disrupt skin markings. Tenderness on direct vertical compression is seen in both conditions. A second discriminating feature is required. Callus is now excluded — calluses produce diffuse thickening without discrete borders and do not disrupt dermatoglyphics.

prompts.label: Dermoscopy
prompts.text: Dermoscopy reveals multiple red-brown dotted vessels arranged in a clustered pattern within white papillae, corresponding to thrombosed dermal capillary loops visible as pinpoint haemorrhages. No parallel ridge pattern or central translucent keratin core is identified.
prompts.reasoning: The dermoscopic appearance of clustered dotted (red-brown) vessels within white papillae represents thrombosed dermal capillaries within the papillomatous projections of a viral wart — a finding not present in corns. A plantar corn shows a central translucent keratin core (the nucleus) on dermoscopy, surrounded by a ring of thicker keratinised skin, without dotted vessels. The presence of thrombosed capillary loops is specific to viral wart and definitively distinguishes it from corn. The diagnosis is confirmed without requiring biopsy.

prompts.label: Histopathological Confirmation (Biopsy if Atypical)
prompts.text: In this typical case, clinical and dermoscopic features alone confirm the diagnosis; histology when performed shows hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, acanthosis, and large vacuolated keratinocytes with a perinuclear halo and wrinkled nuclei (koilocytes) in the upper spinous and granular layers.
prompts.reasoning: Koilocytes — vacuolated keratinocytes with perinuclear halos and dark, wrinkled nuclei — are the histological hallmark of HPV infection and confirm viral wart. They represent productive HPV infection of keratinocytes, where viral replication causes cytoplasmic clearing and nuclear abnormalities. The papillomatous architecture with parakeratosis and hypergranulosis above the koilocytic zone is the complete histological picture of verruca vulgaris. These findings are pathognomonic for HPV-induced epidermal infection.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old woman requests evaluation of a raised skin-coloured lesion on her upper cheek that has been present since her teenage years.
prompts.reasoning: A raised, skin-coloured facial lesion present since adolescence raises a differential of benign and malignant entities. Intradermal melanocytic naevus, dermatofibroma, epidermoid cyst, sebaceous hyperplasia, and BCC all present as flesh-coloured raised lesions. The primary discriminatory axis is the skin-coloured quality and the long-standing stable character. No morphological detail is yet available.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The lesion has been entirely stable for at least eight years and has never bled, ulcerated, or changed in colour; she is seeking evaluation purely for cosmetic reassurance. She recalls it first appearing as a flat brown macule when she was fifteen.
prompts.reasoning: Eight years of stability with no change in colour, bleeding, or ulceration significantly reduces the probability of a malignant process. BCC is now much less likely in a 29-year-old without UV risk factors and a stable lesion. The history of initial presentation as a flat brown macule in adolescence that has since become raised is consistent with the natural evolution of an acquired melanocytic naevus — beginning as a junctional naevus and transitioning to a compound or intradermal naevus over time. Dermatofibroma, epidermoid cyst, and sebaceous hyperplasia remain plausible as stable, raised, skin-coloured facial lesions. Four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: She has no personal or family history of melanoma. She has no history of blistering sunburns and no prior skin biopsies. She reports that her father has a similar lesion on his face in the same location.
prompts.reasoning: A similar lesion in a parent at the same anatomical site suggests a heritable tendency, which is most consistent with an inherited predisposition to developing melanocytic naevi — a recognised pattern. Epidermoid cysts are not typically familial unless associated with Gardner syndrome, which is not relevant in this context; epidermoid cysts are also now less likely given the facial location and skin-coloured papule morphology (epidermoid cysts tend to be dome-shaped with a punctum). Absence of melanoma family history is reassuring. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A 0.8 cm dome-shaped, soft, smooth-surfaced, skin-coloured to pale-pink papule with a uniform surface is present on the right upper cheek. No pigmentation, no telangiectasia, no surface ulceration. On palpation it is soft and compressible, with no firmness or fixation to underlying skin. A few coarse hairs are visible growing from its surface.
prompts.reasoning: The soft, smooth, dome-shaped papule with coarse hairs growing from its surface is characteristic of an intradermal melanocytic naevus — the purely dermal naevus cell population lifts the overlying skin into a dome and commonly contains entrapped hair follicles. Dermatofibroma typically presents on the limbs (not the face), is firmer, and characteristically dimples inward on lateral compression (dimple sign). The absence of telangiectasia makes BCC unlikely. Sebaceous hyperplasia characteristically shows a central dell (depression) and is associated with sebaceous openings. Two diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Dermoscopy
prompts.text: Dermoscopy shows a regular, symmetric, centrally homogeneous tan-brown pigmentation with a light peripheral rim and normal surrounding skin. No atypical pigment network, no blue-white veil, no regression structures, and no irregular streaks or atypical vascular structures are identified.
prompts.reasoning: The dermoscopic pattern of a homogeneous, symmetric, centrally distributed pigmentation without any melanoma-specific features — no atypical network, blue-white veil, regression, or atypical vessels — is consistent with a benign intradermal melanocytic naevus. Dermatofibroma shows a central white scarring area with a peripheral delicate network on dermoscopy, which is absent here. The benign dermoscopic architecture confirms an intradermal melanocytic naevus and excludes the need for excision from a safety standpoint. Cosmetic excision remains an option if desired.

prompts.label: Histopathological Confirmation (Excision for Cosmesis)
prompts.text: Shave excision for cosmetic removal shows nests and cords of bland, uniform naevus cells confined entirely to the dermis, without junctional activity and without cytological atypia, mitoses, or pagetoid spread. Entrapped hair follicles are noted within the lesion.
prompts.reasoning: The exclusive dermal location of bland naevus cells without junctional activity confirms an intradermal melanocytic naevus — the final stage of the naevus maturation sequence, in which naevus cells have migrated fully into the dermis. Absence of pagetoid spread (melanocytes migrating upward through the epidermis), cytological atypia, or mitoses excludes melanoma. The presence of entrapped hair follicles corroborates the clinical finding of hairs growing from the lesion surface. These histological findings confirm the diagnosis of acquired intradermal melanocytic naevus.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old man presents with a soft, painless lump under the skin of his upper back that he first noticed six months ago.
prompts.reasoning: A soft, painless subcutaneous lump on the upper back introduces a broad differential of benign subcutaneous lesions. Neurofibroma, lipoma, epidermoid cyst, reactive lymphadenopathy, and dermatofibroma are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is the soft consistency and the six-month gradual discovery.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The lump has grown very slowly and causes mild intermittent tingling that radiates outward when he presses on it; he has noticed two or three similar but smaller soft lumps elsewhere on his trunk that he had not previously paid attention to.
prompts.reasoning: Radiating tingling on compression of the lump suggests the lesion has a neural component — direct pressure on a peripheral nerve or nerve sheath tumour produces a Tinel-like paresthetic response. This feature is not seen with lipomas or epidermoid cysts and effectively rules out reactive lymphadenopathy. The presence of multiple similar lumps on the trunk increases the probability of a condition producing multiple cutaneous neural tumours, most notably neurofibroma. Lipoma can be multiple but does not produce radiating paraesthesia on compression. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. He was told at a childhood health screening that he had several light brown patches on his skin, but no follow-up was arranged. No family history of skin tumours or neurological conditions that he is aware of.
prompts.reasoning: The historical mention of light brown skin patches in childhood is a significant detail. Multiple café-au-lait macules in childhood combined with the development of multiple soft nerve sheath tumours in adulthood is the clinical pattern of neurofibromatosis type 1 — a condition in which neurofibromas are the characteristic skin tumour. However, solitary neurofibromas also occur sporadically in individuals without NF1. Lipoma and epidermoid cyst remain on the differential as they do not produce paresthesia, but the childhood pigmented patch history raises suspicion for a neural origin. Examination is required.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A 2 cm soft, non-tender subcutaneous nodule is present on the upper back. When the examiner applies lateral pressure to the lesion, it invaginates inward through a defect in the dermis — the 'buttonhole' sign is positive. Six additional similar soft nodules of varying sizes are identified on the trunk and proximal arms. No café-au-lait patches are identified on full skin survey.
prompts.reasoning: The buttonhole sign — invagination of the lesion inward with lateral pressure through a dermal defect — is a characteristic physical sign of cutaneous neurofibroma, reflecting the loose consistency and dermal attachment of the tumour. Lipomas are typically firmer, more lobulated, and do not invaginate through the dermis. Epidermoid cysts have firm keratin-filled contents and a fixed cyst wall; they do not invaginate. The multiplicity of lesions with soft, invaginable consistency narrows the diagnosis to neurofibroma. Absence of café-au-lait macules on full skin survey means NF1 cannot be confirmed on examination alone at this stage.

prompts.label: Excision Biopsy of the Primary Lesion
prompts.text: Excision biopsy is performed. Macroscopically, the specimen is a soft, grey-white, unencapsulated lesion. Histology shows a proliferation of spindle cells with wavy nuclei in a pale, collagenous and myxoid stroma, accompanied by numerous mast cells.
prompts.reasoning: The histological triad of spindle cells with wavy (buckled) nuclei, myxoid and collagenous stroma, and numerous mast cells is the hallmark of neurofibroma. The spindle cells represent a mixture of Schwann cells, endoneurial fibroblasts, and perineurial cells in varying proportions. The myxoid stroma distinguishes neurofibroma from schwannoma (which shows compact Antoni A areas and Verocay bodies). The unencapsulated nature distinguishes it from schwannoma (which is encapsulated). Mast cells are a characteristic and diagnostically useful component of neurofibromas. The diagnosis is confirmed as neurofibroma.

prompts.label: Genetic Counselling Referral and Final Assessment
prompts.text: Given the multiple neurofibromas, the patient is referred for genetic counselling. Wood's lamp examination reveals three axillary freckling spots previously missed; a careful re-examination by a dermatologist identifies six café-au-lait macules >1.5 cm in diameter across the torso. Genetic testing confirms a pathogenic variant in the NF1 gene.
prompts.reasoning: This prompt is an important clinical lesson: solitary and multiple neurofibromas confirmed on histology prompt systematic evaluation for NF1, which requires six or more café-au-lait macules >1.5 cm as a diagnostic criterion. The case is kept as neurofibroma (single lesion diagnosis) rather than being relabelled NF1, because the clinical presentation was of a single lump — the systemic diagnosis of NF1 is explored further in case 0100. The histological features of the excised lesion confirm neurofibroma regardless of the underlying genetic diagnosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 7-year-old boy is referred by his school doctor following the incidental finding of multiple light brown patches on his skin during a routine health check.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple café-au-lait macules in a child require systematic evaluation to determine whether they represent an isolated finding or a manifestation of a multisystem neurocutaneous syndrome. NF1, McCune-Albright syndrome, Legius syndrome, constitutional isolated café-au-lait spots, and tuberous sclerosis are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is multiple pigmented skin patches in a child. No count, size, or distribution is yet established.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His mother reports the patches have been present since infancy and have increased in number over the years; she also recalls noticing some small freckle-like spots in his armpits in the last year. He has no seizures, no learning difficulties, and no bone pain.
prompts.reasoning: Café-au-lait macules present from infancy and increasing in number, combined with the appearance of axillary freckling, is highly characteristic of NF1 — axillary and inguinal freckling (Crowe's sign) is a specific diagnostic criterion. Axillary freckling is not a feature of McCune-Albright syndrome (which involves large segmental macules with irregular 'coast of Maine' borders and endocrine abnormalities) or tuberous sclerosis (which causes hypopigmented patches, not café-au-lait). Constitutional isolated café-au-lait macules and Legius syndrome (SPRED1 mutation causing café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling) remain plausible. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: His father has several small soft lumps under the skin of his arms and back that have never been formally investigated. The boy has no learning difficulties at school and no prior diagnosis. Developmental milestones have been normal.
prompts.reasoning: A father with multiple soft subcutaneous lumps — likely cutaneous neurofibromas — raises the strong possibility of autosomal dominant NF1 segregating through the family. NF1 is autosomal dominant with high penetrance; 50% of cases are familial. Legius syndrome is also autosomal dominant and can cause café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling, but does not cause neurofibromas. The father's soft subcutaneous lumps are not consistent with Legius syndrome. Isolated constitutional café-au-lait macules are now excluded — the familial pattern of lumps points toward a specific neurocutaneous syndrome. Two diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Eight café-au-lait macules, all greater than 1.5 cm in diameter, are distributed across the trunk. Axillary freckling is confirmed bilaterally. No cutaneous neurofibromas are yet palpable. No plexiform lesions, no focal neurology, no scoliosis. Slit-lamp examination is pending.
prompts.reasoning: Six or more café-au-lait macules greater than 1.5 cm in a prepubertal child is a major NIH diagnostic criterion for NF1. Bilateral axillary freckling (Crowe's sign) is a second NF1 diagnostic criterion. Having two criteria meets the NIH threshold for NF1 diagnosis. However, Legius syndrome (SPRED1 pathogenic variant) can also produce six or more café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling — it shares these two criteria with NF1 but does not produce neurofibromas or Lisch nodules. Without ophthalmological assessment or molecular testing, Legius syndrome cannot be excluded on clinical grounds alone at this stage. Two diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Slit-Lamp Ophthalmological Examination
prompts.text: Slit-lamp examination by ophthalmology identifies three small, raised, pigmented iris nodules bilaterally — consistent with melanocytic iris hamartomas.
prompts.reasoning: Melanocytic iris hamartomas (Lisch nodules) are pathognomonic of NF1 — they are not found in Legius syndrome, tuberous sclerosis, constitutional café-au-lait macules, or any other condition in the differential. Their identification in a child who already meets two NF1 criteria (≥6 café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling) provides a third diagnostic criterion, satisfying the NIH diagnostic requirements for NF1. Legius syndrome is definitively excluded by the presence of Lisch nodules.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing Confirmation
prompts.text: Germline sequencing identifies a pathogenic heterozygous loss-of-function variant in the NF1 gene encoding neurofibromin, confirming the diagnosis. The family is referred for genetic counselling and enrolled in a multidisciplinary NF1 surveillance programme.
prompts.reasoning: Identification of a pathogenic NF1 variant confirms the molecular basis of the diagnosis. Neurofibromin is a RAS-GTPase activating protein that normally suppresses RAS signalling; loss-of-function mutations lead to unregulated RAS activity, driving tumour formation (neurofibromas, gliomas) and developmental abnormalities. The combination of ≥6 café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, a first-degree relative with NF1, and a pathogenic NF1 variant confirms Neurofibromatosis Type 1 by both clinical and molecular criteria.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 69-year-old man is referred for assessment of several rough, scaly patches on his scalp and forearms that have developed over the past two years.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (in situ / Bowen's Disease)
prompts.reasoning: Multiple scaly patches on the scalp and forearms of an elderly man over two years introduces a broad differential of keratotic and scaly dermatoses. Actinic keratosis, SCC in situ (Bowen's disease), seborrhoeic keratosis, psoriasis, and lichenified eczema are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is the location on chronically sun-exposed surfaces in an elderly patient with a two-year progressive course.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The patches are intermittently tender to touch and occasionally bleed minimally when he scratches them; they do not itch consistently. He has tried moisturising creams without improvement.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (in situ / Bowen's Disease)
prompts.reasoning: Tenderness on palpation and occasional bleeding on minimal trauma suggest an abnormal, friable epithelium rather than a simple benign keratosis or dry skin condition. Chronic eczema is now less likely — lichenified eczema is pruritic and responds partially to emollients. Seborrhoeic keratosis is typically asymptomatic and not tender. Psoriasis is pruritic with a distinctive scale and would improve with emollient use. Actinic keratosis and SCC in situ are both tender keratotic lesions on sun-damaged skin, and both can bleed minimally. Four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He is a retired outdoor construction worker with over 40 years of cumulative sun exposure. He has fair skin, burns easily, and has never used sunscreen regularly. He was treated for a squamous cell carcinoma on his right forearm two years ago. No immunosuppression.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (in situ / Bowen's Disease)
prompts.reasoning: Decades of cumulative occupational UV exposure and a personal history of prior invasive SCC confirm extensive field cancerisation — the concept of widespread UV-induced genetic damage across a chronically exposed field, giving rise to multiple simultaneous pre-malignant and malignant lesions. This history strongly elevates actinic keratosis and SCC in situ on the differential. Seborrhoeic keratosis and psoriasis are not associated with cumulative UV exposure or a history of SCC and are now excluded. Two diagnoses remain: actinic keratosis (pre-invasive) and SCC in situ (intraepidermal malignancy).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Multiple erythematous macules and thin plaques with adherent rough, sandpaper-textured scale are present on the vertex scalp, forehead, and dorsal forearms. The lesions are more easily felt than seen — rubbing the fingertips across the skin surface detects the roughness before the eye. Individual lesions are 0.5–1.5 cm, with indistinct borders. No indurated or ulcerated nodule is palpable. No lymphadenopathy.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (in situ / Bowen's Disease)
prompts.reasoning: The 'felt before seen' quality of the sandpaper-textured rough scale on sun-damaged skin is a classic clinical description of actinic keratosis. Thin, flat lesions without induration or ulceration favour actinic keratosis over invasive SCC. However, SCC in situ (Bowen's disease) can present as flat, well-demarcated erythematous plaques with a scaly surface that are clinically very similar to actinic keratosis. The absence of an indurated nodule excludes invasive SCC. Biopsy of the thickest or most atypical lesion is required to differentiate AK from SCC in situ.

prompts.label: Skin Biopsy (Thickest Lesion)
prompts.text: Punch biopsy of the thickest lesion shows dysplastic keratinocytes confined to the lower layers of the epidermis, with preservation of the epidermal architecture in the upper layers. Parakeratosis is present and alternates with areas of orthokeratosis. The basement membrane is intact. No full-thickness dysplasia and no dermal invasion.
prompts.reasoning: Dysplasia confined to the lower epidermis (basal and parabasal layers) with an intact basement membrane and no full-thickness involvement confirms actinic keratosis. SCC in situ (Bowen's disease) shows full-thickness dysplasia — atypical keratinocytes at all epidermal levels with preserved basement membrane. The partial-thickness dysplasia with alternating parakeratosis is the histological definition of AK. The intact basement membrane excludes invasive SCC. These histological features confirm actinic keratosis as the diagnosis.

prompts.label: Field Treatment and Confirmation
prompts.text: The patient undergoes field treatment of both forearms and the scalp with topical 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) cream for four weeks, producing a marked inflammatory response followed by crusting and clearance of the visible lesions. Surveillance is arranged at six-monthly intervals given the high-risk field with prior invasive SCC.
prompts.reasoning: Response to field-directed therapy with 5-FU, which selectively destroys rapidly proliferating dysplastic keratinocytes, confirms the pre-malignant nature of the lesions. Field treatment addresses both visible AKs and subclinical dysplastic cells in UV-damaged skin. SCC in situ and invasive SCC would not reliably resolve with topical 5-FU alone and require excision or definitive local therapy. The treatment response, combined with the histological findings of partial-thickness dysplasia, confirms the diagnosis of actinic keratosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 31-year-old woman presents with an intensely itchy skin reaction that developed suddenly while at work.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden onset of an intensely itchy skin reaction establishes the category of an acute urticarial or exanthematous eruption. Drug-induced urticaria, food-allergy urticaria, physical urticaria, contact urticaria, and viral exanthem are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is the sudden onset and intense pruritus. No morphological detail, trigger, or context has been established.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The reaction began approximately 30 minutes after taking a tablet she was given; the rash consists of multiple raised, intensely itchy welts appearing across her trunk and arms, with each individual lesion disappearing and reappearing in a different location over the course of an hour.
prompts.reasoning: The migratory, transient quality of individual wheals — appearing, resolving within an hour, and reappearing elsewhere — is the defining behaviour of urticaria. This pattern excludes viral exanthem and physical urticaria (which would require a physical stimulus and resolve when the stimulus is removed). Contact urticaria typically remains localised to the site of contact. The onset 30 minutes after ingesting a tablet is a critical temporal link to a drug trigger, though food ingestion must also be considered as the source of the allergen. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: She was given ibuprofen by her colleague for a headache; this was only the second time she had ever taken it. She ate her usual packed lunch before the headache but ate nothing unusual or new. She has no prior history of urticaria or allergies.
prompts.reasoning: Ibuprofen (an NSAID) is one of the most common causes of drug-induced urticaria. NSAIDs can cause urticaria through IgE-mediated mechanisms on repeat exposure or through non-immunological COX-1 inhibition increasing leukotriene production. This is her second ever exposure to ibuprofen, which is consistent with a sensitisation followed by elicitation reaction. No new food was consumed, making food allergy less likely, though a previously tolerated food in a new combination could theoretically trigger urticaria. Two diagnoses remain and require clarification of prior exposure history.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Multiple erythematous, raised, irregular wheals of varying sizes are distributed over the trunk and proximal arms; during the examination, one wheal resolves completely while two new ones appear at separate locations. The lesions are blanchable. No lip swelling, no tongue swelling, no stridor, and no hypotension.
prompts.reasoning: Direct observation of a wheal resolving and new wheals forming during examination confirms the diagnosis category of urticaria — the transient, migratory pattern is pathognomonic. Blanchability confirms vascular rather than haemorrhagic lesions. Absence of lip/tongue swelling, stridor, and haemodynamic compromise confirms urticaria without anaphylaxis. Both drug-induced and food allergy urticaria can produce this identical clinical picture; the morphology alone cannot distinguish the trigger. Both ibuprofen (the ingested drug) and a food component from her lunch remain as possible allergens. The trigger requires further elucidation.

prompts.label: Allergy History Review and Serum Tryptase
prompts.text: Detailed review confirms she ate her usual packed lunch with no new ingredients and has eaten the same lunch without issue for months. Serum tryptase drawn one hour after onset: 8.2 μg/L (normal); C4 level normal, excluding complement-mediated disease.
prompts.reasoning: The absence of any new food in her usual lunch, which she has tolerated repeatedly without reaction, effectively excludes a food allergen as the trigger. Ibuprofen remains the only new exposure — taken 30 minutes before symptom onset. Normal tryptase is consistent with localised mast cell degranulation from a drug pseudo-allergic reaction rather than widespread systemic mast cell activation. Normal C4 excludes complement-mediated urticaria. The combination of ibuprofen as the sole new exposure, no food trigger, and a typical IgE- or pseudo-allergic urticarial response confirms drug-induced urticaria.

prompts.label: Resolution and Confirmation
prompts.text: Ibuprofen is ceased and she is treated with a single oral cetirizine dose; the wheals resolve completely within three hours. Formal allergy testing at six weeks confirms a positive intradermal skin test to ibuprofen. All NSAIDs are documented as contraindicated.
prompts.reasoning: Complete resolution within hours of drug cessation and antihistamine administration confirms an IgE-mediated or pseudo-allergic mast cell response to ibuprofen. The positive intradermal skin test at six weeks confirms sensitisation to ibuprofen in this patient. Drug-induced urticaria is confirmed as the final diagnosis. The cross-reactivity profile of NSAIDs means all NSAIDs are contraindicated in this patient, and she should be counselled to use paracetamol as an alternative analgesic.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 63-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden swelling of his lips and tongue that began 45 minutes ago.
prompts.reasoning: Acute-onset lip and tongue swelling is a medical emergency requiring rapid categorisation. ACEi-induced angioedema, IgE-mediated allergic angioedema, hereditary angioedema, anaphylaxis, and perioral infection are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is the acute onset and anatomical location of deep soft tissue swelling. Airway assessment is the immediate clinical priority.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The swelling involves the lower lip and the anterior tongue and is painless; he has no urticaria, no rash, and no abdominal pain. He reports similar episodes on two occasions in the past year, both of which resolved spontaneously within 24 to 48 hours without treatment.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent angioedema episodes over the past year that resolve spontaneously without treatment within 48 hours is more consistent with ACEi-induced or hereditary angioedema than with anaphylaxis (which does not recur predictably without re-exposure) or infection (which would produce pain and fever). The absence of urticaria is a critical discriminator — allergic IgE-mediated reactions almost always produce urticaria alongside angioedema, whereas ACEi-induced and hereditary angioedema are typically non-urticarial (bradykinin-mediated). Perioral abscess is excluded — there is no pain and the involvement is mucosal rather than a discrete collection. Three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He has hypertension managed with ramipril for the past 14 months. He has no prior history of urticaria or allergic conditions. No family history of recurrent unexplained angioedema. He has not eaten any unusual food today and has taken no new medications.
prompts.reasoning: Ramipril is an ACE inhibitor, and ACEi-induced angioedema can develop at any point during therapy — including months to years after initiation. The onset 14 months after starting ramipril is characteristic; ACEi-induced angioedema is notoriously unpredictable in timing and can occur long after drug initiation. Absence of family history significantly reduces hereditary angioedema (HAE) probability — HAE is autosomal dominant and almost always has a positive family history. No new food or drug exposure excludes a new IgE-mediated trigger. Two diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Non-pitting oedema of the lower lip and anterior tongue, with no urticarial wheals anywhere on the body surface. No stridor. No skin erythema. No lymphadenopathy. Oropharynx: uvula mildly oedematous but airway is patent. Vital signs: HR 84 bpm, BP 148/92 mmHg, SpO2 99% on room air.
prompts.reasoning: Non-pitting, non-urticarial angioedema of the mucosa confirms deep dermal and submucosal oedema. The complete absence of urticaria is the key clinical finding distinguishing bradykinin-mediated angioedema (ACEi-induced or HAE) from IgE-mediated allergic angioedema. Anaphylaxis requires at least two organ systems to be involved — skin plus respiratory/cardiovascular — and is excluded by the absence of urticaria and haemodynamic stability. The clinical presentation is consistent with both ACEi-induced and HAE at this stage.

prompts.label: Complement Studies
prompts.text: C4 level: normal. C1-esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) functional assay: normal. C1-INH antigen level: normal. Serum tryptase: normal at 4.1 μg/L.
prompts.reasoning: Normal C4, normal C1-INH function, and normal C1-INH antigen definitively exclude hereditary angioedema types I and II. HAE type I is characterised by low C1-INH antigen; HAE type II by low C1-INH function with normal antigen; both show low C4. Normal tryptase excludes mast cell-mediated (IgE) angioedema. With all alternative diagnoses excluded, the only remaining diagnosis is ACEi-induced angioedema — a bradykinin-mediated reaction caused by ramipril-induced inhibition of the enzyme responsible for bradykinin degradation.

prompts.label: Drug Cessation and Clinical Confirmation
prompts.text: Ramipril is immediately discontinued and the patient is treated with icatibant (a bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist). The lip and tongue swelling resolves completely within six hours. Antihypertensive therapy is changed to amlodipine. No further episodes of angioedema occur over 12 months of follow-up.
prompts.reasoning: Rapid resolution with a bradykinin receptor antagonist (icatibant) confirms that excess bradykinin is the mediator — consistent with ACEi-induced angioedema. ACE inhibitors prevent bradykinin degradation by blocking angiotensin-converting enzyme (kininase II), causing bradykinin accumulation and increased vascular permeability. Response to icatibant, combined with no recurrence after drug withdrawal and substitution with a calcium channel blocker, provides definitive confirmation of ACE inhibitor-induced bradykinin-mediated angioedema.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old woman is admitted to the intensive care unit with a rapidly progressing, severely painful skin reaction that developed over the past three days.
prompts.reasoning: A rapidly progressing, severely painful, widespread skin reaction requiring ICU admission represents one of the most serious cutaneous emergencies. TEN, SJS, SSSS, severe pemphigus vulgaris, and generalised bullous fixed drug eruption are all plausible. The primary discriminatory axis is the acuity, severity, and ICU-level requirement of the presentation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over three days, she developed widespread dark blistering of the skin, with large sheets of skin beginning to detach, alongside severely painful erosions of the mouth and eyes; she can no longer eat or open her eyes fully.
prompts.reasoning: Large-sheet epidermal detachment alongside multi-mucosal involvement (oral, ocular) places this firmly on the SJS/TEN spectrum. SSSS can produce widespread superficial skin peeling but typically spares the mucosae — the presence of severe mucosal erosions makes SSSS much less likely.
