Low synovial glucose reflects bacterial consumption. The findings confirm septic arthritis requiring urgent surgical washout.

prompts.label: Culture and Confirmation
prompts.text: Synovial fluid culture and blood cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) in both samples. The patient undergoes arthroscopic joint washout and is started on intravenous flucloxacillin. At six-week follow-up, the joint has recovered with preserved range of motion.
prompts.reasoning: Isolation of S. aureus from synovial fluid and blood cultures confirms the diagnosis and identifies the causative organism. S. aureus is the most common cause of septic arthritis in adults, and intra-articular injection is a recognised portal of entry for skin-colonising S. aureus. Bacteraemia confirms haematogenous seeding or direct inoculation with systemic dissemination. The combination of acute monoarticular septic presentation after an intra-articular injection, high synovial fluid neutrophilia with no crystals, Gram-positive cocci, and positive culture confirms Septic Arthritis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old man with diabetes mellitus presents with a non-healing wound on the sole of his right foot that has been present for six weeks.
accepted: Peripheral Arterial Disease with Ischaemic Ulceration
prompts.reasoning: A non-healing foot ulcer in a diabetic patient is one of the most common and serious presentations in clinical medicine. Osteomyelitis complicating a diabetic foot ulcer, an uninfected neuropathic ulcer, cellulitis, deep abscess, and ischaemic ulceration from peripheral arterial disease are all plausible. The discriminatory axis is the chronicity (six weeks) and the diabetic context. Any non-healing diabetic foot wound must be evaluated for underlying bone involvement.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The wound developed from a blister caused by ill-fitting footwear; it has been treated with dressings by his community nurse but has shown no sign of healing and appears to be enlarging. He reports no severe pain, which he attributes to always having had reduced foot sensation.
accepted: Peripheral Arterial Disease with Ischaemic Ulceration
prompts.reasoning: A foot blister progressing to a non-healing wound in a diabetic patient with reduced foot sensation (diabetic peripheral neuropathy) is the classic mechanism for a neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer. The absence of pain despite an enlarging wound reflects sensory neuropathy — a key risk factor that allows wounds to progress unnoticed. An uninfected neuropathic ulcer, infected ulcer with cellulitis, ulcer with underlying osteomyelitis, and ischaemic ulceration all remain plausible. Six weeks without healing and gradual enlargement raises the probability of deep infection or bony involvement. Four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He has had type 2 diabetes for 22 years with an HbA1c of 10.1%, peripheral neuropathy confirmed on nerve conduction studies, and peripheral arterial disease with an ankle-brachial pressure index of 0.7. He has had two prior foot ulcers in the last four years, both of which healed with aggressive wound care.
accepted: Peripheral Arterial Disease with Ischaemic Ulceration
prompts.reasoning: Longstanding poorly controlled diabetes with confirmed peripheral neuropathy and peripheral arterial disease (ABPI 0.7 indicating mild-moderate arterial compromise) creates a high-risk substrate for both neuropathic and ischaemic ulceration. The history of recurrent ulcers with full healing argues that the current non-healing is a change from his baseline, increasing concern for a complicating process such as osteomyelitis. Cellulitis is now less likely — the absence of spreading erythema and the chronicity of six weeks argues against primary cellulitis. Three diagnoses remain; clinical examination and probing of the wound are essential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A 2.5 cm deep, punched-out ulcer with sloughy base and surrounding callus is present on the plantar surface of the right first metatarsal head. Surrounding erythema extends approximately 1.5 cm beyond the wound edge. Probing the wound with a sterile metal probe meets hard resistance at 1.8 cm depth. Foot pulses: dorsalis pedis absent, posterior tibial faint. Temperature sensation and monofilament testing: absent bilaterally.
accepted: Diabetic Foot Ulcer (Non-infected with Exposed Bone)
prompts.reasoning: Probing to bone with a sterile metal probe through the wound base — the 'probe-to-bone' test — is the key bedside clinical finding raising high suspicion for osteomyelitis. In a diabetic foot ulcer, a positive probe-to-bone test has a positive predictive value of approximately 89% for underlying osteomyelitis. The surrounding erythema within 2 cm of the ulcer edge is consistent with local periulcer infection but does not confirm deep bone involvement without imaging. Absent foot pulses confirm peripheral arterial disease contributing to impaired healing. Cellulitis extending beyond the wound is limited; deep abscess would produce fluctuance. Two diagnoses remain, requiring imaging to confirm bony involvement.

prompts.label: MRI of the Right Foot
prompts.text: MRI of the right foot (with gadolinium): T1-hypointense signal replacing the normal bright fatty marrow of the right first metatarsal head with corresponding T2-hyperintensity and post-gadolinium enhancement, extending into the metatarsal shaft. Cortical irregularity at the metatarsal head. A sinus tract connects the wound base to the metatarsal head.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is the gold-standard imaging investigation for osteomyelitis, with a sensitivity of approximately 90% and specificity of 82%. T1 marrow signal loss with T2 hyperintensity and gadolinium enhancement represents oedema and hyperaemia within infected bone, replacing normal fatty marrow. Cortical irregularity confirms destructive osteitis. The sinus tract connecting the ulcer to the bone surface confirms contiguous spread of infection from the skin wound to the bone. An uninfected neuropathic ulcer with exposed bone would not show marrow signal changes on MRI. The MRI confirms osteomyelitis.

prompts.label: Bone Biopsy and Microbiological Confirmation
prompts.text: Percutaneous CT-guided bone biopsy of the right first metatarsal is performed under fluoroscopic guidance. Histology shows necrotic bone trabeculae with an acute and chronic inflammatory infiltrate and surrounding fibrous tissue. Tissue culture grows Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus agalactiae. Wound swab from the surface grows a different polymicrobial flora, confirming that surface swab culture does not reliably reflect bone pathogens.
prompts.reasoning: Histology showing necrotic bone with acute and chronic inflammatory infiltrate is the pathological definition of osteomyelitis. Bone tissue culture — not wound swab — is the microbiological gold standard, as surface wound swabs reflect superficial colonisers rather than the true bone pathogen. The discordance between wound swab flora and bone culture organisms confirms the clinical principle that tissue culture must guide antibiotic selection. Staphylococcus aureus as the primary bone isolate is consistent with the most common causative organism in both haematogenous and contiguous osteomyelitis. These findings confirm Osteomyelitis (contiguous, diabetic foot type), requiring prolonged targeted antibiotic therapy and surgical debridement planning.
